source.summary: Copy Query permalink returns 404 when retention backfill job rotates tracehook tokens
source.description: Issue summary: Customer reports that the 'Copy Query as Permalink' action in the Console returns a 404 when the team runs a retention backfill that triggers tracehook token rotation. Impact: external stakeholders using saved permalinks from scheduled reports get 404s and cannot access traces referenced in alerts. Environment: prod us-east during a retention backfill window. Affects scheduled report links shared externally.
source.impact: All scheduled report permalinks generated during a retention backfill window become invalid; customer reports 12 external reports failed to open (BrightLoop marketing dashboards). Business impact: blocked postmortem access for compliance and delayed incident reporting.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Org: brightloop-prod / us-east-1
2) Start a retention backfill job that triggers tracehook token rotation for org-scoped tokens (internal job id RBX-BF-2026-03).
3) In Console > Dashboards, open a saved query and use 'Copy Query as Permalink'.
4) Paste permalink into incognito browser or send to another account.
5) Observe 404 response when loading the permalink and missing tracehook anchor in the UI.
source.expected_result: Permalink should resolve to the saved query state and include a valid tracehook token or reference that is available even if token rotation occurs during export/backfill.
source.actual_result: Received 404 page and console shows 'tracehook token invalid or expired' in network response. Permalink metadata references a tracehook token value that was rotated mid-export by retention backfill process.
source.logs:
Console network trace (trimmed):
- GET /permalink/abcd1234 -> 404 {error: "tracehook_token_not_found"}
- Tracehook service logs around 2026-03-10T03:21:xxZ: rotation triggered (job RBX-BF-2026-03), replaced org-token v2 -> v3
- Export agent log: permalink payload included org_token:v2 (stamped 2026-03-10T03:20:58Z) but rotation completed at 2026-03-10T03:21:12Z
Reference logs: s3://rbx-exports/brightloop/2026-03-10/permalink-abcd1234.json
source.investigation_notes:
Hypothesis 1: Permalink generator reads current tracehook token and embeds it directly in the permalink metadata; if a rotation runs between read and registration, the token will be absent.
Hypothesis 2: Backfill/retention job rotates org-scoped tracehook tokens without also updating or invalidating in-flight export metadata.
Findings: Reproduced locally by triggering a rotation during export agent run. Permalinks created in the 14-second window between token read and registration point to the now-rotated token.
Temporary mitigation attempted: updated export agent to fetch token after registration step, but that caused race with registration API (needs atomic fetch+register).
Relevant PRs/CI: github.com/redwood/tracehook/commit/1f9a2 introduced faster rotation logic (2026-02-28).
comments: Ava Chen (Support): Customer BrightLoop reported 12 permalinks returning 404; provided example permalink and timestamps. Asked for priority escalation.

Diego Morales (SRE): Reproduced in staging by launching retention-backfill and copying permalink; confirmed token in permalink is v2 while active token is v3. Marked as Sev1 due to enterprise customer outage.

SRE Lead (Maya Patel): Noted that rotation window is expected but permalinks should reference a stable identifier (token id) rather than raw token. Suggests changing permalink to reference a token id that can be resolved to current token server-side.

Eng (Tracehook) (Liam O'Connor): Identified code path in export-agent where we read tracehook.getCurrentToken() and inline it into permalink payload before permalink is registered. Proposed fix: switch to server-side token resolution by storing token_id instead of raw token and resolving at permalink read-time.

Customer (BrightLoop contact: Nora Singh): Provided list of affected permalinks and confirmed external teams could not access reports during a critical compliance window. Asks for ETA/rollback option.
source.resolution: In progress - code fix landed in tracehook service to accept token_id in permalink payload and resolve current token at read time. Hotfix PR created for export-agent to include token_id rather than raw token.
source.resolution_notes:
Action items executed: 
- PR tracehook#4872 merged to add token_id lookup and keep backward compatibility for existing permalinks.
- Export agent PR opened (console-ui#4872) to stop inlining raw tokens and instead register token_id with permalink registry.
- Deploy plan: rollout tracehook service change to prod us-east behind feature flag, then deploy export-agent change, enable feature flag, monitor for 10 minutes, then remove flag.
- Customer notified with interim workaround: If you share permalinks created during 2026-03-10T03:19Z-03:23Z, Support can re-register links using new resolution endpoint. Manual re-registration completed for 8 of 12 links so far.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-Tracehook: Instrument token rotation to emit mapping events to permalink-registry (issue ENG-TR-532)
CONSOLE: Add unit/integration tests to simulate rotation during export and assert stable permalink resolution
SRE: Run backfill window test in staging with canary export-agent to validate fix before full rollout
CS: Send final status update to BrightLoop and provide CSV of re-registered permalinks

source.summary: Speculative tool-schema hydration causing delayed streamed chunk delivery and mid-stream timeouts
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer reports sharp increase in end-to-end latency for streamed responses when prompts include tool calls. The stream starts but streamed chunks are delayed ~2-8s mid-response, occasionally leading to client-side timeouts.

Impact: Affects multiple production customers (enterprise tier). Intermittent but reproducible with tool call enabled; increases tail latency and causes streaming sessions to abort after client-side timeouts.

Environment: prod cluster in us-east, streaming route /v1/chat/stream with toolcall preflight enabled and schema-hydration flag set to speculative=true. Observed for rw-open-7b-v1 and rw-llama-13b-v2.

Observed behavior: Initial tokens stream normally for ~200-400ms, then a stall of 2-8s while server emits heartbeats but no content chunks. Traces show a synchronous preflight/schema hydration step invoked mid-stream for tool metadata.

Expected behavior: Preflight/schema validation should either complete before the first chunk is sent or be performed asynchronously without blocking chunk emission. Streaming should not stall due to synchronous schema hydration after initial token emission.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Create a chat request against prod us-east with tool calling enabled and speculative schema hydration toggled on (speculative=true).
2) Use a prompt that will trigger a tool call path (e.g., include a tool signature in assistant instructions and a callable tool in the request).
3) Request streaming response (transfer-encoding: chunked) and observe timeline of chunks in client.
4) Repeat with speculative=false and compare latencies and stall behaviour.
logs: 2026-03-12T14:52:03.312Z [streaming-proxy] INFO start stream request id=trc-9f3b2a (customer=FinSight)
2026-03-12T14:52:03.521Z [serving-runtime] DEBUG first-chunk-sent id=trc-9f3b2a latency_ms=210
2026-03-12T14:52:05.824Z [preflight-validator] WARN speculative-hydration-sync id=trc-9f3b2a waiting_for_tool_schema tool=payment_lookup duration_ms=2300
2026-03-12T14:52:06.027Z [streaming-proxy] DEBUG heartbeat chunk id=trc-9f3b2a (no content)
2026-03-12T14:52:06.331Z [serving-runtime] INFO resumed stream id=trc-9f3b2a resumed_after_ms=2819
investigation_notes: Hypothesis: speculative=true path triggers a synchronous schema hydration call that blocks the stream when tool metadata is not already cached. The hydration executes in the request critical path rather than as a background fetch.

Trace analysis: spans show preflight-validator span executing between token spans; blocked the emission of content chunks until schema returned. Trace link: https://traces.redwood.local/trace/trc-9f3b2a
Cache hit/miss correlation: incidents correlate with cold cache entries for tool schemas after model rollout events or cache eviction windows (~30m).
Rate/queue impact: When many concurrent streams hit cold schema hydration, thread-pool saturation leads to broader tail latency increases and occasional 504s from the proxy under load.
Temporary mitigation applied: disabled speculative=true for FinSight via per-customer flag; preliminary telemetry shows streaming stalls resolved for their traffic.
comments: Support (Ava Chen) 2026-03-12T15:05Z: Acknowledged customer report; reproduced with FinSight test payload. Escalating to SRE. Slack: #support -> thread https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C0123XYZ/p1678707900000

SRE (Liam Patel) 2026-03-12T16:10Z: Confirmed synchronous hydration span present in traces. Created workaround to flip speculative=false for affected customer. Observed immediate latency improvement in their requests.

Eng (Maya Rodriguez) 2026-03-13T09:20Z: Looking at codepath in preflight-validator; speculative branch currently awaits schema resolution to populate tool invocation metadata. Propose changing to async hydration with placeholder metadata and patching runtime to apply schema when available without blocking chunk emission.
Customer (FinSight Support) 2026-03-13T11:02Z: Thank you — turning off speculative mode reduced timeouts. Requesting permanent fix and timeline for rollback to speculative mode once safe.

SRE (Liam Patel) 2026-03-14T08:35Z: Filed PR to make speculative hydration non-blocking and to instrument cache warmers: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/pull/4387. Running canary in staging now.
source.resolution:
Workaround: per-customer toggle set speculative=false for FinSight, which removed mid-stream stalls. Temporary cache warmers enabled for critical tool schemas.
Permanent fix plan: change speculative hydration path to schedule an async schema fetch and continue emitting chunks; apply schema to toolcall metadata when available without blocking streaming. PR #4387 submitted and under canary. Deploy to prod targeted 2026-03-18 after staging validation.
Follow-ups: add cache-warming on model rollouts, increase preflight-validator thread pool elasticity, add a streaming-specific SLA test to CI to detect regressions early.

source.summary: Staggered autoscale window: transient GPU attrition + noisy-tenant correlated throughput collapse
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports large p99/p50 throughput collapse during the daily autoscale window (09:00-09:12 UTC) for their dedicated pool. Impact: production request latency p99 jumped from ~700ms to 6–12s, and sustained token throughput dropped by ~55% for ~8 minutes.

Impact:
- Customer-facing inference frontends experienced long tail latencies and 5xx spikes (mostly 502/504).
- Several requests timed out causing downstream job failures in ScribeAI pipelines.
- Business impact: queued jobs and delayed processing for ~350k inference tokens.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool id: dp-78a4 (ScribeAI reserved cluster)
- Region: us-east-1
- Serving runtime version: v2.18.3
- Autoscaler version: v1.9.1
- Model variants: redwood/gpt-4o-mini-v1 pinned at 2026-03-10

Steps to reproduce (customer provided):
1) Send steady QPS ~25 with mixed short/long streams between 08:50-09:10 UTC.
2) Wait for autoscale window at 09:00 UTC (predictive increase expected).
3) Observe sudden p99 and throughput collapse between 09:02–09:10 and intermittent 502s.

Observed behavior:
- Autoscaler detached 2 GPU nodes in staggered fashion (09:01:30 and 09:02:10) while queue depth briefly rose.
- Several GPU attachments failed with NVLink handshake WARNs in node logs and one driver reset observed (dmesg recorded nvidia-mig reset).
- A noisy tenant in the same reserved pool produced many short-lived high-frequency multiplexed streams at the same time (traffic spike on tenant-id t-1946).

Expected behavior:
- Autoscaler should avoid removing capacity mid-window when queue depth is above the hot threshold and should not induce driver resets.

Logs/metrics provided:
- link to customer dashboard (redacted): https://console.redwood.ai/dp-78a4/metrics?from=2026-03-12T08:30&to=2026-03-12T09:30
- kernel/driver excerpt attached by customer (snippet included in comments).
comments: 2026-03-12 15:04 - Support (Marisol Vega): Acknowledged receipt, assigning to SRE on-call for initial triage. Requested immediate traces, kube events, and full node logs for dp-78a4.

2026-03-12 15:16 - SRE (Ethan Park): Collected node kube-events and autoscaler decision logs. Initial observation: autoscaler executed two scale-in decisions while queue_pending_count > 30. Opening investigation. Marked as Sev1 due to production impact.

2026-03-12 16:02 - Customer (ScribeAI - ops@scribe.ai): Uploaded node logs and a short flame graph of token processing. Notes: "we saw rapid short stream fanout from an internal worker at 09:01". Customer asks whether we can throttle noisy tenant while we investigate.

2026-03-12 18:20 - SRE (Ethan Park): Implemented temporary tenant concurrency shaping for t-1946 (max_concurrency=6) as a mitigation. Observed partial recovery in throughput; still seeing NVLink WARNs on one host. Continuing analysis.
investigation_notes: 09:00–09:05 UTC trace correlation: autoscaler decision IDs a-dec-442 and a-dec-443 correspond to scale-in of two hosts (host-az1-12, host-az1-09).

Kube events: host-az1-09 reported 'FailedAttachVolume' followed by nvidia-driver-reset in dmesg at 09:02:05. Host-az1-12 reported high GPU memory pressure and evicted background prefetchers at 09:01:50.

Driver logs show repeated NVLink link training WARNs from 08:59:50–09:03:15 on host-az1-09. The driver reset coincided with a kernel OOPS in the GPU sidecar on the same node (stack trace attached to PR).

Autoscaler policy review: cooldown set to 60s, predictive window enabled with staged shrink policy. The staged shrink incorrectly used CPU-only metric for initial step, causing aggressive node removal under transient IO-bound pressure.

Workload correlation: tenant t-1946 produced ~12k short streams in a 90s window with average lifetime < 600ms; these streams caused slot fragmentation and frequent small-batch kernels increasing PCIe/host IO load.
Observability: per-slot NVLink error counters spiked concurrently with the noisy tenant burst. Node-level IO wait increased 8x. Console trace id: trace-20260312-09-az1-XYZ.
actions_taken: 2026-03-12 16:15 - Applied tenant shaping on t-1946 (concurrency cap). Immediate QPS smoothing observed.
2026-03-12 16:30 - Rolled back staged shrink policy to prevent mid-window detach; increased cooldown from 60s -> 180s as emergency mitigation for dp-78a4.
2026-03-12 17:05 - Rebooted host-az1-09 GPU driver after validating no critical workloads were on host. Post-reboot NVLink training stabilized and driver resets ceased.
2026-03-12 17:40 - Deployed hotfix to autoscaler: use combined gpu_ready metric (slot / nvlink health) for scale-in eligibility in dedicated pools (hotfix PR linked).
2026-03-13 09:00 - Coordinated with customer to pin model batch_size to 2 for short streams to reduce small-batch kernel overhead while autoscaler tuning continues.
resolution: Root cause: Combination of staged autoscaler shrink using CPU-centric signals, a noisy tenant generating many short-lived small-batch streams that increased PCIe/NVLink training load, and a tolerant cooldown that permitted mid-window detach. This triggered a driver reset on host-az1-09 and transient GPU attrition leading to throughput collapse.

Permanent fix deployed: Autoscaler logic updated to include GPU health (nvlink error rate and slot saturation) before scale-in; default cooldown for dedicated pools increased to 180s. Hotfix merged: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/1287.

Customer mitigation: Applied tenant-level shaping and reduced model_batch_size for short streams. Support recommended customer implement client-side backoff for internal worker producing short bursts.

Follow-ups: Create internal task to add autoscaler e2e test for short-stream fanout patterns (INT-5567). Schedule postmortem with ScribeAI and SRE to review SLA impact and timeline for full rollout of autoscaler GPU-aware policy.
Status: Monitoring in prod for 72h. If no recurrence, will transition ticket to Resolved and schedule postmortem.
next_steps: Monitor dp-78a4 for 72 hours (observability dashboard saved: https://console.redwood.ai/dp-78a4/dashboards/autoscale-health).
SRE to publish postmortem draft and invite ScribeAI for review by 2026-03-18.
Product to assess default autoscaler cooldown and staged shrink heuristics across dedicated pools (work item assigned to infra roadmap).
Customer to apply client-side rate limiting for the internal worker (recommendation provided in support comment).

source.summary: PulseRx — mid-stream token interleaving from multiplexer misrouting causing garbled streaming completions and spike in billable tokens
source.description: Issue summary: During PulseRx's morning ingest window (09:30-10:15 UTC, 2026-03-11) a subset of streaming completions returned interleaved token sequences and occasionally duplicated segments from concurrent requests. This produced garbled patient-facing summaries and inflated token counts on billing. Impact: high — customer-facing clinical summarization pipeline produced invalid notes for ~0.9% of requests in the window, and PulseRx reports a ~22% increase in billable tokens for the same period.
source.impact: Customer-visible data corruption for real-time clinical summaries; temporary pause of production ingest by PulseRx for 35 minutes; billing spike and urgent executive escalation from the customer.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use PulseRx streaming endpoint with concurrency of 80+ short sessions (avg seq len 200 tokens). 2) Enable streaming with prefix caching and the default router policy. 3) Observe token interleaving when tests are run against us-east-1 serving pool while autoscaler is initiating node scale-up. Note: reproduced only with the exact concurrency and short TTL caching configuration.
source.logs: Representative log snippets attached as plain text in the ticket: 1) multiplexer debug: 'mux: session 0x8a3 collided with 0x9f1 on stream-id 43' 2) router: 'route fallback -> pool-b: raced req-id=...  timestamp mismatch' 3) billing: 'token-count raw 158 vs recorded 198 (dup tokens)'. Full log ranges: 2026-03-11T09:29:12Z - 2026-03-11T10:17:45Z (cut into diagnostics bundle).
source.timeline:
2026-03-11 09:32 UTC - PulseRx first alert to support about garbled summaries and surge in tokens.
2026-03-11 09:40 UTC - Support validated and escalated to SRE/Serving.
2026-03-11 09:52 UTC - SRE observed multiplexer collision patterns in serving-runtime; initiated traffic fencing to isolate affected GPU pool.
2026-03-11 10:01 UTC - Mitigation: route PulseRx traffic to a separate warm pool and temporarily disable prefix caching for their tenant.
2026-03-11 10:20 UTC - Customer re-enabled traffic; observed no further interleaving.
2026-03-11 12:00 UTC - Billing anomalies triaged; provisional manual adjustment agreed pending reconciliation.
2026-03-14 09:00 UTC - Root cause confirmed and rollout of fix to multiplexer landed in staging.
2026-03-14 11:30 UTC - Fix deployed to prod; verified with soak tests and customer sign-off.
source.investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: noisy neighbor or kernel-level preemption. After correlating mux debug traces with router events, we found a race in the internal multiplexer where per-connection stream IDs could be re-used by the same worker before KV cache flush completed. This condition is exacerbated during scale-up bursts when the autoscaler reassigns worker PIDs and the mux state machine does not fully quiesce. Evidence: sequence of events in mux trace shows overlapping stream-id windows and replayed token writes. Billing discrepancy arises from duplicated token emission logged by the encoder stage when the handler retried partial frames. Linked PRs address atomic tear-down of mux state and a defensive dedupe step in the encoder.
source.customer_communication:
- 2026-03-11 09:45 UTC: Acknowledged receipt and escalated; we communicated we were investigating and recommended pausing heavy ingest.
- 2026-03-11 10:15 UTC: Provided interim mitigation (traffic fencing + disable prefix caching) and ETA for a full fix.
- 2026-03-11 12:30 UTC: Confirmed temporary billing credit action and open reconciliation.
- 2026-03-14 12:00 UTC: Shared root cause summary, permanent fix timeline, and offered one month service credit for the impacted window. PulseRx accepted the credit and requested the post-incident report.
source.resolution: Permanent code fix (gh-5501) to the multiplexer state machine that ensures stream-id uniqueness during worker handover, plus an encoder-level dedup filter to drop repeated token frames mid-stream. Deployed to prod on 2026-03-14 11:30 UTC with a controlled rollout and 4-hour soak in us-east-1. Mitigations included tenant fencing and temporary prefix-cache disablement. Billing team issued a provisional credit for the affected window; reconciliation completed on 2026-03-13 with a final invoice adjustment.
source.follow_up_actions: 1) Postmortem doc created and published: confluence/redwood/incident-2026-03-11-pulserx-multiplexer. 2) Backport fix to 1.x maintenance branch and schedule canary (owner: Priya Natarajan, due 2026-03-20). 3) Add unit/integration tests around mux handover and stream-id lifecycle (owner: Serving QA). 4) Instrument an internal metric 'mux_stream_collision_rate' and alert at >0.1% per 15m (owner: SRE). 5) Billing pipeline to run daily reconciliation checks for unusual token deltas (owner: Finance Ops).
source.postincident_checklist: - [x] RCA completed and published. - [x] Fix merged and deployed to prod. - [x] Customer credited and notified. - [x] Additional e2e tests added to CI. - [ ] Backport release scheduled. - [ ] Runbook for mux handover created and linked.
comments: Alex Gomez (Support) 2026-03-11 09:36 UTC: Received initial customer report — summaries contain partial repeats and occasional token interleaving. Opening ticket and escalating to SRE.
SRE_Team (SRE) 2026-03-11 09:50 UTC: Observed mux collisions on worker-gpu-7 while autoscaler was spinning up new nodes. Isolating traffic now.
Priya Natarajan (Owner) 2026-03-11 10:05 UTC: Applied tenant fencing and disabled prefix cache for PulseRx; requesters report normalizing results. Tracking root cause.
BillingOps 2026-03-11 12:10 UTC: Placed provisional credit hold; will reconcile once we have definitive token delta. Assigned finance ticket FIN-731.
PulseRx (Customer) 2026-03-11 12:35 UTC: Appreciate the quick mitigations; will resume ingestion after your confirmation. Requesting incident report and credit confirmation.
Priya Natarajan 2026-03-14 11:45 UTC: Deployed fix to prod; running verification, will follow up with postmortem link shortly.
Alex Gomez 2026-03-14 12:15 UTC: Postmortem link shared and customer notified of credits. Closing ticket after follow-up checklist items are scheduled.

source.summary: Intermittent generation requests with very large max_tokens time out near token-window boundary (KV-cache refill stall suspected)
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent 504 / request timeouts for single generation requests when using very large max_tokens (typically >= 120k token window). Failures occur late in the generation (after ~70-95% of requested tokens produced). Suspected stall during KV-cache refill / eviction cycle in serving runtime when prefill and decode phases overlap under heavy batching.

Impact:
- Affects multiple request attempts from NimbusAI production workloads on Dedicated pools in us-east; blocking their long-transcript generation features.
- Symptoms: spike in 5xx (504 gateway timeouts) and client-observed disconnects; partial outputs received before termination.

Environment:
- Dedicated customer pool (dedicated), prod:us-east; models: gptx-16b-fp16 primarily.
- Requests routed through API gateway with default 60s client timeout in their code, but server-side timeouts observed beyond that.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) POST /v1/generate using model gptx-16b-fp16 on dedicated pool.
2) Set prompt ~40k tokens, max_tokens=120000, stream=false, temperature=0.2.
3) Send concurrently from 6 clients to simulate production load.
4) Observe intermittent 504 from API gateway after long-running generation (often after ~30-50k tokens produced).

Observed behavior:
- Request occasionally returns 504 with no finalizing token. Partial stream of tokens earlier in the response.
- Serving runtime logs show long stall in "kv_cache_refill" and spikes in kernel scheduling latency.

Expected behavior:
- Long-context generation should continue to completion (or return a graceful error with a helpful hint) rather than sudden 504 mid-generation.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-78a4a3b, trace-us-east-78a4a3c (attached to ticket).
- Client-side logs showing request timings and partial payloads (attached).

Notes: This differs from previously reported "streaming hang" tickets because the requests do return partial tokens before failing and failure appears correlated to internal KV cache operations when requests are near the model token-window boundary.
comments: 2026-03-12 12:05 - NimbusAI (customer): We're seeing ~5-10% of long-generation jobs fail with 504s. This started after we increased max_tokens for a transcript feature. Attaching traces and sample request IDs.

2026-03-12 12:20 - Support (Ava Chen): Acknowledged. Opening P1 incident and routing to SRE + Serving runtime team. Could you confirm whether you're using streaming or non-streaming requests and whether you can temporarily lower max_tokens as a mitigation?

2026-03-12 13:02 - SRE (Marco Reyes): Initial triage - found correlated spikes in 'kv_cache_refill_ms' metric and increased kernel execution stalls on host pool rp-dedicated-12. Traces show long blocking in 'prefill->decode' handoff. Reproduced locally with synthetic load using customer-like concurrency.

2026-03-12 15:40 - Eng (Priya Nair): Added deeper prints to the runtime; observed that when batch mixing includes both very long prefill and short requests, eviction/refill of KV shards triggers synchronous disk/host-memory copies for certain quantized kernels causing stalls. Patch proposed to make KV refill asynchronous and add backpressure on batches that exceed token-window thresholds.

2026-03-13 09:15 - Support (Ava Chen): Shared a temporary mitigation with customer: (1) Reduce max_tokens to <= 80k or (2) enable streaming=true to flush tokens incrementally, or (3) route large requests to a low-concurrency pool. Customer applying (2) streaming and (3) routing; reporting fewer failures but still seeing rare timeouts.

2026-03-14 11:30 - SRE (Marco Reyes): Deployed a canary patch to rp-dedicated-12 that makes KV refill async and limits batch coalescing of very long prefill requests. Early telemetry (12h) shows 85% reduction in kv_cache_refill_ms spikes and near-zero 504s for affected traces on canary hosts. Planning broader rollout after runbook and automated test coverage added.
2026-03-14 14:00 - NimbusAI (customer): Thanks—canary seems promising. We'll keep streaming=true for now and report if we see new regressions. Please share ETA for full rollout.

2026-03-14 14:45 - Eng (Priya Nair): ETA for full rollout across dedicated pools: 2026-03-18 pending one more stability run and a regression test in CI that simulates heavy long-prefill coalescing. Follow-up action: create an alert for kv_cache_refill_ms > 5s per host and add a dashboard panel to per-pool view.
investigation_notes: SRE reproduced failure pattern with synthetic workload mixing long (>=40k token) prefills and short requests at concurrency >=6.
Profiling shows blocking memory copies during KV shard refill on quantized kernels; the refill path executed synchronously within the decode thread under certain batch interleavings.
Host rp-dedicated-12 showed memory pressure and increased scheduler latency correlated with the failures. Not all hosts reproduce; correlated to specific kernel selection and quantized model variant.
Comparing traces to SUP-4827 indicates similar long-context timing but different root cause (there was a streaming socket hang there; here it's internal KV refill stalls).
resolution_notes: Canary patch deployed 2026-03-14: KV refill made asynchronous and batch coalescing limited for requests with prefill token count > 30k.
Telemetry after canary shows 85% reduction in refill latency spikes and 0/1200 504s on canary hosts for the sampled traffic window.
Temporary customer workarounds accepted: enable streaming=true, reduce max_tokens to <=80k, or route large jobs to low-concurrency pools.

Planned follow-ups: full rollout 2026-03-18 after stability run; add CI synthetic test to cover long-prefill + mixed-concurrency; add dashboard and alert for kv_cache_refill_ms; update long-context runbook with recommended request patterns and a recommended default batch-limit for large prefills.
Postmortem: pending after full rollout and 48h stable telemetry; owner: Priya Nair (Eng).

source.summary: Mid-cycle seat add led to prorated surcharge removing negotiated volume discount on consolidated invoice
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer SiftMail reports a charge on their March consolidated invoice that appears to negate their negotiated volume discount after adding 12 seats mid-cycle. The invoice shows a prorated "surcharge" line where per-seat pricing reverted to list price for the prorated portion rather than applying the customer's tiered volume discount.

Impact:
- Customer sees a $4,322 unexpected line item affecting month close and internal chargebacks.
- Customer concerned this will recur on future mid-cycle seat changes.

Environment:
- Enterprise org on reserved/dedicated plan with negotiated tier: 31+ seats => $1.80/seat; 11-30 seats => $2.10/seat.
- New seats added 2026-03-05T15:40:12Z (12 seats) via admin console. Billing cycle starts 1st of month.

Requested outcome:
- Clarify why prorate used list price for added seats instead of applying the negotiated tier once total seat count exceeded threshold mid-cycle.
- Provide provisional credit for the overcharge and confirm permanent fix to pricing-engine logic for mid-cycle seat tiering.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Console: navigate to Organization → Seats → Audit log; locate seat additions on 2026-03-05T15:40:12Z (12 seats added).

2) Billing console: open invoice INV-2026-03-SIFT-0001 and expand line items. Observe prorated seat line dated 2026-03-01 to 2026-03-31 showing 12 seats charged at $2.10 for 26 days prorate instead of $1.80 after threshold.

3) Query billing ledger for org_id 'siftmail-7' in BigQuery partition billing_2026_03; run SQL sample: SELECT * FROM billing.line_items WHERE invoice_id='INV-2026-03-SIFT-0001' AND description LIKE '%seat%';
4) Cross-check pricing snapshot: GET /pricing/v1/snapshots?snapshot_date=2026-03-05 for org siftmail-7 to confirm effective price applied by invoice-generator.
logs: Billing job run: invoice-generator/v1 @ 2026-03-06T02:12:33Z - job_id ig-20260306-91
Billing ledger entries: line_item_id li-932188 created 2026-03-06T02:12:34Z - price_per_unit=2.10 - quantity=12 - prorate_days=26
Pricing engine eval log: pricing-engine@2026-03-05T15:40:15Z - evaluated tier for org siftmail-7 -> effective_tier=11-30 seats (list fallback), note: seat_count_snapshot=19 at midnight UTC
comments: 2026-03-12 - Maya Patel (Customer): Attached invoice PDF and seat audit CSV. Customer says negotiated volume discount was approved in PS contract (rate_id: RATE-SIFT-ENTERPRISE-2025) and expected all seats after exceeding 31 during the month to be billed at $1.80 pro rata.

2026-03-12 - Jordan Blake (Support): Acknowledged receipt and escalated to Billing. Requested authorization to pull ledger and pricing snapshots, and asked customer for the signed rate card attachment.

2026-03-13 - Billing Engineer (Priya Sundar): Initial findings - invoice-generator used nightly seat snapshot at 00:00 UTC on 2026-03-05 showing 19 seats instead of 31 due to a delayed seat-event propagation from the identity-service. Opening investigation with pricing-engine and identity sync team.
2026-03-13 - Jordan Blake (Support): Informed customer we are investigating and will provisionally credit any clear overcharges pending root cause.

2026-03-14 - Identity Sync Lead (Antoine Moreau): Confirmed there was a race during SSO import on 2026-03-05 that wrote seats to staging_table and the replication job to billing_snapshot failed for ~18 minutes. Patch deployed to make seat writes synchronous for replication.

2026-03-14 - Priya Sundar (Billing): Calculated overcharge = $4,322.00. Created provisional credit CR-2026-03-SIFT-82 to be applied to customer account immediately. Filing follow-up internal task to backfill corrected invoice (INT-8127).
investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Invoice-generator uses a single daily 'seat_snapshot' at 00:00 UTC to compute prorates. If seat count crosses a tier later in the day, that snapshot is authoritative for the whole invoice run.

Hypothesis 2: A replication gap between identity-service (where seat adds are written) and billing_snapshot service caused the 12 seats added at 15:40 UTC not to be present in the 00:00 UTC snapshot stored for 2026-03-05, resulting in the org being evaluated at the lower tier for the invoice run.

Data points: pricing-engine evaluation log shows seat_count_snapshot=19; identity service audit shows 31 after 15:40:12Z. Replication job logs show failed partition commit between 15:35:00Z and 15:53:00Z.

Related items: INT-8127 (internal ticket to re-run invoice compactor and patch snapshot replication), PR#billing/patches@github.com/redwood/billing (pr: 6123) contains the fix to introduce eventual-consistency guard and retry logic for seat snapshot export.
Risk assessment: If invoice-generator continues to rely solely on midnight snapshot without reconciling mid-day seat events, other enterprise customers who add many seats mid-cycle could be impacted.
source.resolution: Provisional credit CR-2026-03-SIFT-82 for $4,322.00 applied on 2026-03-14. Internal follow-up INT-8127 created to: (1) re-run invoice compactor for INV-2026-03-SIFT-0001 and generate corrected invoice, (2) deploy the replication retry patch from PR#6123 to eliminate mid-day replication races, (3) add a guard in invoice-generator to reconcile same-day seat events that cross tier thresholds before finalizing invoice lines. Customer notified and accepted provisional credit; billing team will confirm corrected invoice and close with customer once recompacted invoice is posted.

source.summary: Intermittent 502/503 errors during large embedding uploads due to chunked body aborts at API gateway
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer LexiconSoft reports elevated 502/503 errors and failed embedding upload jobs when sending large batched embedding payloads (~50–200MB) using our REST API over HTTP/2 with chunked transfer encoding. Errors began 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC after a gradual traffic increase.

Impact:
- Several customer embedding jobs failed (25% failure rate on a 40-job run).
- Increased API error-rate across their tenant (~P50->P95 error increase).
- Affects production P95 latency and caused retry storms from client side.

Environment:
- prod cluster in us-east-1; customer on enterprise plan with Dedicated routing to high-priority pool.
- Requests use HTTP/2 with chunked transfer-encoding and per-request streaming.
- Model: text-embedding-3-large pinned for this customer.

Observed behavior and tentative root cause hypothesis:
- API gateway logs show upstream proxy closing the request stream with 502 when the client finishes chunked body. Runtime logs show the runtime process logging: "ClientRequest aborted while reading request body" immediately followed by a worker restart in a subset of pods.
- Hypothesis: a mismatch in keepalive/read-timeout handling between apigw and runtime-proxy for large chunked uploads leads to the runtime aborting body read, causing the gateway to surface a 502. The abort path is triggering an upstream crash or worker restart under memory pressure when multiple concurrent large uploads coincide.

Steps to reproduce (internal):
1) Use a test client to POST 10 embedding batches of 1000 items each, chunked over HTTP/2 with ~10MB chunk sizes.
2) Target the LexiconSoft tenant route and run at concurrency 8 from 3 clients to simulate their sustained upload.
3) Observe apigw responses and runtime stderr. Reproduce reliably in ~4/10 runs in our staging cluster when configured with low keepalive (10s) and request read timeout of 30s.

Logs and metrics (samples):
- apigw: request_id=apigw-04b2c, upstream_status=502, upstream_message="upstream read aborted" (2026-03-12T09:17:32Z)
- runtime: [worker-7] ERROR: ClientRequest aborted during request body read (stack trace attached in ticket artifacts).
- k8s: OOM kill count on runtime pod remained 0, but rss spiked to 78% of memory before restart.
- traces: span shows request body finish event occurs after runtime has flagged EOF read timeout.

Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Advise LexiconSoft to reduce chunk sizes to <2MB and use lower concurrency for bulk uploads. This reduces observed failures while we investigate. Customer accepted and confirmed temporary workaround on 2026-03-12 18:00 UTC.

Investigation notes:
- SRE reproduced the abort locally when apigw keepalive timeout set to 8–12s and runtime request-read timeout at 30s; extending the runtime read timeout to 120s eliminated the abort in repro.
- The runtime code path for chunked body parsing does not gracefully handle mid-stream connection resets and triggers an assert followed by an emergency worker restart in a race condition.
- The issue is amplified when multiple large uploads hit the same worker and memory footprint increases; while not OOM, the worker restarts during memory compaction cycles.
- No evidence of keystore/tls rotation involvement in initial repro.

Proposed immediate fixes:
- Backport hotfix to runtime-proxy to tolerate early client-side resets and return a 499-style response to apigw instead of triggering a worker restart. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/apigw/pull/2248 (in review).
- Adjust default request-read timeout in apigw->runtime route to 120s for large-upload routes and add a per-tenant override for enterprise customers.
- Add guardrails in runtime to avoid restart on client-abort during body read; convert assert to soft-fail with controlled resource cleanup.

Longer-term actions:
- Add end-to-end load test scenario for chunked/streamed large payloads and include in canary gating.
- Improve memory accounting for streaming workers and add an alert when rss > 70% sustained during large-upload windows.

Expected next steps and owners:
- SRE (Miguel Ortega): finish runtime hotfix review and deploy to canary by 2026-03-15.
- Runtime eng (Priya Ramesh): change assert-to-soft-fail, prepare postmortem draft.
- Support (Ava Chen): coordinate with LexiconSoft for gradual rollout and confirm fix impact.

Artifacts attached: stack traces (logs/stacktrace-221111.txt), apigw traces (traces/apigw-04b2c.json), reproduction script (scripts/repro_chunked_upload.sh).
comments: 2026-03-12 10:05 UTC - Ava Chen (Support): Customer opened with logs attached. They reported ~25% failure on a 40-job embedding batch and included request IDs.
2026-03-12 12:40 UTC - Miguel Ortega (SRE): Reproduced in staging. Observed runtime worker abort on client body finish. Adding as P1 and escalating to runtime team.
2026-03-12 15:22 UTC - Priya Ramesh (Runtime Eng): Confirmed assert path. Will prepare PR to change behavior to safe-fail and add more logging. ETA for PR: 2026-03-14.
2026-03-12 18:05 UTC - Ava Chen (Support): Informed customer of temporary workaround (reduce chunk size, lower concurrency). Customer acknowledged and agreed to test workaround.
2026-03-13 09:10 UTC - Miguel Ortega (SRE): Created apigw PR to increase request-read timeout for large-upload routes. Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/apigw/pull/2248
2026-03-14 08:40 UTC - Priya Ramesh (Runtime Eng): Hotfix PR submitted to runtime repo; awaiting review. Putting runtime change behind feature flag for canary rollout.
source.resolution: Pending fixes in runtime and apigw; temporary workaround in place for customer. Full resolution will be updated after canary deployment and customer validation.

- Backport hotfix to runtime-proxy to tolerate early client-side resets and return a 499-style response to apigw instead of triggering a worker restart. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/apigw/pull/2248 (in review).
- Adjust default request-read timeout in apigw->runtime route to 120s for large-upload routes and add a per-tenant override for enterprise customers.
- Add guardrails in runtime to avoid restart on client-abort during body read; convert assert to soft-fail with controlled resource cleanup.

Longer-term actions:
- Add end-to-end load test scenario for chunked/streamed large payloads and include in canary gating.
- Improve memory accounting for streaming workers and add an alert when rss > 70% sustained during large-upload windows.

Expected next steps and owners:
- SRE (Miguel Ortega): finish runtime hotfix review and deploy to canary by 2026-03-15.
- Runtime eng (Priya Ramesh): change assert-to-soft-fail, prepare postmortem draft.
- Support (Ava Chen): coordinate with LexiconSoft for gradual rollout and confirm fix impact.

Artifacts attached: stack traces (logs/stacktrace-221111.txt), apigw traces (traces/apigw-04b2c.json), reproduction script (scripts/repro_chunked_upload.sh).

source.summary: Concurrent client retries split transaction boundary causing duplicate tool executions across Python and Go SDKs
source.description: Issue summary: Customer reports duplicated external side effects (webhook POST to order processing) when their app issues concurrent retries from both Python (2.4.1) and Go (1.7.0) SDK clients in a short window. Impact: Duplicate orders reaching downstream systems, billing exposure, and customer trust concern. Observed behavior: Under burst failure conditions the orchestrator appears to split what the client treats as a single transactional interaction into two distinct server-side request groups, and two tool invocations are delivered to the downstream webhook. Expected behavior: A single external tool call (webhook) per logical user action even if the client retries locally; SDK/platform should provide clear idempotency guarantee guidance and mechanisms to prevent duplicate side-effects.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Deploy a NovaFlow test harness that calls the Redwood streaming API with a function/tool that triggers an outbound webhook (order create).
2) Use two clients in parallel: python-sdk v2.4.1 and go-sdk v1.7.0, both configured to retry on transient network errors with exponential backoff and max attempts=3.
3) Simulate a transient network partition (short tcp reset) at the edge-proxy for ~300ms to force client retry paths to overlap.
4) Observe server-side request grouping and resulting webhook POSTs returned to the downstream system (NovaFlow order service).
5) Repeat with request-scoped idempotency header set to the same value from both clients, then repeat without idempotency header to compare behavior.
observed_logs: edge-proxy: conn_id=9845 RESET pri=E temporary failure, routing to fallback pool
serving-runtime: tool_exec - tool=order_webhook url=https://orders.novaflow.internal POST status=200 trace_id=ccf-9a1e exec_id=exec-122
serving-runtime: tool_exec - tool=order_webhook url=https://orders.novaflow.internal POST status=200 trace_id=ccf-9a1e exec_id=exec-123
customer downstream: Received duplicate order create events order_id: 2026-03-13-999 and 2026-03-13-1000
source.impact_details: Approximately 4 duplicate orders observed in NovaFlow's production test cluster over a 24 hour burst window. Customer billing shows double token usage for the same logical interaction. Customer has temporarily disabled auto-fulfill on production and requests guidance and mitigation. This is blocking a go-live scheduled next week.
investigation_notes: Initial triage by CS: duplicates correlate with short (100-400ms) transient failures at edge-proxy resulting in near-simultaneous retries from multiple clients.

SRE hypothesis: request-affinity hashing currently uses (client-ip, connection-id, request-id) and when a proxy resets connection mid-request the request affinity may not tie the retried request back to the original server-side transaction, enabling the runtime to schedule a second execution path.

Reproduced in staging by engineering with synthetic resets; when we force header 'X-Session-Transaction: same-id' on both clients duplicates do not occur, which suggests header propagation preserves atomicity.

SDK differences observed: python-sdk automatically attaches an idempotency token by default when using non-streamed 'call_tool' patterns, while go-sdk attaches a batch-level idempotency key for batched calls and does not attach per-action token unless explicitly enabled.
hypotheses: 1) Proxy/edge resets result in two distinct server-side scheduling groups because a canonical idempotency key was not present on the retried request.
2) Cross-language clients issuing retries with different idempotency scoping (per-action vs batch) create mismatched dedupe keys, so the platform treats retries as separate logical requests.
3) A race in request affinity lookup when connection metadata changes causes the runtime to decide the retried request is unrelated to the initial inflight transaction.
4) Downstream webhook confirmation/ack pattern is not idempotent on NovaFlow side (their webhook endpoint creates a new order on every POST without dedupe token), increasing observed impact.
mitigations_attempted: 1) Asked NovaFlow to enable a simple dedupe check on the webhook (idempotency-key header) as short-term mitigation.
2) Instructed customer to set explicit X-Idempotency-Key per logical action from the orchestrator layer (both Python and Go) and tested — duplicates stopped in staging.
3) SRE toggled conservative affinity fallback in staging to prefer header-based affinity over connection metadata; reproduction rate dropped significantly.
4) Created a temporary support-runbook instructing dev teams to avoid mixing batch-level and per-action idempotency in the same transaction until SDK changes land.
next_steps: 1) Engineering to add server-side canonicalization: prefer explicit idempotency header if present; if not present, compute a stable request fingerprint that survives short proxy resets. (owner: serving-runtime)

2) SDK teams to align idempotency scoping across languages: add per-action idempotency option to go-sdk and document behavior for python-sdk to avoid implicit vs explicit mismatch. (owners: sdk-go, sdk-python)
3) Update edge-proxy runbook to reduce resets during retries and surface connection-reset telemetry to the api-gateway. (owner: infra/edge)
4) Coordinate a backward-compatible changelog and provide NovaFlow with targeted guidance and example patches for their orchestrator to send X-Idempotency-Key. (owner: CS/solutions)
5) Set a follow-up internal ticket to validate telemetry coverage for split transaction cases and add alerting if duplicate tool_exec_count > 0.5% baseline. (linked: INT-412)
6) Plan SDK releases: go-sdk 1.8.0 (add per-action idempotency), python-sdk 2.4.2 (document default behavior), target dates: 2026-03-21.
source.resolution_notes: (workaround in place) Customer enabled explicit idempotency header from their orchestrator and re-enabled auto-fulfill in a controlled rollout; no duplicates observed in the last 36 hours in production test lane. Engineering will implement server-side canonicalization and SDK alignment to fully remediate. SRE will monitor for regressions.
comments: 2026-03-13 - Marisol Vega (CS): Customer hit duplicates during peak deploy window; provided initial logs and asked to triage urgently.
2026-03-13 - Ethan Park (SRE): I reproduced in staging with synthetic resets; adding serving-runtime and edge team for next steps. Created INT-412 for telemetry gap.
2026-03-14 - Priya Nair (SDK Lead): Confirmed python-sdk behavior and drafting change for go-sdk to expose per-action idempotency flag; will open PRs today.
2026-03-14 - NovaFlow (customer): We applied the idempotency header patch and observed no duplicate order creates for 36 hours. Thank you — please advise ETA for permanent fix.
2026-03-14 - Ethan Park: Marking this support ticket as In Progress while engineering owners implement fixes; next status update by 2026-03-18 or earlier if a deploy completes.

source.summary: TCP timestamp stripping by corporate firewall causes intermittent gRPC reconnects and degraded streaming in private deployment
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customers on a private VPC deployment intermittently observe gRPC stream resets and frequent reconnects when sending long-lived streaming inference requests. Impact: streaming clients experience token duplication, reconnect churn, and increased tail latency, causing transient user-facing failures during payments reconciliation jobs.

Impact:
- Multiple customers reported reconnect storms during peak batch inference windows (FinNova observed ~8% request failures, clients retried and duplicated outputs).
- Tail latency increases from 120ms median to >1.5s p95 for streaming responses.

Environment:
- Deployment: Private (customer-managed VPC peering + egress through corporate firewall)
- Region: us-east-1 (customer-routed egress)
- Redwood runtime: serving-runtime v2026.02.10
- OBS: TLS stays up; application-level gRPC shows RST and transient EOFs.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Customer runs a long-lived streaming generation job (streaming chat with >1024 token context) against a pinned redwood-7b-v2 model on their private deployment.
2) Load generator runs 100 parallel streams per node for ~30 minutes.
3) Observe intermittent request resets and reconnects in client logs; server sees TCP-level retransmits and occasional out-of-order segments.

Observed behavior:
- gRPC status CANCELLED / UNAVAILABLE on clients with reconnects multiple times per minute.
- Kernel/tcpdump on serving node shows TCP timestamp fields stripped or zeroed on some packets once flow exits through corporate firewall.
- When timestamps are stripped, TCP duplicate-detection heuristics mis-evaluate sequence numbers under reordering, leading to spurious retransmits and head-of-line blocking.

Expected behavior:
- No mid-path modification of TCP options (timestamps) for established flows; stable long-lived gRPC streams with graceful keep-alives.

Logs/Traces:
- Attached extract (sanitized) from serving node tcpdump: repeated TCP options length mismatch and TSval=0 in packets after 2026-03-12T15:21:10Z.
- Stackdriver/Datadog spans: multiple grpc:stream closed events correlated with increased retransmit counters on node NIC.

Temporary workaround:
- Customer routed egress around corporate firewall for a subset of serving nodes (temporary egress NAT through a managed firewall) and saw immediate reduction in reconnects.
- Short-term mitigation: reduce concurrent streams per node and enable smaller keepalive intervals to force faster recovery while we investigate.

Next steps:
- Coordinate with customer networking to capture full pcap across egress path and corporate firewall appliance.
- Reproduce in controlled staging with a middlebox that strips TCP timestamps.
- Evaluate runtime-level mitigation (disabling reliance on RFC1323 TS for duplicate detection in our user-space TCP stack or tuning kernel parameters) and a longer-term recommendation for corporate firewall config (preserve TCP options).
comments: 2026-03-12 16:03 - Maya Rodriguez: Customer reported reconnect spikes after weekend deployment. Provided initial tcpdump and streaming client logs. Marked as P1 due to production impact.
2026-03-12 18:20 - CS Engineer (Vikram S.): Confirmed symptoms align with packet option mutation. Asked customer for firewall model and egress path diagram.
2026-03-13 09:12 - Customer (FinNova netops): We see an inline 'optimization' feature on our NGFW that advertises performance improvements; logs show option rewriting during peak throughput.

2026-03-13 13:46 - Assignee (Ethan Park): Reproduced in lab by inserting a policy that strips TCP timestamps on our staging firewall; observed identical stream churn. Starting work on mitigation plan.

2026-03-14 08:05 - SRE (Nadia K.): Kernel tcp_retrans_out and tcp_reordering counters spike when timestamps zeroed. Suggest testing net.ipv4.tcp_smart_metrics tuning and disabling timestamp-based heuristics for affected nodes as experiment.
2026-03-14 12:30 - Customer (FinNova netops): We'll schedule a firewall rule change in a canary set tomorrow morning to preserve TCP options and confirm impact.
investigation_notes: Lab reproduction: enforced TCP timestamp stripping on staging NGFW -> confirmed gRPC reconnects and p95 latency regression.
Kernel traces: tcp_input_timewait and tcp_fastretrans triggered when TSval==0 and segment reordering present.
Hypothesis: corporate firewall has an inline optimization that normalizes TCP options under high throughput leading to malformed or zeroed TS option values.
Risk assessment: high for long-lived streaming workloads; short RPCs less affected. Private deployments relying on customer-managed middleboxes are exposed.

Potential mitigations: (1) recommend firewall patch/config to preserve TCP options, (2) runtime: reduce dependence on TS-based heuristics and increase tolerance for reordering, (3) route critical egress via managed egress that preserves options.
source.resolution: Pending - customer will canary firewall config change to preserve TCP options. Engineering to validate post-change and evaluate kernel/runtime tuning as fallback. Follow-up: if customer cannot change appliance, propose managed egress or document runtime knob and risk.

source.summary: Main-thread GC starvation in session-sidecar causing cascading 503s and api-gateway retries
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customers observed a sudden rise in 5xx responses (primarily 503 / upstream unavailable) from the hosted API in us-west during high-concurrency streaming workloads. Error wave started 2026-03-13 02:12 UTC and lasted ~45 minutes with intermittent recovery windows.

Impact:
- LexiDocs (dedicated tenant) reported ~35% of streaming sessions failing with 503s and mid-request disconnects.
- Platform-wide p99 tail latency for text-generation increased from 110ms -> 1.8s for affected hosts.
- API gateway showed elevated retry rates causing localized threadpool exhaustion.

Environment:
prod/us-west, dedicated pool for LexiDocs. Session-sidecars (Python 3.11) present on each node to manage streaming sessions and multipart uploads. Serving runtime (Rust) remained mostly healthy on GPU hosts.

Timeline / Observed signals:
- 02:10 UTC: LexiDocs bulk streaming load begins (concurrent sessions spike + long-lived streaming).
- 02:12 UTC: Platform alerts for increased 5xx from apigw.
- 02:13-02:22 UTC: Host-level CPU on sidecar processes climbs to 95%+; we see frequent GC stop-the-world pauses sampled by eBPF traces and python-timings (>250ms pauses).
- 02:18 UTC: API gateway begins returning 503s as health checks fail to get timely responses from sidecar endpoints; retries amplify backlog.
- 02:25 UTC: Autoscaler attempts to scale but new instances inherit warmed sidecar image with same workload; partial mitigation only after 02:50 UTC when traffic subsides.

Steps to reproduce (internal reproduction recipe):
1. In a dedicated us-west pool, spin up 200 concurrent streaming sessions pinned to the same sidecar binary.
2. Each session streams ~12KB/s tokens with intermittent small prompt transformations that allocate many small Python objects.
3. Observe sidecar CPU and GC pause durations via py-spy eBPF sampling.

Logs / Metrics (high-level links):
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/streams/streaming-5xx?orgId=1&from=now-6h&to=now (pan to 2026-03-13 02:10)
- Loki: query 'job="session-sidecar" level=error "healthcheck"' for 02:10-03:00 UTC
- eBPF traces attached in investigation notes (link above Confluence page)

Investigation notes:
- The serving-runtime (GPU worker) remained responsive; crash dumps show no SIGSEGV or OOM on GPU processes.
- Primary failure vector identified in session-sidecar (Python) where GC induced stop-the-world pauses blocked the request dispatcher. Sidecar implements synchronous GC-unaware loops that block on large ephemeral lists during streaming transforms.
- Healthcheck endpoint handler is served by the same thread pool; during GC pauses HTTP handlers become unresponsive causing the API gateway to mark upstream as unhealthy and return 503s to clients.
- Amplification: API gateway retries queued requests to the same unhealthy host, increasing load and further lengthening GC pause windows (feedback loop).
- Correlated grafana panel: GC pause count track overlaps with 503 spike; p95 of sidecar request duration increased from 18ms -> 620ms during spike.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- 02:30 UTC: Support applied a soft-rate limit to LexiDocs tenant at the apigw layer (reduced concurrency by ~40%); error rate dropped but not to baseline.
- 02:38 UTC: Rolled a temporary probed-sidecar image with GC tuning flags (PYTHONMALLOC=malloc, PYTHONGC_THRESHOLD adjusted) to a small canary subset (3 nodes) — canary showed 60% reduction in pause frequency.
- 03:05 UTC: Manual routing removed the most impacted hosts from the pool until restart and GC-tuned image rollout completed.

Workarounds for customers:
- Ask customers to reduce concurrent streaming sessions or increase per-session batching where possible until a permanent fix is deployed.
- For LexiDocs we offered temporary request throttling and extended credits for the downtime window.

Root-cause hypothesis (current):
- Sidecar's streaming transformation path allocates many short-lived Python objects under heavy concurrency; default GC thresholds cause stop-the-world collection that blocks healthchecks. Combined with synchronous healthcheck handlers and apigw retry behavior this produced a positive feedback loop and 503 wave.

Permanent remediation plan (next steps):
1. Engineering (/runtime and /platform) to implement: move healthcheck endpoint to an isolated thread/process (non-blocking) OR make healthchecks serviced by the runtime side rather than the Python sidecar. Owner: Diego Morales. ETA: 2026-03-20.
2. Refactor streaming transforms to use allocation-pooling or to run CPU-bound transforms in a worker subprocess that can be restarted without failing healthchecks. Owner: Runtime team. ETA: 2026-04-02.
3. Add platform-level guardrail: apigw should detect upstream GC flapping via a new health metric (GC pause ratio) and avoid aggressive retry amplification. Owner: API Platform. ETA: 2026-03-27.
4. Add automated canary rollout of GC-tuned images for sidecars when rolling worker images to reduce blast radius. Owner: SRE. ETA: 2026-03-22.

Post-incident follow-up:
- Drafting a short postmortem; planned retro meeting with Runtime and API Platform on 2026-03-17.
- Open action items added to INT backlog for healthcheck isolation and GC-safe transform patterns.
comments: 2026-03-13 02:20 UTC — Support (Ava): Customer reported 35% of streams failing; elevated 503s visible on dashboards. Escalating to SRE.
2026-03-13 02:35 UTC — SRE (Diego): Identified python sidecar GC pauses using eBPF sampling. Implementing tenant rate limit as temporary relief.
2026-03-13 02:48 UTC — Runtime eng (Priya Rao): Can confirm heavy allocation patterns in transform code; preparing GC-thresholded sidecar image for canary.
2026-03-13 03:10 UTC — Support (Ava): LexiDocs confirmed lower error rates after manual host removal and throttling; continuing monitoring.
2026-03-14 09:15 UTC — Diego: Created initial remediation tickets and scheduled cross-team retro (INT-44521, INT-44522).
source.resolution: Mitigated via tenant throttling, GC-tuned sidecar canary, and host rotation. Permanent fixes planned (healthcheck isolation + transform refactor).
source.notes: No GPU faults or OOMs detected; issue localized to session-sidecar CPU/GIL/GC behavior. Monitoring will remain at elevated priority for 72 hours.

text:
Issue summary:
Customers observed a sudden rise in 5xx responses (primarily 503 / upstream unavailable) from the hosted API in us-west during high-concurrency streaming workloads. Error wave started 2026-03-13 02:12 UTC and lasted ~45 minutes with intermittent recovery windows.

Impact:
- LexiDocs (dedicated tenant) reported ~35% of streaming sessions failing with 503s and mid-request disconnects.
- Platform-wide p99 tail latency for text-generation increased from 110ms -> 1.8s for affected hosts.
- API gateway showed elevated retry rates causing localized threadpool exhaustion.

Environment:
prod/us-west, dedicated pool for LexiDocs. Session-sidecars (Python 3.11) present on each node to manage streaming sessions and multipart uploads. Serving runtime (Rust) remained mostly healthy on GPU hosts.

Timeline / Observed signals:
- 02:10 UTC: LexiDocs bulk streaming load begins (concurrent sessions spike + long-lived streaming).
- 02:12 UTC: Platform alerts for increased 5xx from apigw.
- 02:13-02:22 UTC: Host-level CPU on sidecar processes climbs to 95%+; we see frequent GC stop-the-world pauses sampled by eBPF traces and python-timings (>250ms pauses).
- 02:18 UTC: API gateway begins returning 503s as health checks fail to get timely responses from sidecar endpoints; retries amplify backlog.
- 02:25 UTC: Autoscaler attempts to scale but new instances inherit warmed sidecar image with same workload; partial mitigation only after 02:50 UTC when traffic subsides.

Steps to reproduce (internal reproduction recipe):
1. In a dedicated us-west pool, spin up 200 concurrent streaming sessions pinned to the same sidecar binary.
2. Each session streams ~12KB/s tokens with intermittent small prompt transformations that allocate many small Python objects.
3. Observe sidecar CPU and GC pause durations via py-spy eBPF sampling.

Logs / Metrics (high-level links):
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/streams/streaming-5xx?orgId=1&from=now-6h&to=now (pan to 2026-03-13 02:10)
- Loki: query 'job="session-sidecar" level=error "healthcheck"' for 02:10-03:00 UTC
- eBPF traces attached in investigation notes (link above Confluence page)

Investigation notes:
- The serving-runtime (GPU worker) remained responsive; crash dumps show no SIGSEGV or OOM on GPU processes.
- Primary failure vector identified in session-sidecar (Python) where GC induced stop-the-world pauses blocked the request dispatcher. Sidecar implements synchronous GC-unaware loops that block on large ephemeral lists during streaming transforms.
- Healthcheck endpoint handler is served by the same thread pool; during GC pauses HTTP handlers become unresponsive causing the API gateway to mark upstream as unhealthy and return 503s to clients.
- Amplification: API gateway retries queued requests to the same unhealthy host, increasing load and further lengthening GC pause windows (feedback loop).
- Correlated grafana panel: GC pause count track overlaps with 503 spike; p95 of sidecar request duration increased from 18ms -> 620ms during spike.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- 02:30 UTC: Support applied a soft-rate limit to LexiDocs tenant at the apigw layer (reduced concurrency by ~40%); error rate dropped but not to baseline.
- 02:38 UTC: Rolled a temporary probed-sidecar image with GC tuning flags (PYTHONMALLOC=malloc, PYTHONGC_THRESHOLD adjusted) to a small canary subset (3 nodes) — canary showed 60% reduction in pause frequency.
- 03:05 UTC: Manual routing removed the most impacted hosts from the pool until restart and GC-tuned image rollout completed.

Workarounds for customers:
- Ask customers to reduce concurrent streaming sessions or increase per-session batching where possible until a permanent fix is deployed.
- For LexiDocs we offered temporary request throttling and extended credits for the downtime window.

Root-cause hypothesis (current):
- Sidecar's streaming transformation path allocates many short-lived Python objects under heavy concurrency; default GC thresholds cause stop-the-world collection that blocks healthchecks. Combined with synchronous healthcheck handlers and apigw retry behavior this produced a positive feedback loop and 503 wave.

Permanent remediation plan (next steps):
1. Engineering (/runtime and /platform) to implement: move healthcheck endpoint to an isolated thread/process (non-blocking) OR make healthchecks serviced by the runtime side rather than the Python sidecar. Owner: Diego Morales. ETA: 2026-03-20.
2. Refactor streaming transforms to use allocation-pooling or to run CPU-bound transforms in a worker subprocess that can be restarted without failing healthchecks. Owner: Runtime team. ETA: 2026-04-02.
3. Add platform-level guardrail: apigw should detect upstream GC flapping via a new health metric (GC pause ratio) and avoid aggressive retry amplification. Owner: API Platform. ETA: 2026-03-27.
4. Add automated canary rollout of GC-tuned images for sidecars when rolling worker images to reduce blast radius. Owner: SRE. ETA: 2026-03-22.

Post-incident follow-up:
- Drafting a short postmortem; planned retro meeting with Runtime and API Platform on 2026-03-17.
- Open action items added to INT backlog for healthcheck isolation and GC-safe transform patterns.

source.summary: Intermittent 429 bursts for multiplexed websocket chat causing degraded UX
source.description: Issue summary: NimbusHealth is seeing intermittent bursts of HTTP 429 responses returned to multiplexed websocket clients on the hosted API during peak business hours. Impact: Users in their clinical triage chat are experiencing message failures and UI retries, increasing perceived latency and leading to dropped conversations. Scope: Affects ~10 client connection pools from NimbusHealth (enterprise account) in us-east region; seen across redwood-chat-small and medium models. Expected: Multiplexed websocket connections should see smoothing by token-level and connection-level rate limits with transparent backpressure, not sudden 429 bursts. Observed: Spikes of 429s lasting 20-40s every ~15 minutes, correlating with bursty fan-out from a single internal service. Steps to reproduce: 1) Establish multiplexed websocket session using shared API key for multiple browser clients (as customer implemented). 2) Simulate parallel message bursts (20 simultaneous requests over the same websocket multiplex) with messages that produce ~80-200 tokens. 3) Observe intermittent 429 responses returned with X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and Retry-After headers. Logs: Attached sample gateway headers and rate-limiter trace (see comment #2). Customer note: This started after migration to a client-side multiplexer used to reduce websocket count. Customer requests guidance on configuration to avoid disruption without dramatically lowering throughput.
source.investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: shared API key + multiplexing causes client-level bucket exhaustion because token and concurrency limits are aggregated per-API key in current rate-limiter config. Evidence: Rate-limiter traces show single token bucket hit (bucket_id=acct-nimbushealth) with short refill cycles; connection-level counters spike to 100+ concurrent inflight requests. API gateway metrics indicate per-key bursts coincide with 429 timestamps. Alternate hypothesis: upstream quota-service mis-evaluating multiplexed connection headers; less likely given consistent bucket_id. Mitigation applied: temporarily increased per-key burst window for NimbusHealth from 200 to 500 tokens for 2 hours via emergency override (PR referenced). Next steps: implement per-connection bucketing (split by websocket connection-id) as a more robust fix and add an opt-in config for enterprises using multiplexers. Engineering owner: Liam O'Connor (rate-limiter). Related work: design doc draft in linked Confluence page.
comments: 2026-03-12 09:22 - Priya Natarajan (Support): Customer reported first occurrence at 08:50 UTC. Provided sample request IDs and replay steps. Asked for priority escalation due to clinical impact.

2026-03-12 09:35 - Liam O'Connor (Eng): Reproduced locally using multiplexed client simulator. Observed per-key bucket depletion with short high-frequency bursts. Requesting logs and the customer's proxy implementation details.

2026-03-12 10:01 - NimbusHealth (Customer): Provided client multiplexer code snippet and evidence that they intentionally multiplex to reduce connection count. Asked for recommended best-practice config.
2026-03-12 11:20 - Priya Natarajan (Support): Applied emergency per-key burst uplift for 2 hours to reduce immediate user impact. Noted that uplift should be reverted after stability validated.
2026-03-13 08:47 - Liam O'Connor (Eng): Created experimental branch to introduce connection-scoped buckets and run canary in staging. PR link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4872
2026-03-14 07:15 - Priya Natarajan (Support): Customer confirmed UX improved after uplift but requests permanent config guidance and a durable fix.
source.resolution_notes: Short-term: emergency per-key burst uplift applied for NimbusHealth while engineering builds a connection-scoped bucket model. Medium-term: roadmap item to support optional per-connection and per-subscription rate-limiter keys for enterprise customers using multiplexers. Action items: 1) Engineering to ship connection-bucketing change and add integration tests (owner: Liam O'Connor). 2) Support to publish best-practice note: avoid sharing API key across high-fanout multiplex clients; prefer per-client subkeys or per-connection tokens. 3) Postmortem/learnings to Confluence and notify account team before uplift revert. Follow-up ticket INT-33377 created for infra work.

source.summary: Configuring sustained vs burst quota for multiplexed ingestion workers to avoid 429s
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer Arcadia Labs reports recurring 429 throttles during steady-state ingestion runs from a fleet of short-lived uploader workers. They see 429s even when token usage looks within expected monthly/rolling quotas.

Impact: Intermittent failed uploads (10-25% of requests during 1–2 hour ingestion windows), delays in downstream indexing, and elevated retry traffic causing secondary waves of throttles. No billing disputes opened, but customer is concerned about reliability for nightly syncs.

Environment: self_serve tenant in prod (us-east primary, eu-west failover). Workers spawn ephemeral API keys (lifetime ~5–10 minutes) and open 20–50 concurrent connections per worker process. Ingestion pattern: steady sustained throughput ~120 requests/s for 90–120 minutes, with microbursts when workers restart.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create N ephemeral keys and fan out 300 worker processes across 3 hosts.
2) Each worker initiates 20 parallel streaming sessions to /v1/generate for small prompts (avg 64 tokens).
3) Start all workers within a 90 second window (simulates a deployment rollout).
4) Observe 429 responses beginning ~30s after start; persists intermittently for the ingestion window.

Expected behavior: Smooth sustained throughput with occasional short-lived bursts handled by token bucket bursting.

Actual behavior: Tenant-level quota tokens are exhausted and 429s appear despite per-key buckets showing available tokens for some keys. 429s correlate with worker restarts and the fanout of ephemeral keys.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Provision 300 ephemeral keys (5m TTL) and assign to uploader processes. 2) Start processes within a short window so many keys become active simultaneously. 3) Each process makes 20 concurrent streaming starts to the same route (/v1/generate). 4) Monitor 429 rate and token-bucket metrics in the console and edge logs.
source.logs: Edge metrics (2026-03-10 02:14:xx - 02:20:xx): tenant_token_exhaustion_count=482, per_key_429s=variable, route_concurrency_exhausted=312. Example trace id: trace-6b3f9a2c -> 429 reason=TOKEN_BUCKET_EXHAUSTED tenant_id=arcadia-001 window_ms=60000. Gateway logs show many short-lived connections closed/reopened: "connection reset by peer" during the warmup. No 5xx spikes observed on serving-runtime endpoints.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:21 - Tara Nguyen: Customer opened asking why 429s appear even when individual API keys look under quota; they provided a short call replay and ingestion timeline.

2026-03-11 11:03 - Jordan Blake: Initial triage - hypothesis: ephemeral-key fanout causing tenant token accounting to consume burst window quickly due to per-key bucket seeding and per-tenant accounting (burst shared across tenant). Asking SRE to pull token-bucket time-series for the window.

2026-03-12 14:56 - SRE (A. Patel): Pulled edge metrics. Observed synchronized increase in active keys and a matching steep drop in tenant burst tokens. Provided links to Grafana panels and request traces. Suggested test with staggered worker start and longer-lived keys.

2026-03-13 10:08 - Support (Jordan Blake): Proposed mitigations: increase worker key TTLs to reduce fresh-key fanout, stagger rollouts over 10+ minutes, and implement jittered backoff on 429s. Asked customer to try a small experiment with staggered start and to share results.

2026-03-14 08:37 - Arcadia Labs (customer): Performed staggered rollout and reduced key churn; 429s dropped to <3% but not eliminated. Requesting configuration guidance for sustained ingestion patterns and whether dedicated quota options exist for self_serve tenants.

2026-03-14 15:02 - Jordan Blake: Confirmed follow-up to provide written guidance and product options. Escalating to product for clarifying console controls and to sales for Dedicated readiness if they need SLA-backed capacity.
source.investigation_notes:
What we looked at: token bucket traces (tenant and per-key), edge proxy connection lifecycle, console quota charts, and gateway + serving runtime metrics. Findings: 1) Tenant burst bucket is shared and seeded infrequently relative to key churn; when many short-lived keys activate simultaneously the effective burst capacity is consumed quickly. 2) Per-key buckets can show availability because they are provisioned independently, but tenant-level accounting triggers 429s once tenant windows/credits are exhausted. 3) Worker restart storms (many processes starting within the same 60–120s) produce microbursts that outpace the refill rate. 4) No evidence of misbilling or token double-counting. 5) No serving-runtime overload; 429s originate at gateway/rate-limiter.

Hypotheses tested: staging replay of customer pattern with staggered vs simultaneous starts showed stagger reduces 429s significantly. Increasing simulated refill rate reduced 429s but is not available to self_serve tenants via console. Verified that route-level quotas are supported on Dedicated and can be used to isolate heavy ingestion routes from interactive routes.
source.resolution_notes:
Workarounds provided: 1) Reduce API key churn — prefer reusing longer-lived keys (10–60m) for uploader pools. 2) Stagger rollout/warmup of worker processes over several minutes (introduce randomized start offsets). 3) Implement exponential backoff with full jitter on 429s (suggested initial backoff: 200ms -> 2s, max 8s) and limit aggressive retry concurrency when receiving 429s. 4) Group uploads into slightly larger batches (amortize connection overhead). 5) If using ephemeral keys for security reasons, pre-warm keys one at a time before starting concurrent streams to avoid simultaneous token seeding.

Product/longer-term suggestions (for internal follow-up): 1) Add a console-visible "burst budgeting" indicator for self_serve tenants showing tenant-level burst credit and estimated refill. 2) Consider exposing a configurable burst window or paid add-on for high-sustained ingestion patterns (similar to Dedicated reserved capacity but for burst smoothing). 3) Document best practices for ephemeral-key patterns and provide a short runbook template.

Customer outcome: After applying recommended changes (stagger, reuse keys, jittered backoff), Arcadia Labs reduced 429s from ~15% to <3% and accepted the tweaks as immediate mitigation. They're evaluating a Dedicated trial for larger nightly syncs; sales notified.
