source.summary: Edge probe split causes half-formed streamed messages after reconnect
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer reports that after an intermittent network disconnect, SSE streams resume but the first resumed payloads are half-formed JSON fragments and the client parser fails. Impact: Several NexaHealth production workflows ingesting streamed clinical summaries are producing invalid partial objects; customer reports ~15% of resumed sessions encounter parse errors and drops. Environment: prod, us-east, edge-proxy path through third-party CDN probe that occasionally inserts synthetic TCP probe packets. Observed behavior: On reconnect the server sends a resumed chunk that starts mid-UTF8 sequence and omits the JSON array opener; client logs "JSON parse error: unexpected token". Expected behavior: Stream resume should deliver complete, well-delimited framing and finalization events so clients can stitch without parse errors. Business impact: High — downstream ingestion pipeline discards incomplete records, causing missing clinical notes in processing.

Initial customer-provided details: NexaHealth observed issue at approx 2026-03-13T20:12:34Z during a regional CDN reroute. They captured client-side SDK logs and network capture showing a tiny probe RST/ACK/half-close followed by resumed SSE payloads with missing leading bytes.

Required next steps: Confirm whether edge-probe or load balancer is splitting TCP segments or injecting probes that cause the serving runtime's resume framing to emit a partial fragment; reproduce with controlled probe injection; add defensive guarding in the server resume path to ensure framing re-sync before emitting resumed payloads.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use NexaHealth production-like SSE client with auto-reconnect enabled and streaming enabled for model meta-llama-2-7b
2) Simulate network probe/hard-close from edge (TCP RST then immediate reconnect) or use a test CDN that injects health probes during an open connection
3) Allow client to reconnect and request resume token (client SDK send last-offset header)
4) Observe first resumed chunks emitted by the API and attempt to parse as JSON array of choices
5) Compare capture to baseline where reconnect occurs without edge probe injection
logs: 2026-03-13T20:12:34.112Z EDGE-PROXY INFO conn-id=abc123 probe: injected keepalive sequence
2026-03-13T20:12:34.115Z API-GW DEBUG resume_token=tok_9b8k resume_offset=4821 sending resumed-chunk bytes=128
2026-03-13T20:12:34.116Z SERVING-RUNTIME WARN resume: detected non-delimited-prefix, emitting chunk (len=96)
client-sdk: 2026-03-13T20:12:34.118Z ERROR JSON parse error at byte 0: unexpected token '\u00E2' (partial UTF-8)
investigation_notes: Support triage: reproduced with lab harness by toggling kube-proxy socket keepalive causing half-close during SSE. Repro rate in lab ~20% when small probe inserted within a 10ms window.
SRE checked edge proxy logs and saw probe pattern matching third-party CDN health check (Path: /probe/health) correlated with symptom windows.

Serving runtime analysis: resume path currently trusts resume_offset and replays buffered bytes without an explicit framing resync; if TCP segment boundary aligns inside a UTF-8 multi-byte char or JSON token, client-side parser fails.

Hypothesis: The edge probe triggers a half-close that causes client reconnection path to request resume with an offset that points mid-symbol; server replays from that offset producing a chunk that is not aligned to logical framing boundaries.
Temporary mitigation tested: adding a small defensive resync on server (scan back up to 8 bytes for JSON array opener '[' or '{"') reduces parse errors to <1% in lab without further infra changes.

Repro notes: issue reproduced on both SSE and websocket fallback with binary frames when probe occurred at same timing window. Browser clients are more forgiving due to internal buffering; embedded SDKs fail more often.
comments: Support (2026-03-13 21:02) - Ava Chen: Logged initial ticket after receiving NexusHealth incident report and analyst captures. Customer provided client SDK logs and pcap. Marked P1 due to data loss.

SRE (2026-03-13 22:10) - Priya Singh: Confirmed CDN probe pattern in edge logs. Captured server-side resume offset and observed chunk starting with 0xE2 (mid UTF-8). Hypothesis: resume offset pointing mid-codepoint. Requesting runtime team to review resume semantics.

Runtime Eng (2026-03-14 09:35) - Diego Marquez: Added local patch that scans backward from resume_offset up to 16 bytes to align to a valid UTF-8 boundary and to nearest JSON delimiter when feasible. Lab validation shows significant improvement. Preparing PR for serving-runtime.

Customer (2026-03-14 11:12) - NexaHealth Oncall (M. Jacobs): Confirmed that the mitigation reduced errors in their staging 12h soak. Requesting ETA for hotfix in prod and whether there is an edge configuration to avoid probes during active connections.

SRE (2026-03-14 13:00) - Priya Singh: Reached out to CDN support rep to ask about probe window settings; CDN acknowledged probes can be configured but requires change window and impact assessment. Escalating to account team.
Support (2026-03-14 15:20) - Ava Chen: Noted customer's appetite for a coordinated rollout: runtime hotfix + CDN probe config change. Scheduling maintenance window proposal.

Runtime Eng (2026-03-14 16:45) - Diego Marquez: PR opened: github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/2892 includes defensive resync logic and unit tests covering UTF-8 boundary alignment and JSON delimiter resync. Marked for expedited review.
source.resolution: Workaround applied: enabled defensive resume resync in canary (serving-runtime v1.32.4-canary) which scans backward up to 16 bytes from resume_offset to align to valid UTF-8 and a JSON structural marker. Customer validated staging; errors dropped from ~15% to <1%. Permanent fix path: merge PR 2892, run canary across two us-east clusters, then promote to prod in maintenance window coordinated with CDN probe configuration change to avoid probe injection during long-lived streams.
follow_up_tasks: Merge and deploy PR 2892 to prod canary cluster (assigned to Diego Marquez)
Coordinate with NexaHealth and CDN provider to shift probe timing/config for their traffic (assigned to Priya Singh)
Add unit/integration test that injects probe mid-stream and validates resume alignment (assigned to Runtime QA)
Document resume resync behavior in runbook: confluence.redwood.ai/runbooks/streaming-reconnect-troubleshooting (assigned to Support)
Monitor production for 72h post-rollout; add a dashboard showing resume_parse_errors by region and model (assigned to Observability)

source.summary: Ephemeral port aliasing across multi-NAT egress causes TLS SNI mismatch and intermittent presigned artifact 403s
source.summary_details: Customer reports intermittent 403s when fetching presigned model shards from S3 during inference in a Private deployment. Failures correlate with high serving throughput and appear transient (5–30s), impacting live traffic and causing increased retry storms.
source.impact: Request-level 403s on presigned artifact downloads led to degraded inference throughput and increased tail latency for customer DatumStream. Approximately 8% of shard fetch attempts failed intermittently during peak load windows.
source.description: DatumStream private deployment (VPCE-enabled) started seeing intermittent 403s from presigned S3 URLs used by the serving runtime to stream model shards. The errors are not consistent per-shard; same shard will sometimes succeed and sometimes return 403s for the same task. Client-side retries succeed most of the time. Customer observed increased 5xxs and request retries cascading into throttling-related symptoms.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Deploy private Redwood agent via VPCE and configure NAT gateways across two AZs with per-AZ NAT autoscaling.
2) Start a high-concurrency inference job that streams large model shards (multipart range requests) from S3 using presigned URLs.
3) Observe intermittent 403 errors and range download failures in serving node logs under load; errors are transient and often cleared by a retry.
4) Correlate timestamps with VPC flow logs and NAT gateway metrics to see port reuse spikes during bursty connection patterns.
logs: 2026-03-10T15:03:21Z serving-node-17 ERROR range-download 403 GET https://s3-presigned... range=bytes=0-1048575
2026-03-10T15:03:21Z nat-gw-1 conntrack DROP: existing mapping to ephemeral srcport 51234 reused for different backend IP
2026-03-10T15:03:22Z control-plane auth WARN failed artifact validation: host header mismatch
2026-03-10T15:03:22Z agent debug HTTP/2 stream reset by peer (RST_STREAM) after TLS alert (unexpected_message)
investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis considered SNI stripping or proxy TLS-inspection (common causes for 403/presigned mismatch). Customer confirmed no inline TLS inspection on egress path.

Packet captures showed short-lived connection handoffs where server responses arrived on an existing socket with a different SNI/Host than the original request (observed via TLS ClientHello/ServerHello pair mismatch in timing windows).
VPC flow logs and NAT gateway metrics show ephemeral source-port reuse across AZ NATs during concurrent multipart downloads and NAT autoscale events.

Conntrack table churn correlated with high concurrency and autoscaling of NAT instances; some NAT instances performed asymmetric SNAT mapping due to per-AZ egress path changes, causing return packets to be attributed to the wrong TCP/TLS 5-tuple at the serving node (short window where same ephemeral port mapped to different origIP:origPort).

This produced 'host header/SNI mismatch' symptoms at application layer manifesting as presigned URL signature verification failures (S3 treats request as for a different host or invalid signature) and occasional TLS level resets.
source.root_cause: Race between NAT instance churn and ephemeral port reuse under high concurrency: transient SNAT mapping collisions and asymmetric return path mapping led to responses being delivered to sockets associated with different TLS SNI/Host state, causing signature/host mismatches and 403s for presigned S3 fetches.
mitigation_actions_taken: 1) Short-term: disabled multi-AZ NAT autoscale flapping for the customer's deployment and pinned NAT egress to per-AZ static NAT instances to reduce churn (customer-approved).
2) Applied temporary iptables rule on serving nodes to increase conntrack timeout and reduce recycling of ephemeral ports for the duration of peak load window.
3) Increased DNS TTL on internal split-horizon records from 30s to 120s to reduce control-plane resolver churn while NAT pinning is in effect.
4) Implemented a temporary serving runtime retry backoff to avoid retry storms while network paths stabilized.
source.resolution: Root cause validated via packet captures and reproducer in staging. Long-term fix scheduled: adjust redwood agent egress behavior to prefer stable per-connection SNAT affinity and add NAT-aware port-allocation to avoid ephemeral port collisions across NAT instances; upstream engineering ticket created to harden conntrack handling and NAT affinity logic.
source.resolution_workaround: Pin NAT egress by AZ and increase conntrack ephemeral port range/timeout on serving nodes. These mitigations reduced observed 403 rate from ~8% to ~0.1% during customer verification window.
long_term_plan: 1) Engineering ticket to add explicit SNAT affinity/port pinning in agent (ETA: 2 sprints).
2) Update VPCE deployment docs recommending static NAT instances or provider-managed sticky SNAT when running high concurrent multipart shard downloads.
3) Add proactive monitoring for ephemeral port reuse and conntrack churn in Console alerts (runbook page to include thresholds and playbook).
4) Postmortem and customer-facing RCA draft scheduled after full remediation.
next_steps: - Diego to coordinate a controlled canary of NAT-affinity agent change in staging (2026-03-16).
- Solutions engineer (assigned) to walk DatumStream through recommended VPCE NAT configuration and confirm rollback/adjustments are safe for their network team (scheduled 2026-03-14).
- SRE to add a temporary Dashboard panel for conntrack churn and ephemeral port reuse per AZ (ETA 2 days).
comments: Ava Chen (Support): Customer first reported at 2026-03-10 14:58 UTC. Attached logs show pattern during peak shard traffic.
Diego Morales (SRE): Confirmed correlation between NAT autoscale events and spike in ephemeral port reuse. Recommending pinning NAT for immediate mitigation.
DatumStream (Customer - ops): We can pin NATs temporarily; will need instructions for our cloud team. Impact window is business-critical mornings (14:00–18:00 UTC).
Diego Morales (SRE): Applied NAT pinning and conntrack timeout increase at 2026-03-11 02:10 UTC. Customer verified reduced 403s in subsequent load test.
Ava Chen (Support): Opening engineering ticket to implement SNAT affinity logic: related PR linked above. Will keep customer in the loop and schedule RCA.
source.customer_communication: Initial acknowledgement sent 2026-03-10; provided mitigation steps and ETA for long-term fix. Customer requested regular updates; next update scheduled 2026-03-14.
follow_up_tasks: Create engineering story to implement NAT affinity and port allocation changes (linked PR referenced for initial prototype).
Document recommended VPCE deployment pattern for high-concurrency shard downloads in Solutions KB.
Draft RCA and customer-facing postmortem.

title: Ephemeral port aliasing across multi-NAT egress causes TLS SNI mismatch and intermittent presigned artifact 403s
preview: DatumStream private deployment (VPCE-enabled) started seeing intermittent 403s from presigned S3 URLs used by the serving runtime to stream model shards. The errors are not consistent per-shard; same shard will sometimes succeed and sometimes return 403s for the same task. Client-side retries succeed most of the time. Customer observed increased 5xxs and request retries cascading into throttling-related symptoms.

source.summary: SSE TLS 0-RTT resumption during edge handoff produces partial markdown code fence and drops trailing list items
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer experiences truncated SSE streams when TLS 1.3 0-RTT (early data) is used and the client path migrates across edge nodes. The truncation consistently removes the closing markdown code fence (```), and drops the final 1-3 list items from enumerations. Impact:
- Customer's live documentation generator (NoteWave) shows malformed Markdown and missing bullet points in ~4% of streaming sessions during peak traffic.
- Affects user-facing web previews; breaks downstream parsers that expect closed code blocks.
Environment:
- Customer: NoteWave Labs (dedicated tenant)
- API: Hosted Redwood SSE endpoint via api.redwood.ai (prod-us-east)
- Model: gpt-redwood-3b-v1 pinned version 2026-02-25
- Network: client mobile app on LTE -> public mobile NAT -> CDN edge -> Redwood edge proxy -> serving pool
Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Use mobile app with TLS 1.3 0-RTT enabled (client uses early data session resumption).
2) Start an SSE generation request that returns multi-paragraph Markdown with code fences and an ordered list at the end.
3) Trigger a short path migration by toggling mobile data (airplane mode on/off) or causing the client IP to change during streaming.
Observed behavior:
- Stream closes without an explicit end-of-stream event.
- Final code fence closing triple backticks are missing.
- Last 1-3 enumerated bullets are dropped.
- On reconnect the resumed stream may replay a short prefix but never retransmits the missing trailing tokens.
Reproducer used by Support/SRE:
- curl -N --http3 --resolve api.redwood.ai:443:34.201.10.7 \n  -H 'Accept: text/event-stream' \n  -H 'Early-Data: 1' \n  --data-binary @payload.json https://api.redwood.ai/v1/streams/generate
- Observed same truncation when simulating sudden TCP teardown and SYN from new IP within 1s window.
Logs / traces:
- Trace IDs: trace-20260312-8b4a, trace-20260312-8b4b (attached redacted).
- Edge proxy (node-edge-7) shows connection close with TLS 0-RTT early_data_aborted at timestamp 2026-03-12T09:42:21.203Z.
- Serving runtime (worker r-14) forwarded stream with end-of-stream absent; internal stream offset reached but tail tokens not flushed to client.
Hypothesis:
- Early-data (0-RTT) session resumption interacts with connection handoff logic in the edge proxy: when a RESUME + path change occurs, the edge prematurely signals half-close without flushing buffered trailing chunks for streaming SSE sessions.
- There may be a mismatch between chunk boundaries used for prefix replay and finalization token emission when early-data is used (edge discards tail CRLF sequence that marks completion).
Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Disable TLS 0-RTT / Early Data on the client (mobile) while we investigate. Customer reports immediate disappearance of the truncation after that change.
- As alternate mitigation, switch to short polling instead of SSE for critical flows until fix is deployed.
Severity and business impact assessment:
- Functional: User-facing content corruption for a subset of streaming sessions; parser-sensitive flows can fail.
- Business: Dedicated customer on reserved capacity; risk to renewal if not addressed.
- Classification: Sev1 (P1) for this customer due to data integrity impact.
Next steps / proposed action items:
1) SRE: instrument edge proxy to log early-data/0rtt handoff events with stream offsets and buffered chunk counts (actioned).
2) Dev: add defensive flush on handoff path to ensure trailing CRLF and close-token are emitted before half-close is propagated (investigation PR drafted).
3) QA: create deterministic test that simulates 0-RTT resume + path migration with SSE and checks for markdown closure.
4) Rollout: patch proxy in a canary region and validate with NoteWave test harness before global rollout.
Investigation notes summary:
- 2026-03-12 10:20Z: reproduced by SRE on staging using synthetic early-data client; confirmed missing final tokens.
- 2026-03-12 15:05Z: Eng noted that proxy code path for early-data uses a separate chunk buffer which wasn't being drained on connection rebind.
- 2026-03-13 08:40Z: Temporary client-side toggle confirmed as mitigation.
- 2026-03-13 12:50Z: PR #452 in realtime-proxy adds drain-on-handoff and a test.
Open questions for engineering:
- Does the same issue affect websocket-based streaming (client reported primarily SSE, but websockets may share close logic)?
- Are there any model/kernel-specific interactions (KV cache flush timing) that affect the final token emission?
comments: Customer (NoteWave Support) 2026-03-12T10:00Z: Provided example session IDs and minimal payloads; noted they only see the issue when their Android app has 0-RTT enabled.
Support (Ava Chen) 2026-03-12T11:10Z: Confirmed receipt of logs and asked customer to temporarily disable 0-RTT; customer agreed and validated mitigation.
SRE (Marcus Rivera) 2026-03-12T14:35Z: Reproduced on staging; collected proxy debug traces and attached to ticket. Hypothesis: buffered tail not drained on early-data abort.
Eng (Priya Nair) 2026-03-13T09:12Z: Investigated proxy code path; found missing drain call in early-data rebind branch. Drafted PR and added unit + integration test to simulate path migration.
Support (Ava Chen) 2026-03-13T09:30Z: Notified customer that we have a fix candidate and will schedule a canary rollout. Asked customer for a preferred test window.
SRE (Marcus Rivera) 2026-03-13T13:05Z: Canary deployment started in us-east-1; running NoteWave harness against canary edge for 2 hours.

source.summary: Dedicated cluster: GPU contention causing sustained throughput collapse and autoscale oscillation
source.description:
Issue summary:
Scanly Labs reports a sharp, sustained drop in throughput from their dedicated GPU pool in us-west starting 2026-03-11 09:12 UTC. Latency for streaming generation requests increases and many requests time out. The Redwood autoscaler began oscillating (rapid scale-up then scale-down) around the same time, making the throughput degradation persistent.

Impact:
- Production traffic for Scanly Labs' inference endpoints degraded: 60-80% drop in token throughput on pinned routes.
- Errors observed: tail latency spikes >10s, client-side timeouts, and a small increase in 5xx from serving-runtime.
- Customer-facing feature (real-time report generation) impacted for ~80% of requests routed to dedicated pool.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: scanly-prod-pool-usw (reserved 12 GPUs across 3 nodes)
- Region: us-west
- Models: gptj-compat-13b (primary), redwood-rlhf-7b (fallback)

Observed behaviour / logs summary:
- Prometheus graphs show GPU utilization uneven: some GPUs pegged near 98% memory usage, others at 5-10% idle.
- Node exporter shows frequent GPU ECC/corrected error increases on node gpu-02-usw.
- Autoscaler logs: frequent scale events every 3-7 minutes triggered by queue growth, followed by immediate scale-down when some requests fail with GPU allocation error.
- Kubelet logs on affected nodes contain repeated container OOM and GPU allocation timeout messages.

Steps to reproduce (customer pattern):
1) Send steady production traffic to pinned model route for 30+ minutes (Scanly load test reproduced issue).
2) Observe queue length and latency grow, autoscaler adds capacity.
3) Some newly added nodes immediately show GPU allocation failures; scheduler reschedules pods causing node churn and eviction spikes.

Initial hypothesis:
- A single tenant process on one node is causing GPU memory fragmentation or a driver-level fault that prevents the scheduler from placing pods evenly, leading to practical GPU starvation on some nodes (noisy-tenant-like behaviour).
- Autoscaler is reacting to queue length but not accounting for allocation failures, so it oscillates and amplifies instability.
- Potential hardware/driver issue on gpu-02-usw (ECC counters, thermal events) contributing to allocation timeouts.

Customer's immediate ask:
- Restore stable throughput and stop autoscale oscillation.
- Short-term mitigation while root cause is identified.

References: slack #support-scanly (thread TS12345), infra PR 2345, Confluence runbook above.
comments: 2026-03-11 10:05 - Priya Natarajan: Triage note: Customer reported at 09:12 UTC. Assigned to SRE on-call. Collected initial logs (prometheus, kube events) and requested a full gpu node health dump from the customer. Marking P1 / Sev1 due to production impact.

2026-03-11 10:30 - Marco Ruiz (SRE): Observed ECC corrected increases on gpu-02-usw and repeated allocation timeouts. Temporarily cordoned gpu-02-usw and rebalanced pods; customer reports slight improvement but autoscaler still oscillating.

2026-03-11 11:00 - Emily Chen (Customer Success): Customer confirms throughput improved from 20% to ~50% after cordon, but latency still elevated. They are running a load test to reproduce; shared logs in the ticket.

2026-03-11 12:10 - Marco Ruiz: Found node-level driver logs referencing NVIDIA UVM leak warnings and several kernel messages around PCIe link state. Hypothesis: driver leak causing fragmentation; raised INT-412 for infra to check driver/firmware state across pool.

2026-03-12 09:45 - Platform Eng (Luis Soto): Ran kube-scheduler predicate trace. Scheduler is preferring nodes with 'gpu.available_memory' label; label values on some nodes stale after recent patch causing imbalanced placements. Created a small patch to update labels from telemetry on schedule (PR referenced).

2026-03-12 14:22 - Marco Ruiz: Applied mitigation: 1) Temporarily increased min replicas in autoscaler policy for scanly-prod-pool-usw to 6 to reduce oscillation sensitivity; 2) cordoned gpu-02-usw for deeper diagnostics; 3) added a short-lived admission hook to avoid scheduling to nodes with ECC errors. Customer reporting throughput ~85% of baseline now.
2026-03-13 08:30 - Scanly Ops (customer): Thanks — throughput better. We see occasional tail latency spikes still during peak. Please advise on long-term fix and whether we need to migrate workload.

2026-03-13 11:00 - Marco Ruiz: Next steps: schedule maintenance window to repro with kernel/driver debug flags, plan firmware/driver upgrade for gpu-02-usw if hardware diagnostics confirm issues, and merge scheduler label sync fix. Open follow-up ticket to review autoscaler logic around allocation failures (INT-435).
investigation_notes: Prometheus: 'gpu_memory_used_bytes' across nodes shows large variance; heatmap indicates sustained pinned allocations on gpu-01 and gpu-02.
Node gpu-02-usw shows repeated 'UVM: process leaked pinned memory' warnings in dmesg timeframe correlating with allocation timeouts.
Kube scheduler predicate results: several pods failed 'InsufficientGPU' despite overall pool-level free memory due to label mismatch and stale telemetry used by custom predicate.
Autoscaler behavior: uses queue growth (HTTP queue length) as primary signal; does not currently decrement score for allocation failure rate, enabling oscillation.
No direct evidence of malicious tenant; more consistent with driver/firmware leak combined with scheduler mislabeling and autoscaler sensitivity.
source.resolution:
Mitigations applied (temporary): 1) cordoned and isolated gpu-02-usw (avoids affected device); 2) increased autoscaler min replicas for the pool to reduce oscillation; 3) deployed admission guard to avoid scheduling on nodes with ECC errors; 4) scheduler label sync fix merged to prevent stale telemetry from driving placement.

Planned long-term actions: firmware/driver validation and upgrade on gpu-02-usw (pending hardware diagnostics), autoscaler change to incorporate allocation-failure signal before scaling decisions (INT-435), runbook update for GPU allocation failure handling. Customer agreed to a maintenance window 2026-03-16 to run driver upgrade and deeper repro with debug flags.

Ticket remains In Progress until maintenance and follow-ups complete.
follow_up_actions: Schedule maintenance window with Scanly: 2026-03-16 04:00-06:00 UTC for driver/firmware upgrade and repro
INT-435: Change autoscaler to factor GPU allocation failures into scale decisions
Perform hardware diagnostics on gpu-02-usw and consider RMA if ECC and PCIe errors persist
Update runbook: add steps to cordon nodes on ECC spikes and to temporarily raise min replicas for dedicated pools during noisy-tenant events

References: slack #support-scanly (thread TS12345), infra PR 2345, Confluence runbook above.

source.summary: Streaming completions time out (504) when client-side HTTP/2 WINDOW_UPDATE is not sent by corporate proxy
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer AcmeFin reports repeated streaming completions from our hosted API returning 504s after long tails of silence. This appears only for some users behind a corporate network appliance.

Impact:
- ~30 requests/hr from AcmeFin experience 40-120s stalls then 504s when generating long completions (>512 tokens).
- End-user UX broken for interactive flows.
- Customer is enterprise tier; impact threatens a contract renewal conversation.

Environment:
- Prod edge (us-east) -> API Gateway (HTTP/2 enabled) -> NGINX sidecars -> serving-runtime GPU pool.
- Observed on models: gpt-open-13b, redwood-rl-7b.

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Customer client opens HTTP/2 connection to /v1/stream with streaming=true.
2) Server starts emitting many small DATA frames (KV-warmup + prefix output) over the stream for ~30-90s.
3) Corporate proxy between client and our edge silently fails to send WINDOW_UPDATE or stalls sending it back to our edge.
4) Our APIGW side hits server-side flow-control limits and begins buffering; write stalls accumulate; eventually the edge or NLB resets the connection resulting in 504/connection reset.

Observed behavior / evidence:
- tcpdump at edge (pid 2710) shows server DATA frames sent with no corresponding WINDOW_UPDATE from client side for ~65s (trace id f7a3...).
- APIGW metrics: per-request egress bytes continue but send queue grows; connection_write_latency spikes correlated with 504s.
- NGINX error log excerpt: "upstream timed out (110: Connection timed out) while sending request to upstream" at timestamps matching customer traces.
- No abnormal CPU/GPU load on serving nodes. Latency regression not seen for clients on consumer ISPs.

Hypothesis:
- The customer's corporate proxy (or a middlebox) implements a buggy HTTP/2 implementation that ignores or delays WINDOW_UPDATE frames under certain conditions (e.g., when TLS inspection is enabled). This prevents our edge from progressing flow-control, causing buffers to fill and the gateway to hit its connection idle/write timeouts. This is a network-level backpressure problem, not an application-level compute regression.

Investigation notes:
- Captured pcap at edge for two failed sessions and one successful session; comparison shows missing WINDOW_UPDATE frames only for failing sessions.
- Correlated with customer's proxy vendor (identified in support call) and their proxy logs showing HTTP/2 stream state transitions being held open for >60s.
- Reproduced internally by inserting a test proxy that delays WINDOW_UPDATE; identical behavior observed (write stalls -> 504).
- Confirmed sidecar NGINX configured to allow HTTP/2; keepalive and send timeouts default to 60s.

Workaround applied to customer (temporary):
1) Disabled HTTP/2 on the edge route for AcmeFin (fall back to HTTP/1.1), which eliminates the WINDOW_UPDATE negotiation and immediately resolved the 504s for their streaming requests.
2) Adjusted sidecar send_timeout to 120s for the customer route to reduce immediate resets while longer-term fix is evaluated.

Proposed long-term remediation options:
- Option A (preferred): Route affected customers to HTTP/1.1 on the edge when middlebox incompatibility is detected. Implement server-side detection heuristic for missing WINDOW_UPDATE patterns and auto-fallback.
- Option B: Introduce small server-side coalescing window: buffer small DATA frames up to X ms to reduce frequency of WINDOW_UPDATE reliance for proxies that mishandle fine-grained updates. Requires careful latency testing.
- Option C: Document recommended proxy settings for enterprise customers (TLS inspection exceptions or HTTP/2 compatibility modes) and include in onboarding checklist for Private/Dedicated sales.

Next steps assigned:
- Diego: roll out per-customer HTTP/2 fallback toggle and automation (P0 follow-up).
- SRE (L. Park): add metric/alert for "no WINDOW_UPDATE for >30s while server sending" to detect other affected customers.
- Support (Ava): coordinate with AcmeFin to engage their proxy vendor and share pcap evidence.

Status: mitigation applied (HTTP/2 disabled for AcmeFin), root cause strongly correlated to client-side proxy behavior, longer-term automation planned.
comments: 2026-03-12 09:14 - Ava Chen: Customer reports 'stream hangs for ~1m then errors out' on interactive flows. Provided request IDs and times. Routed to SRE for packet capture.

2026-03-12 10:02 - Diego Martinez (SRE): Captured tcpdump on edge; initial look shows many server DATA frames with no WINDOW_UPDATE for failing flows. Suspect middlebox. Creating internal reproduction proxy to validate.

2026-03-12 13:45 - Logan Park: Confirmed NGINX send_timeout default (60s) is causing premature upstream timeouts when client window doesn't advance. Temporarily increased send_timeout on customer route to 120s as stopgap.
2026-03-12 15:20 - Ava Chen: Applied HTTP/2 disable on route for AcmeFin; customer reports streaming restored. Coordinating a call with customer's network team tomorrow to share pcap.

2026-03-13 11:05 - Diego Martinez: Created PR for auto-fallback heuristic (detect 30s of send stall without WINDOW_UPDATE -> set connection-level header to force HTTP/1.1 next attempt). Linked PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/4432
source.resolution: Mitigated (HTTP/2 disabled for customer route). Automation/heuristic work in progress.
follow_up_tasks: Instrument EDGE to surface missing WINDOW_UPDATE patterns in traces
Implement per-customer HTTP/2 auto-fallback heuristic
Add guidance to enterprise onboarding docs about HTTP/2/TLS-inspection compatibility

source.summary: Payload-size throttle triggers upstream restart cycle and elevated 5xx for large embedding uploads
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer BrightChat reports a sustained elevation in 5xx responses beginning 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC during large embedding multipart uploads. Impact: multiple clients seeing ~15-30% failed requests (5xx) for embedding endpoints; customer observed timeouts and client-side retry storms. Environment: prod us-east; self-serve tenant; burst of concurrent multipart uploads (~100 parallel multipart sessions from a single customer API key). Steps to reproduce:
1) Use BrightChat embedding bulk uploader with 100 parallel workers, each submitting multipart upload with ~12MB parts (total ~120MB per session).
2) Observe API gateway accept-queue growth and some requests truncated by body parser.
3) After sustained churn (~3–5 minutes), upstream serving-runtime processes restart (watchdog), causing 502/503/504 depending on stage.
Observed behavior: apigw emits 502->503 waves; logs show repeated body-parser WARNs about exceeding per-request buffer, then runtime processes OOM or watchdog-triggered restart. Keepalive socket counts increase (stuck half-closed), accept queue saturates and proxy starts dropping new connections. Expected behavior: large uploads should be gracefully rejected with 413 Payload Too Large or accepted and streamed to backend without triggering runtime restarts.
Key logs (excerpts):
- apigw: "body-parser: payload buffer exceeded; rejecting part" (2026-03-12T02:12:03Z)
- ingress-proxy: "accept_queue_len=512 -> 2048 in 30s" (2026-03-12T02:12:35Z)
- runtime: "watchdog: process pid=2345 killed due to vmem spike" (2026-03-12T02:13:10Z)
- kernel:dmesg shows oom_kill for python-worker pid 2345 at 02:13:12Z
Additional notes: customer uses a naïve uploader that sends large parts and performs retries without backoff, amplifying load. The issue appears to be a composite of apigw body-size buffer behavior, upload-service buffering strategy, and runtime memory handling for multipart streams.
comments: 2026-03-12 03:05 - Ava Chen (Support): Initial triage completed; escalated to SRE team and opened this incident. Informed customer we are investigating and suggested temporary client-side throttling.

2026-03-12 03:20 - Lucas Rivera (SRE): Reproduced locally with simulated uploader traffic. Observed per-request buffer allocation in upload-service grows to full part size (12MB) before streaming to runtime; combined with 100 parallel sessions this trips process vmem threshold. Filed runtime concern to engineering.

2026-03-12 04:00 - SRE (on-call): Short-term mitigation applied: lowered apigw per-connection max buffer to 4MB and enabled early 413 response for >8MB parts for affected route. This reduced immediate OOM restarts and lowered 5xx rate to ~3%.

2026-03-12 09:12 - BrightChat (customer): Confirmed reduction in failures after advised rate-limiting and suggested uploader changes; however they still see intermittent 5xx during spikes. Asked for permanent fix timeline.

2026-03-12 11:45 - Meera Patel (Runtime eng): Found that runtime's multipart handler eagerly materializes parts into memory for checksum/validation; added an investigation note and a draft PR that changes checksum to streaming mode (runtime PR linked).

2026-03-13 07:30 - Lucas Rivera (SRE): Deployed hotfix pipeline: patched upload-service to stream parts directly to temp file and apply backpressure to upstream; rolling deployment across us-east completed at 07:12 UTC. Monitoring shows 5xx back down to baseline (~0.2%).
2026-03-13 08:05 - Ava Chen (Support): Notified customer of fix and asked them to resume normal upload cadence and report any anomalies.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: body-parser per-connection buffer plus naive uploader leads to cumulative memory pressure and runtime OOMs.
Hypothesis 2: keepalive socket handling combined with half-closed streams caused accept-queue to remain saturated (delayed close), amplifying upstream pressure.
Additional finding: no sign of kernel-level IO stalls; nvme metrics normal. nvml logs show no GPU resets; this is CPU/memory pressure in runtime processes handling multipart parsing.
Dashboards referenced: /dashboards/ingress/accept-queue, /dashboards/runtime/mem-usage, /dashboards/apigw/5xx-rate (see confluence runbook link).
source.resolution: Hotfix deployed: upload-service switched to streaming checksums and temp-file spill for parts; apigw early-reject threshold lowered and improved 413 responses for oversized parts. Runtime multipart handler PR #2745 merged to make checksums streaming-first. Advised customer to upgrade uploader to chunk <=8MB and add exponential backoff on retries. Long-term: add per-tenant concurrent upload caps and improve keepalive reclamation logic in ingress-proxy.

source.summary: WAF header rewrite causing subaccount quota attribution shift and unexpected 429s on private deployment
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightCart (private deployment in eu-west) reported intermittent 429 throttles that started after a WAF policy change rolled out on their edge. The 429s are concentrated on a single subaccount and coincide with high background traffic from a third-party image-optimizer pipeline.

Impact:
- Several storefront ingestion workers began receiving 429s between 03:20-04:05 UTC on 2026-03-12, causing delayed catalog updates for ~12% of jobs.
- Customer reports intermittent user-facing slowness for product updates during the window.

Environment:
- Private deployment (customer-managed VPC) running Redwood private control plane (v1.8.3).
- Edge WAF sits in front of the customer ingress and applies custom header rewrites.

Observed behavior:
- Requests that previously attributed usage to distinct subaccounts have been attributed to a single subaccount id (subacct-9812) after the WAF rule change.
- Rate-limiter logs show token deductions against subacct-9812 for requests that include an X-Customer-Subacct header rewritten by the WAF into a header we use for subaccount mapping.

Expected behavior:
- WAF should not rewrite or drop the identity headers used for subaccount attribution, or the rewrite should preserve original values so per-subaccount quotas remain correct.

Notes:
- Customer rolled out a WAF rule to add an X-Forwarded-User header earlier the same morning. That rule also normalized Authorization headers for downstream routing. The normalization appears to have removed or replaced the original X-Customer-Subacct header in some flows.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) On BrightCart private deployment with WAF rule active: send mixed requests from two different API keys that map to subacct-9812 and subacct-1173 respectively.
2) Observe ingress access logs at WAF and at Redwood edge for header transformations.
3) Check rate-limiter logs for token deductions and which subaccount ID was decremented.
4) Temporarily disable the WAF header rewrite and repeat the mixed-request run to confirm attribution returns to per-subaccount buckets.
logs: 2026-03-12T03:22:14Z edge.rate-limiter INFO deduct subaccount=subacct-9812 tokens=45 request_id=be3f3a
2026-03-12T03:22:14Z edge.access INFO path=/v1/generate headers={"X-Customer-Subacct":"subacct-1173", "Authorization":"REDACTED"} -> routed_subacct=subacct-9812
2026-03-12T03:22:15Z waf.transform DEBUG POST /v1/generate rewrites: {drop: ["X-Customer-Subacct"], add: {"X-Forwarded-User":"svc-image-opt"}}
2026-03-12T03:25:02Z edge.rate-limiter WARN 429 path=/v1/generate subaccount=subacct-9812 window_exhausted=true request_id=cf7a91
investigation_notes: - Correlated spike of 429s with timestamp of WAF rule rollout (03:18-03:20 UTC).
- Access logs show original X-Customer-Subacct present at origin client but dropped/overwritten by WAF in ~60% of sampled requests.

- Rate-limiter attribution relies on preserved X-Customer-Subacct header in this customer's private routing configuration. When missing, system falls back to tenant-level id which maps to subacct-9812 due to customer routing table ordering.
- Reproduced locally by applying the same header rewrite at an upstream proxy: saw identical 429 pattern and single-subacct consumption.
- No change observed in model-serving or token accounting code paths; issue is attributed to upstream header mutation.
comments: 2026-03-12 08:02 - BrightCart (Samir Patel): Noticed increased 429s after we enabled a WAF rule to tag requests from our image optimizer. Only started after the rule pushed. Can you confirm if rewrites would affect quota?

2026-03-12 09:10 - Elena Morris (Support): Thanks — we're seeing an exact time correlation. We're investigating whether header rewriting could change subaccount attribution. Could you share a recent request_id (we asked for cf7a91) and the WAF rule text?
2026-03-12 10:05 - BrightCart (Samir Patel): Provided WAF rule; example logs attached (see logs above).

2026-03-12 11:30 - Mateo Alvarez (SRE): Ran repro with a controlled proxy rewrite. Confirmed WAF drop of X-Customer-Subacct causes fallback to tenant default which is subacct-9812. Suggested mitigation: change WAF to preserve X-Customer-Subacct or add X-Redwood-Subacct header with original value.

2026-03-12 12:10 - Elena Morris (Support): Temporary workaround applied: customer disabled the rewrite and 429s dropped to baseline within 5m. We're coordinating a permanent rule change with customer WAF team and opening an internal task to add defensive logging when subaccount header missing.
source.resolution: Workaround: Customer disabled the WAF header rewrite at 2026-03-12T12:05 UTC which immediately stopped the concentrated 429s. Root cause: upstream WAF rule dropped/overwrote the X-Customer-Subacct header used for per-subaccount quota mapping. Fix applied: updated WAF rule to preserve original X-Customer-Subacct value and add X-Forwarded-User as an additional header (no overwrite). On Redwood side, we created INT-4412 to implement a defensive check and metric: emit a ‘missing-subacct-header’ telemetry event when fallback attribution occurs. Follow-up: recommend customer add tests to their WAF rollout playbook and we've shared a short runbook on safe header rewrites in private deployments (see related_confluence_pages).
follow_up_tasks: INT-4412: Add metric and alert for missing subaccount header fallback (assigned to SRE).
Ask BrightCart to add WAF rewrite integration test in CI to prevent regressions.
Documentation: add WAF header guidance to private deployment onboarding checklist.

source.summary: Intermittent tail-drain timeouts for very large max_tokens / long-prefix streaming requests
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customers are observing intermittent streaming disconnects and finalization timeouts when submitting requests with very large max_tokens (>=150k) and extended conversation history (many system/user messages). The generation runs for tens of seconds and then the client receives a 504 or streaming connection is closed without final chunk. This appears to correlate with sustained long-token drains and sudden KV cache thrashing on Dedicated pools.

Impact:
- Affected customer (NoteChat Inc., dedicated) reports ~10% of long-run streaming completions fail to finalize, causing truncated transcripts and user-visible errors.
- Business impact: production feature degradation for premium customers; customers are throttling long-context usage.

Environment:
- Dedicated GPU pool (g4dn-like), prod-us-east, isolated tenant.
- Requests routed to serving runtime version v1.48.0 with batching enabled and default cache policy.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming starts normally; progress chunks arrive for long periods. Around 30-90s into generation the stream stalls and then client sees a 504 or socket closed. Server logs show finalizer timeout and worker marked as overloaded then request requeued and dropped.

Expected behavior:
- Long-generation requests should stream to completion (within documented SLO) or be failed with graceful error that includes diagnostic id.

Logs / traces / evidence:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-20260310-7b3f, trace-us-east-20260311-c9a1.
- SRE dashboard: spike in finalizer-queue-time and kv-cache-evict rate between 03/10 11:12–11:30 UTC.
- Router logs show transient priority-queue backpressure and worker-level contention.

Steps to reproduce (customer repro):
1) Create a conversation with ~40 short turn messages (system + user tokens ~70k) as prefix.
2) Submit a single streaming generation with max_tokens set to 160000 and temperature 0.7 using model redwood-3b-quant.
3) Observe streaming chunks for ~20–60s, then either final chunk missing or 504 returned.

Workaround attempted by customer:
- Reducing max_tokens to 80k reduces occurrences to <1% but limits UX.
- Pinning to a non-quantized variant (llama-2-13b-instruct) changed failure mode to increased latency but fewer finalizer timeouts.
- Disabling client-side streaming and polling for full response caused same server-side timeout after ~2 minutes.
steps_to_reproduce: Assemble a long prefix of ~40 messages totalling ~70k tokens
POST /v1/generate (stream=true) with max_tokens=160000 against redwood-3b-quant on prod-us-east
Observe stream for missing final chunk or 504 after 20-90s
comments: 2026-03-10 12:04 - Support (Aisha): Received initial report from NoteChat via email. Opened P1 ticket due to customer SLA and data loss risk.
2026-03-10 12:20 - SRE (Liam Chen): Correlated with finalizer-queue-time spike on dedicated pool. Suspect KV cache eviction churn when sequence length + max_tokens exceed local cache capacity.

2026-03-10 15:05 - Eng (Maya Patel): Reproduced locally with a stress test harness. Observed worker GC/serialize stall during KV checkpoint under heavy prefix churn. Added instrumentation to capture KV eviction events and finalizer latency.

2026-03-11 09:12 - Support (Aisha): Provided temporary mitigation steps to customer: lower max_tokens, set X-Priority:high to avoid low-priority preemption, and use model variant with higher KV capacity. Customer to test and report results.

2026-03-12 16:30 - Eng (Ethan): Implemented a short-lived change in canary to avoid immediate KV checkpoint during finalization. Canary showed reduction in finalizer timeouts from 9.8% -> 1.7% for same workload.
2026-03-13 10:02 - Customer (NoteChat): Confirmed notable improvement on canary; will schedule migration once patch is in stable channel.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Batching scheduler + very large max_tokens causes worker to hold oversized KV states; periodic KV checkpoint/serialize triggers pauses that block finalizer thread.
Hypothesis 2: Quantized model memory layout leads to more frequent small evictions when prefix contains many small system messages, increasing eviction rate and leading to cache thrash.

Findings: Canary instrumentation shows finalizer-queue-p99 reduced after suppressing synchronous KV checkpoint on completion path. Eviction rate metric (kv.evictions/sec) correlates with observed failures.
Relevant logs: /logs/prod/us-east/serving-runtime/trace-us-east-20260310-7b3f.log, linked to GH PR 1214 for runtime change.
Risk assessment: Suppressing immediate checkpoint reduces durability on node failure window; needs compensating change (background async checkpoint + faster incremental delta).
source.root_cause: Synchronous KV checkpoint and eviction churn on dedicated serving workers under extreme token drains caused finalizer thread starvation and request finalization timeouts.
source.resolution: Deployed a canary patch that defers KV checkpointing from the finalizer critical path and adds a lightweight eviction backoff for streaming requests with very large max_tokens. Patch reduced occurrence rate substantially; planning full rollout after additional validation.
resolution_notes: Canary rollout (v1.49.2-canary) across 10% of dedicated-us-east capacity decreased finalizer timeouts for repro workload from ~9.8% to ~1.7%.

Follow-up work: implement async incremental KV checkpointing (ENG-3492), add throttling guard for extremely large max_tokens (PRODUCT-121), and update docs with recommended limits and priority hints for dedicated customers.
Customer follow-up: NoteChat to migrate to patched pool next week and run their integration tests; Support to monitor for 72h post-migration.
Permanent fix ETA: 2 sprints (estimated 2026-04-10) for full async checkpointing and guard rails.

source.summary: Burst of 5xx (mostly 503/502) traced to runtime threadpool starvation from hedged streaming/prefetch traffic
source.description: Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-12 20:15 UTC we observed a sustained uptick in 5xx errors (predominantly 503 and 502) for a Dedicated customer (BrightWrite Inc.) in us-east. Errors correlate with a heavy number of long-lived streaming sessions that use hedged prefetch requests. Impact: API calls to generation/chat endpoints intermittently return 502/503; streaming sessions abruptly close; customers report degraded latency and dropped responses. Initial hypothesis: runtime threadpool exhaustion / blocking IO in prefetch path leading to upstream failures at the API gateway.
source.impact: Multiple BrightWrite production routes degraded. Estimated 12% of requests for the customer returned 5xx during peak window (~20:15–21:10 UTC). Customer reported feature-affecting outages for end-users (chat sessions drop). Financial impact potentially high for the customer; they are on a Dedicated plan with SLA.
source.timeline:
2026-03-12 20:12 UTC - First alert from synthetic checks.
2026-03-12 20:15 UTC - Customer (BrightWrite) opened support channel reporting dropped streams.
2026-03-12 20:20 UTC - Alerts: spike in upstream 5xx from serving-runtime and api-gateway accept queue growth.
2026-03-12 20:30 UTC - Support engaged SRE and Runtime on-call.
2026-03-12 21:05 UTC - Temporary mitigation (disable hedged prefetch for customer) applied; error rate dropped to baseline by 21:10 UTC.
2026-03-13 10:00 UTC - Follow-up patch PR created to harden streaming prefetch path.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Configure high concurrent streaming sessions to the same tenant (BrightWrite test harness used 1k concurrent streams).
2) Enable hedged prefetch for small prompts so runtime issues speculative prefetch calls.
3) Observe runtime threadpool usage and accept queue depth on api-gateway.
4) Reproduce elevated 503/502 when threadpool threads block on socket reads in prefetch path.
source.logs_and_artifacts:
- api-gateway metrics: accept_queue_depth spiked to 8k, 502/503 rate climbed to 11%.
- serving-runtime logs (us-east-rt-12): repetitive stack traces showing runtime worker threads blocked on synchronous socket read in prefetch handler.
- pstack / thread dumps attached: threadpool showing ~600 blocked threads waiting on io.
- perf profile: high syscall time for epoll_wait / recv.
- customer-provided trace ids: trace-20260312-BW-4512, trace-20260312-BW-4683.
investigation_notes: Initial triage: API gateway reported healthy upstreams but queuing increased; downstream returning 502/503 when upstreams became unresponsive.

Runtime team examined thread dumps and found large count of runtime worker threads in 'blocking socket read' state tied to prefetch/hedge requests. These long-lived streams held threads for extended durations leading to starvation for active generation work.
Hypothesis: hedged prefetch implementation used a synchronous/blocking read path that is safe at low concurrency but causes threadpool fragmentation at scale (combined with a tenant burst pattern).
Perf experiment: disabling hedged prefetch for the tenant immediately lowered blocked thread counts and accept queue depth; error rate returned to baseline (confirms causal link).
Further note: the Dedicated fleet for this customer had slightly smaller runtime threadpool sizing (customer-specified to limit cost), increasing sensitivity to this pattern.
Logs point to socket-level timeouts being extended by client-side keepalives; long tail of idle-but-open prefetch connections prevented reuse of threads.
Related historical tickets flagged a similar resource-pressure pattern but for a different prefetch variant (see SUP-472901).
actions_taken: 2026-03-12 21:05 UTC - Applied emergency mitigation: disabled hedged prefetch for BrightWrite tenant via feature-flag (console toggle).
2026-03-12 21:10 UTC - Increased runtime threadpool soft limit for affected Dedicated nodes to provide immediate headroom.
2026-03-12 22:00 UTC - Adjusted api-gateway accept queue and keepalive timeouts to reduce backlog sensitivity.
2026-03-13 09:30 UTC - Runtime patch developed to convert prefetch handler to non-blocking async reads and add per-stream idle timeout; PR opened.
2026-03-13 10:15 UTC - Customer notified of mitigations and planned patch; support scheduled canary rollout.
2026-03-13 14:00 UTC - Canary rollout on staging and one production shard; monitoring shows no regression at low to medium load.
2026-03-13 17:00 UTC - Plan to roll out to rest of Dedicated nodes with monitoring and rollback window.
source.resolution: Mitigation and remediation in progress. Short-term mitigation (disable hedged prefetch + threadpool soft-limit bump) reduced error rate to baseline. Medium-term fix: runtime PR to implement non-blocking prefetch reads + per-stream idle timeout; targeted for production rollout 2026-03-15 after extended canary. Post-deployment action items include load regression tests, update to Dedicated sizing recommendations, and a runbook change to add hedged-prefetch as a known pressure vector.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-12688 - Backport non-blocking prefetch handler to stable release and schedule production rollout.
OPS-9112 - Update Dedicated cluster sizing doc to call out stream-heavy tenants and recommend threadpool min sizes.
DOCS-452 - Add hedged prefetch troubleshooting steps to streaming runbook.
CS-733 - Reach back to BrightWrite with RCA and SLA credit assessment after full postmortem.
comments: 2026-03-12 20:22 - BrightWrite Support (customer): 'We've been seeing dropped streams and 502s in production for the last 10 minutes. Can you investigate? Our engineering team is on standby.'
2026-03-12 20:28 - Support (Ava Chen): 'Thanks for the report — we've triaged and engaged runtime. We'll apply temporary mitigations and follow up.'
2026-03-12 21:07 - SRE (Samir Gupta): 'Disabled hedged prefetch for tenant and observed 70% reduction in blocked threads within 3 minutes. Proceeding to increase threadpool soft limits.'
2026-03-13 09:45 - Runtime Lead (Priya Patel): 'Patch ready to convert prefetch to async; PR opened at https://github.com/redwood/serving-runtime/pull/4271. Need QA on stress harness.'
2026-03-13 10:30 - Support (Miguel Torres): 'Customer notified. We'll schedule canary and keep BrightWrite updated.'

source.summary: Repeat-prompt latency spike (2-3x) after KV shard-mapping rollout; increased cold reads for prefix lookups
source.description: Customer reports 2-3x higher tail latencies for repeated prompt requests in long-running chat sessions starting after 2026-03-09 04:00 UTC. Symptoms match cold KV/prefix lookups: prefix cache hit-rate dropped sharply leading to synchronous remote reads and request timeouts (504) for a subset of requests. Impact: Several hundred requests/minute for the customer's production org experienced >1s extra latency and intermittent timeouts; affecting their chat product's UX. The customer is on an enterprise Dedicated plan and is running production traffic through our us-east cluster where a KV shard-mapping change was rolled out in the morning of 2026-03-09.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Use customer API key and send series of repeated prompts with identical prefix over sustained session (~50 requests).
2) Observe median and p95 latency for tokens after first warm request.
3) Compare kvcache prefix-hit metric on grafana: /d/kvcache/prefix-hit-rate?from=now-24h&org=NimbleDocs.
4) Toggle model pinning to redwood/gpt-instruct-3b and repeat.
5) Optionally reproduce at lower scale in staging by deploying kv-router PR-487 to a canary cluster and replaying production-like traffic trace.
source.logs_and_snippets:
[2026-03-09T04:12:31Z] serving-runtime: prefix_lookup miss key=org:NimbleDocs:conv:abc123 shard=7 -> remote_read latency=342ms
[2026-03-09T04:12:31Z] kv-router: mapping: hashed_key=0x7fa12 collision=true existing_shard=3 assigned_shard=7
[2026-03-09T04:12:31Z] proxy: upstream timeout after 1s for request id=xy-9b
Error patterns: repeated misses on identical prefix hashes interleaved with successful in-memory hits for other orgs.
source.investigation_notes:
- Timeline: kv-router shard-mapping change (PR#487) deployed to us-east at 2026-03-09 03:50 UTC; customer regressions started ~04:00 UTC.
- Metrics: prefix cache hit-rate for NimbleDocs dropped from ~92% to ~61% between 03:50-04:10; kvcache remote-read QPS spiked x6.
- Root-cause hypothesis: new shard mapping algorithm introduced non-deterministic assignment for some hashed prefix keys under rolling update -> duplicate entries temporarily placed in non-primary shard causing lookups to miss local in-memory replica and fall back to remote store. Observed non-zero 'collision' tag in kv-router logs.
- Reproduction: We reproduced increased cold-read behaviour locally by running PR-487 in canary with replayed trace; confirmed increases in remote-read latency and reduced local hit-rate.
- Related: INT-4021 notes an orthogonal bug where cache-write acknowledgements were not awaited during redeploy; this likely amplified the window where duplicates and missed writes coexisted.
- Grafana dashboards and flamegraphs attached in Confluence page.
- No evidence of model-serving regressions or GPU saturation; CPU and mem on serving nodes normal.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:15 - Elena Park (Support): Initial triage done. Confirmed customer-facing latency and collected logs. Opening ticket and escalating to SRE/Serving.

2026-03-10 10:02 - Mateo Alvarez (SRE): Observed 'collision=true' tag in kv-router for several keys. Rolling back PR-487 on canary reproduced baseline hit-rate. Investigating whether the mapping function is stable under rolling update.

2026-03-10 12:30 - CS (NimbleDocs contact, Priya Sharma): Customer reporting intermittent 504s for agents in production; they want ETA for mitigation. Provided temporary mitigation: toggle to older shard map via flag 'kv-router.mapping=stable-v4'.

2026-03-11 01:05 - Mateo Alvarez (SRE): Performed emergency rollback of kv-router mapping change in us-east at 2026-03-10 23:50 UTC. Early telemetry shows prefix hit-rate recovering to ~91% and remote-read QPS decreasing. Need to verify full roll-forward plan with fixes and tests.

2026-03-12 16:20 - Eng (Lina Chen): Confirmed race where write-ack path could be dropped during rolling updates (see INT-4021). Preparing a patch to make assignments idempotent and to add write-sync handshake before draining shards.
source.resolution: Mitigation applied: rolled back kv-router mapping change in us-east at 2026-03-10 23:50 UTC which restored prefix hit-rate and reduced timeouts for NimbleDocs. Hotfix deployed to add additional checks to mapping function to avoid non-deterministic assignments during rolling updates (serving-runtime PR#1293). Post-rollout plan: 1) run canary for 48h with synthetic repeat-prompt workload and additional golden-key unit tests; 2) add alert for sudden delta in prefix-hit-rate >15% within 30m; 3) follow-up runbook update and postmortem. Customer notified of rollback and mitigation; they accepted workaround and requested postmortem.
source.next_steps:
1) Complete code patch and unit tests for kv-router mapping idempotency (owner: Lina Chen, due 2026-03-15).
2) Expand canary replay tests to include heavy repeated-prefix workloads (owner: QE).
3) Create alerting runbook and add to Console: prefix-hit-rate regression alert (owner: SRE).
4) Draft postmortem and share with customer by 2026-03-17.

source.summary: Catalog sync drift caused wrong model routing, quality regression, and enterprise customer escalation
source.description:
Issue summary:
On 2026-03-10 at ~09:23 UTC we observed a large increase in customer-reported hallucinations and unrelated completions for Fresco Retail. Root cause traced to catalog-sync drift: the model-catalog service had an out-of-sync cache window and continued advertising an older (incompatible) variant for the 'product-description' route, causing requests to be routed to gptx-13b-v2-quant instead of the intended gptx-13b-v2 (non-quantized). This introduced repeated generation artifacts and a measurable quality regression for Fresco's production traffic.

Impact:
- Fresco Retail (enterprise) experienced elevated hallucination rate and semantic errors across catalog-enrichment requests for ~2 hours.
- API response quality SLO degraded (precision drop) and several customers opened escalations with support and account team.
- Billing exposure: some requests were served by a higher-cost quantized fallback path leading to a small invoice delta.

Environment:
- prod/us-east; Redwood hosted API; model-router + catalog-sync + serving-runtime in same region.
- Fresco's route: /v1/generate (route pinned to model alias 'fresco-prod:product-description').

Steps to reproduce (how support validated):
1) Replayed Fresco's sample request against the live router (timestamped trace id 20260310-0927-FR) -> observed model header set to gptx-13b-v2-quant.
2) Forced a catalog-sync refresh via admin endpoint -> subsequent requests routed to gptx-13b-v2 and quality returned to expected baseline.
3) Replayed the same prompt against both variants to compare outputs and token-level divergence.

Logs / Observations / Metrics:
- Catalog-sync service logs show an unsuccessful partial refresh at 09:17 UTC due to transient metadata store timeouts (backpressure). Cache TTL logic continued to serve stale entries until forced refresh at 11:30 UTC.
- Routing telemetry: model-mismatch rate spiked to 11% of fresco traffic between 09:20–11:20 UTC (baseline ~0.2%).
- Quality telemetry (prompt-eval harness): ROUGE-like and semantic similarity metrics dropped ~18% for sample prompt set.
- Billing meter reported ~3.6% of Fresco requests charged at quantized path unit pricing for the window.

Investigation notes (chronological):
- 09:25 UTC: Support received first Fresco alert; initial triage suspected quantized fallback due to GPU pool autoscale.
- 09:40 UTC: SRE ruled out GPU pool exhaustion; serving runtime healthy.
- 10:05 UTC: Instrumented model-router and catalog-sync. Found cache TTL decision had a race with incremental metadata updates (race introduced in recent change to TTL backoff).
- 10:45 UTC: Engineering reproduced drift locally by simulating metadata-store latency; confirmed wrong alias mapping persisted until manual refresh.
- 11:30 UTC: Manual catalog refresh + controlled rolling restart of catalog-sync workers restored correct routing.

Timeline (key events):
- 09:17 UTC: catalog-sync attempted refresh; metadata store returned 503 transiently.
- 09:20–11:20 UTC: increased model-mismatch traffic and customer complaints.
- 09:32 UTC: Fresco escalated to CSM and requested urgent remediation.
- 10:15 UTC: Support engaged SRE and Eng; incident declared P0.
- 11:30 UTC: manual refresh applied; routing corrected.
- 12:05 UTC: support reached out to Fresco with interim mitigation and credit offer.

Resolution:
- Immediate fix: forced catalog-sync refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers to clear stale entries; added a short-term guard to prevent TTL-from-expiring-during-refresh behavior.
- Mitigated customer impact by routing Fresco traffic to pinned replica and issuing proactive credit for affected window.

Root cause:
- A recent change to catalog-sync backoff/TTL logic allowed a stale mapping to persist when the metadata store returned transient errors. The stale mapping pointed to an incompatible quantized variant for Fresco's route.

Actions taken during incident:
- Support escalated and coordinated cross-functional war room (Support, SRE, Serving Eng, Account Management).
- Manual refresh and restart restored correct routing.
- Customer notified with initial incident summary and remedial credits.

Post-incident analysis / follow-ups (see followup tasks):
- Engineering to back out/patch TTL-backoff logic and add stronger invariants (2026-03-12).
- Add automated canary test in catalog-sync to detect alias-to-variant regressions (2026-03-17).
- Add alerting for 'model-mismatch rate' anomalies per-customer with higher sensitivity (2026-03-15).
- Billing team to validate invoice delta and communicate adjusted invoice to Fresco (2026-03-14).

Customer communication summary:
- 11:50 UTC: Support sent Fresco a private update describing the mitigation steps and expected timeline.
- 12:05 UTC: Support offered a billing credit for the affected window and committed to a follow-up technical review.
- 2026-03-12: Post-incident call scheduled between Fresco Engineering and Redwood Serving Eng to review changes to routing safety nets.

Notes on risk / recurrence:
- The change that introduced the race has limited blast radius but can affect any enterprise customer with alias pinning when metadata store latency occurs.
- The short-term guard reduces probability but permanent fix required to prevent recurrence.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:35 - Fresco Support (Jamie Holt): We are seeing unrelated product descriptions being injected; some SKUs have hallucinated attributes. This started ~09:20 UTC. Please advise.
2026-03-10 09:50 - Support (Ava Chen): Acknowledged and escalated to SRE. Opening investigation and capturing traces. We'll keep you updated every 30 mins.
2026-03-10 10:08 - SRE (Marcus Ortega): Checked GPU pools and serving runtime; no exhaustion. Elevated to Eng to look at model-router and catalog-sync. Initiating deeper telemetry pull.
2026-03-10 10:32 - Eng (Priya Nair): Found evidence of stale alias mapping in catalog-sync after a 503 from metadata-store at 09:17 UTC. Recommending forced refresh to restore correct mapping.

2026-03-10 11:35 - Support (Ava Chen): Forced catalog refresh and rolling restart completed; routing corrected. Initial quality metrics moving back to baseline. Notifying customer with mitigation and offered credit.
2026-03-10 12:10 - Fresco CSM (Daniel Ruiz): Fresco is grateful for the quick fix; they request a technical postmortem and assurance this won't recur during peak season.
2026-03-12 15:00 - Eng (Priya Nair): Patch to TTL-backoff logic landed in branch and under review (PR references in metadata). Canary tests scheduled for 2026-03-14.
2026-03-13 09:00 - Support (Ava Chen): Billing team confirmed invoice delta and applied credit; customer notified and accepted the credit. Incident marked resolved pending postmortem.
2026-03-13 14:30 - SRE (Marcus Ortega): Post-incident report prepared and shared on Confluence. Action items assigned to Eng and Observability teams.
source.resolution: Manual refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers; TTL-backoff logic patched; billing credit issued to customer; monitoring and canary tests scheduled.
followup_tasks: Patch TTL/backoff logic and release (owner: Priya Nair) - due 2026-03-14
Add canary alias-to-variant validation test (owner: Observability) - due 2026-03-17
Implement per-customer model-mismatch alerting (owner: SRE) - due 2026-03-15

title: Catalog sync drift caused wrong model routing, quality regression, and enterprise customer escalation
preview:
Issue summary:
On 2026-03-10 at ~09:23 UTC we observed a large increase in customer-reported hallucinations and unrelated completions for Fresco Retail. Root cause traced to catalog-sync drift: the model-catalog service had an out-of-sync cache window and continued advertising an older (incompatible) variant for the 'product-description' route, causing requests to be routed to gptx-13b-v2-quant instead of the intended gptx-13b-v2 (non-quantized). This introduced repeated generation artifacts and a measurable quality regression for Fresco's production traffic.

Impact:
- Fresco Retail (enterprise) experienced elevated hallucination rate and semantic errors across catalog-enrichment requests for ~2 hours.
- API response quality SLO degraded (precision drop) and several customers opened escalations with support and account team.
- Billing exposure: some requests were served by a higher-cost quantized fallback path leading to a small invoice delta.

Environment:
- prod/us-east; Redwood hosted API; model-router + catalog-sync + serving-runtime in same region.
- Fresco's route: /v1/generate (route pinned to model alias 'fresco-prod:product-description').

Steps to reproduce (how support validated):
1) Replayed Fresco's sample request against the live router (timestamped trace id 20260310-0927-FR) -> observed model header set to gptx-13b-v2-quant.
2) Forced a catalog-sync refresh via admin endpoint -> subsequent requests routed to gptx-13b-v2 and quality returned to expected baseline.
3) Replayed the same prompt against both variants to compare outputs and token-level divergence.

Logs / Observations / Metrics:
- Catalog-sync service logs show an unsuccessful partial refresh at 09:17 UTC due to transient metadata store timeouts (backpressure). Cache TTL logic continued to serve stale entries until forced refresh at 11:30 UTC.
- Routing telemetry: model-mismatch rate spiked to 11% of fresco traffic between 09:20–11:20 UTC (baseline ~0.2%).
- Quality telemetry (prompt-eval harness): ROUGE-like and semantic similarity metrics dropped ~18% for sample prompt set.
- Billing meter reported ~3.6% of Fresco requests charged at quantized path unit pricing for the window.

Investigation notes (chronological):
- 09:25 UTC: Support received first Fresco alert; initial triage suspected quantized fallback due to GPU pool autoscale.
- 09:40 UTC: SRE ruled out GPU pool exhaustion; serving runtime healthy.
- 10:05 UTC: Instrumented model-router and catalog-sync. Found cache TTL decision had a race with incremental metadata updates (race introduced in recent change to TTL backoff).
- 10:45 UTC: Engineering reproduced drift locally by simulating metadata-store latency; confirmed wrong alias mapping persisted until manual refresh.
- 11:30 UTC: Manual catalog refresh + controlled rolling restart of catalog-sync workers restored correct routing.

Timeline (key events):
- 09:17 UTC: catalog-sync attempted refresh; metadata store returned 503 transiently.
- 09:20–11:20 UTC: increased model-mismatch traffic and customer complaints.
- 09:32 UTC: Fresco escalated to CSM and requested urgent remediation.
- 10:15 UTC: Support engaged SRE and Eng; incident declared P0.
- 11:30 UTC: manual refresh applied; routing corrected.
- 12:05 UTC: support reached out to Fresco with interim mitigation and credit offer.

Resolution:
- Immediate fix: forced catalog-sync refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers to clear stale entries; added a short-term guard to prevent TTL-from-expiring-during-refresh behavior.
- Mitigated customer impact by routing Fresco traffic to pinned replica and issuing proactive credit for affected window.

Root cause:
- A recent change to catalog-sync backoff/TTL logic allowed a stale mapping to persist when the metadata store returned transient errors. The stale mapping pointed to an incompatible quantized variant for Fresco's route.

Actions taken during incident:
- Support escalated and coordinated cross-functional war room (Support, SRE, Serving Eng, Account Management).
- Manual refresh and restart restored correct routing.
- Customer notified with initial incident summary and remedial credits.

Post-incident analysis / follow-ups (see followup tasks):
- Engineering to back out/patch TTL-backoff logic and add stronger invariants (2026-03-12).
- Add automated canary test in catalog-sync to detect alias-to-variant regressions (2026-03-17).
- Add alerting for 'model-mismatch rate' anomalies per-customer with higher sensitivity (2026-03-15).
- Billing team to validate invoice delta and communicate adjusted invoice to Fresco (2026-03-14).

Customer communication summary:
- 11:50 UTC: Support sent Fresco a private update describing the mitigation steps and expected timeline.
- 12:05 UTC: Support offered a billing credit for the affected window and committed to a follow-up technical review.
- 2026-03-12: Post-incident call scheduled between Fresco Engineering and Redwood Serving Eng to review changes to routing safety nets.

Notes on risk / recurrence:
- The change that introduced the race has limited blast radius but can affect any enterprise customer with alias pinning when metadata store latency occurs.
- The short-term guard reduces probability but permanent fix required to prevent recurrence.

source.summary: Quota boundary and throttle-shepherding guidance for mixed bulk + realtime workload
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer BeaconMaps seeing recurring 429s during daily bulk index refresh and low-latency routing for map tile annotations. Impact: Intermittent 429s causing failed embedders and delayed annotations in production (~18% of index refresh jobs fail).

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-beacon-3), prod us-west. Heavy mixed workload: scheduled bulk embeddings (multipart uploads) + real-time annotation calls from mobile clients.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Trigger nightly bulk ingest for 80k documents via multipart upload with 16 parallel workers.
2) While ingest runs, send ~200 low-latency chat/annotation requests per second pinned to the same model variant.
3) Observe rate-limit headers and return codes.

Observed behavior: Throttles (429) occur predominantly on multipart upload endpoints and the streaming annotation calls report Retry-After of 5-7s. Customer reports that reducing parallelism to 4 workers removes 429s, but throughput drop is unacceptable.

Expected behavior: Segregated quota accounting or burst pools should allow short-lived low-latency calls to continue while bulk ingestion backfills without causing tenant-wide 429s.

Attached logs: API responses with headers, sample request traces, and telemetry timestamps provided in attachments.

Customer request: Guidance on configuring dedicated pool settings, recommended rate-window splits, and whether we can enable an isolated burst pool for long-lived multipart uploads without requiring migration to private deployment.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:18 Support - Ava Chen: Acknowledged receipt. Escalating to infra to validate dedicated pool rate-window config. SLA first response due 2026-03-10.

2026-03-10 11:02 Infra - Rafael Ortiz: Observed tenant token burn during bulk ingest; windows show sustained consumption exceeding background-burst threshold. Need more telemetry: token-window vs route-level counts. Asking customer for sample request ids.
2026-03-10 13:45 Customer - BeaconMaps (S. Patel): Provided sample request ids and timeline. Reconfirmed parallelism reduction avoids 429s but is not viable long-term.
2026-03-11 08:32 Support - Ava Chen: Provided interim workaround: throttle bulk upload workers to 8 and enable route-level isolation for /v1/embeddings/upload. Sent configuration steps.

2026-03-12 10:15 Eng - Rafael Ortiz: Ran simulation on staging dedicated-beacon-3. Confirmed that enabling 'split_burst_pools' with a dedicated background lane for multipart uploads prevented tenant-wide 429s in test. Low-latency lane maintained latency SLOs.

2026-03-13 09:40 Customer - BeaconMaps (S. Patel): Applying recommended config now; asked about billing/quota implications and whether burst credits are shared. Requesting next steps for stabilizing at production scale.
investigation_notes: Checked dedicated pool metrics: token refill curves show refill interval 60s with burst_credit of 5000 at tenant level. Multipart uploads were consuming 70% of burst credits in first 3 minutes.
Route-level accounting currently not enabled for /v1/embeddings/upload; requests are counted against tenant bucket. This explains tenant-wide throttles affecting low-latency routes.

Staging replication: create two route buckets (interactive vs bulk), apply split_burst_pools flag. Simulation with realistic fanout preserved interactive lane and allowed bulk lane to be rate-limited without tenant 429s.

Hypothesis: short-term solution is route isolation + reduced parallelism for bulk workers. Long-term: enable dedicated burst pools per route in dedicated configuration or request Reserved burst capacity for heavy bulk customers.
source.resolution:
Workaround implemented: Provided step-by-step console and API commands to create a route-level isolation for /v1/embeddings/upload and advised throttling bulk workers to 8. Engineering validated split_burst_pools flag in staging and recommended enabling for this dedicated tenant. Customer to confirm config in prod; follow-up ticket for quota purchase if persistent.

Follow-ups: (1) If customer opts for Reserved burst capacity, open sales ticket to discuss committed burst bundles. (2) Engineering task to add automated suggestion in console when mixed workloads detected: create SUP task INT-2026 to track.
status_updates: 2026-03-10: In Progress - infra investigating token accounting
2026-03-11: Waiting on Customer - sent workaround steps
2026-03-12: In Progress - engineering validated split_burst_pools in staging
2026-03-13: Waiting on Customer - customer applying config in prod
