source.summary: Dedicated capacity pulse: sudden p50/p99 latency increases correlated with misaligned sidecar heartbeats
source.description: Issue summary: Customer Lumina Analytics reported a recurring, short-lived pulse in p50 and p99 latency across their dedicated pool starting 2026-03-09 14:00 UTC. Impact: user-facing API calls (generation and streaming) saw p50 increase from ~120ms to ~320ms and p99 spikes to 1.2s for 10-30s windows, causing degraded interactive experience. Pattern: pulses occur roughly every 5-12 minutes and last ~10-30s; throughput (tokens/s) drops by 20-40% during pulses. Initial hypothesis: interference from background sidecar telemetry/metrics emitters causing transient scheduler/attach delays and autoscaler sensing noise.
source.impact: Intermittent latency spikes (p50/p99) and temporary throughput reduction for dedicated customer routes; affects production SLAs for Lumina (dedicated). Not a full outage but repeated UX regressions during business hours.
source.environment_details: Dedicated pool: redwood-dedicated-usw2-42; GPU type: A100-80GB mix (heterogeneous), autoscaler config: predictive window=60s, smoothing=0.15, max_replicas=8, min_replicas=2. Customer routes pinned to model gpt-3.5-xl. Telemetry sidecars deployed on every host emitting metrics at default 5s interval.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Send steady stream of generation requests to Lumina's dedicated route (100 concurrent short streams, 8-16 token outputs).
2) Observe baseline latency and throughput for 5 minutes (establish baseline).
3) Wait for the next 10-12 minute window — monitor p50/p99 and tokens/s — pulses observed reproducibly in production.
4) Correlate pulses with sidecar metrics emitter bursts from host-level telemetry (see logs).
source.logs:
Selected excerpt from host metrics log (times UTC):
2026-03-09T14:04:20Z sidecar-metrics[host-42]: emit bundle size=780 metrics (duration 120ms)
2026-03-09T14:04:21Z allocator[host-42]: attach-request delayed 180ms due to busy nvlink queue
2026-03-09T14:04:21Z autoscaler: sample spike detected (cpu 65% -> 92%) smoothing applied
2026-03-09T14:04:21Z serving-runtime: batch-queue length increased 3x; p99=1.02s
----
Correlation note: sidecar emit bursts precede attach delays by ~200-400ms in multiple samples.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:12 - Support (Ava Chen): Ticket opened after customer alert. Attached flamegraphs and request traces. Asked SRE to triage potential host noise.

2026-03-10 10:03 - SRE (Marcus Delgado): Initial observation: short I/O bursts from metrics sidecar causing CPU/PCIe contention on subset of hosts. Spoke to infra to pull sidecar metrics cadence and check emitter rate-limiter.
2026-03-10 14:40 - Customer (Lumina - Priya Rao): Confirmed user reports line up with the windows in our UX tests; provided sample request IDs. We see degraded interactivity during pulses.

2026-03-11 02:15 - Eng (Rui Patel): Collected allocator and nvlink telemetry; noticed small but consistent queueing. Proposed mitigation: lower sidecar emit bursts (increase jitter and backoff) and add autoscaler smoothing patch.

2026-03-13 08:30 - Support (Ava Chen): Temporary mitigation rolled to subset of hosts: sidecar emit cadence changed from 5s fixed to randomized 5s-15s with token bucket rate limit. Monitoring for recurrence.
investigation_notes: Hosts impacted were a coherent cohort (host-42, host-43, host-47) running an older metrics collector image with synchronous flush on rotate.
Telemetry emitter produced short CPU/PCIe bursts when flushing large metric payloads; on heterogeneous A100 cluster this manifested as nvlink completion delays and blocked batch attach.
Autoscaler reacted to sample noise; predictive autoscaler window amplified short spikes into small, unnecessary scale adjustments increasing attach churn.
No evidence of model-level regression or kernel errors; root cause appears infrastructural (sidecar emission behavior + autoscaler sensitivity).
source.mitigation: Short-term: throttled and randomized sidecar metrics emit on affected hosts; temporarily increased autoscaler smoothing factor from 0.15 to 0.30 for the pool. Expected effect: reduce sensing noise and attach churn.
source.resolution: Workaround applied to affected cohort; observed reduction in pulse frequency and amplitude over 24h monitoring (p50 returned ~130ms, p99 rarely above 400ms). Engineering patch to update metrics collector to async flush + jitter scheduled and tracked in PR. Follow-ups: roll patch to fleet, improve autoscaler heuristics to ignore sub-2s pulses, add telemetry counters to detect sidecar burst patterns automatically.
next_steps: 1) Merge and rollout async sidecar emitter patch (PR github.com/redwood/infra/pull/4592) — assigned Eng team, ETA 2026-03-18.
2) Autoscaler: add pulse-filtering logic in predictive sampler to ignore spikes <2s and add hysteresis — tracked in serving PR 2781.
3) Post-rollout monitoring: 72h elevated telemetry window for Lumina routes and dedicated pool; collect sample traces for any reappearance.
4) Customer follow-up: schedule a 30-minute postmortem review with Lumina CS on 2026-03-20.

source.summary: Prorated invoice line for async toolcall retry traffic not matching postback records
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports an unexpected prorated invoice line that appears to bill for ~18,000 toolcall invocations during a two-hour window on 2026-03-06. The customer asserts those invocations were retry traffic from a previous deploy and should have been tagged as test/heartbeat and therefore not billed.

Impact:
- Customer sees a +$1,250 prorated line on their 2026-03-06 provisional invoice.
- Customer is an enterprise Private deployment customer with reserved hours; unexpected charge impacts finance reconciliation and internal chargeback.

Environment:
- Peregrine Health private deployment (VPC) with hosted fallback enabled.
- Region: us-west-2.
- Affected model: redwood-gpt-large-v1 (toolcall-enabled variant).

Observed behavior:
- Invoice shows a prorated hosted-toolcall line; billing key points to hosted rate ID for toolcall invocations.
- Postback replays and reconciler logs show 0 accepted postbacks for production API key for the same timestamps; most events were marked as "retry-dropped" by the async-queue.

Expected behavior:
- Retry-dropped or test-tagged invocations should not be emitted as hosted postbacks and should not result in billed hosted-toolcall lines.

Steps to reproduce / investigation checklist performed by customer before filing:
1) Confirmed provisional invoice line present 2026-03-06 09:00-11:00 PDT.
2) Queried org postback exports for production key: zero successful postbacks in window.
3) Verified async-queue logs in their VPC showed repeated retry attempts with header X-Test-Traffic: true for a subset of connections.
4) Verified no canary alias was in use during time window.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- postback_export_2026-03-06.csv (attached)
- async-queue-retry-trace.txt (attached)

Requested outcome:
- Explain why invoice line was generated when reconciler shows no successful postbacks.
- If metering misattributed, request provisional credit and a root-cause explanation.

Notes for support: customer is escalated to account team; treat as high priority for billing reconciliation and temporary credit consideration.
comments: 2026-03-08 - Ava Chen (Reporter): Opening ticket on behalf of Peregrine Health. Customer provided postback export and async logs. They request provisional credit while investigation occurs.

2026-03-09 - Marcus Patel (Assignee): Acknowledged. Raised INT-1102 for postback-reconciler deeper trace and requested transaction-level reconciler logs from engineering. ETA for first pass: 2026-03-10 EOD.

2026-03-10 - Riley Gomez (Billing Eng): Initial reconciler query shows billed events originate from a 'fallback-pipeline' path that lacked the X-Test-Traffic header normalization step during a short config mismatch between hosted and private fallback connectors. Adding artifacts: reconciler-query-id=rb-20260310-77. Hypothesis: header lost during fallback caused retries to be classified as prod and emitted as postbacks.

2026-03-11 - Marcus Patel: Requested a deeper audit of edge-proxy release 2026-03-06T08:45 which touched header forwarding logic. Added related PR to investigation: https://github.com/redwood-inference/edge-proxy/pull/412.

2026-03-12 - Finance Liaison (Elena Ruiz): Customer asked for provisional credit while we confirm RCA. Recommended provisional credit of $1,250 until reconciler replay completed. Paused autopay for this invoice pending resolution.

2026-03-13 - Marcus Patel: Reconciler replay completed. Confirmed 18,214 emitted postbacks matched billed line items; root cause: edge-proxy release 2026-03-06 inadvertently dropped test header when routing to hosted-fallback connector for a 9-minute window. Patch merged to restore header normalization. Next steps: issue credit, monitor for recurrence, postmortem due.

2026-03-13 - Support (Marcus Patel): Provisional credit of $1,250 applied to customer's provisional invoice. Customer notified and autopay remains paused until final invoice reconciliation. Postmortem scheduled with Edge/Private teams.
investigation_notes: Reconciler trace (rb-20260310-77): shows source pipeline 'fallback-pipeline' emitted 18,214 events between 2026-03-06T16:02:00Z and 2026-03-06T16:11:00Z.
Edge-proxy release 2026-03-06T08:45 removed a header normalization step in commit 9a7f3b. That commit regressed behavior for private->hosted fallback connectors.
Async-queue logs from customer's VPC show retries with X-Test-Traffic: true but the header was missing when reaching hosted-fallback due to proxy regression.
Billing rate used: hosted-toolcall-rate (rate_id: r-hosted-toolcall-2025-v2); reconciler matched to billing ledger entries by fingerprint idempotency keys.

No evidence of duplicate double-billing; events were emitted once as hosted postbacks and billed. Corrective path: patch edge-proxy and reclassify emitted events as test in reconciler where possible; otherwise issue credit.
source.resolution: Provisional credit of $1,250 applied 2026-03-13. Root cause: edge-proxy header normalization regression for private->hosted fallback during release 2026-03-06; patch merged and deployed to all affected proxies. Final invoice will be adjusted if reconciler replay results in reclassification; postmortem to be published 2026-03-18.

Issue summary:
Customer reports an unexpected prorated invoice line that appears to bill for ~18,000 toolcall invocations during a two-hour window on 2026-03-06. The customer asserts those invocations were retry traffic from a previous deploy and should have been tagged as test/heartbeat and therefore not billed.

Impact:
- Customer sees a +$1,250 prorated line on their 2026-03-06 provisional invoice.
- Customer is an enterprise Private deployment customer with reserved hours; unexpected charge impacts finance reconciliation and internal chargeback.

Environment:
- Peregrine Health private deployment (VPC) with hosted fallback enabled.
- Region: us-west-2.
- Affected model: redwood-gpt-large-v1 (toolcall-enabled variant).

Observed behavior:
- Invoice shows a prorated hosted-toolcall line; billing key points to hosted rate ID for toolcall invocations.
- Postback replays and reconciler logs show 0 accepted postbacks for production API key for the same timestamps; most events were marked as "retry-dropped" by the async-queue.

Expected behavior:
- Retry-dropped or test-tagged invocations should not be emitted as hosted postbacks and should not result in billed hosted-toolcall lines.

Steps to reproduce / investigation checklist performed by customer before filing:
1) Confirmed provisional invoice line present 2026-03-06 09:00-11:00 PDT.
2) Queried org postback exports for production key: zero successful postbacks in window.
3) Verified async-queue logs in their VPC showed repeated retry attempts with header X-Test-Traffic: true for a subset of connections.
4) Verified no canary alias was in use during time window.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- postback_export_2026-03-06.csv (attached)
- async-queue-retry-trace.txt (attached)

Requested outcome:
- Explain why invoice line was generated when reconciler shows no successful postbacks.
- If metering misattributed, request provisional credit and a root-cause explanation.

Notes for support: customer is escalated to account team; treat as high priority for billing reconciliation and temporary credit consideration.

source.summary: Time-bounded evidence playback with per-entity obfuscation and canonical checksums
source.description: Customer requests a time-bounded "playback" export for a legal discovery window (2026-02-10T00:00:00Z to 2026-02-15T23:59:59Z) that meets the following constraints: 1) Remove or obfuscate any customer-provided PII at entity level (names, SSNs, account numbers) while preserving conversation structure and action timestamps; 2) Provide a canonical, tamper-evident checksum manifest (SHA256 per payload + HMAC envelope signed with KMS) that proves ordering and integrity for chain-of-custody; 3) Include RFC3161 timestamp for the manifest; 4) Allow partial playback of a single tenant's events filtered by session_id and model_version; 5) Export must be available as a downloadable bundle and as a short-lived S3 presigned URL for third-party counsel. Customer explicitly requests that model-generated suggestions that echo PII are redacted but system-level metadata (route, model_version, request_id, timestamps) remains intact for forensic traceability.
source.impact: FinSentry legal team needs an evidentiary bundle for a subpoena. They cannot accept full raw logs containing sensitive client identifiers. Lack of a verified manifest and per-entity redaction blocks legal compliance and delays the matter. High urgency; requested turnaround within 5 business days.
source.environment_details: Production tenant: tenant_id=fs-prod-09. Observed ingestion across multiple hosts in us-east-1 during window. Standard audit retention configured (90 days) with archival to s3://redwood-legal-archives/fs-prod-09/.
source.steps_to_reproduce_request: 1) Customer uploaded a legal evidence request via Console -> Support -> Evidence Export. 2) Attached subpoena and redaction rules CSV with entity types and regexes. 3) Selected window and requested 'canonical manifest + RFC3161' option. 4) Support validated request and escalated to Eng/Security for safe export.
source.expected_behavior: Generate an export that: - excludes or pseudonymizes PII per customer's redaction spec, - retains event ordering and request-level metadata, - includes SHA256 checksums per artifact plus a KMS-signed manifest, - provides RFC3161 timestamp token attached to the manifest, and - is delivered via encrypted S3 bundle with audit trail entries recorded in tenant audit stream.
source.actual_behavior: Initial export produced by the quick-export tool contained full text payloads (including unredacted PII) and only MD5 checksums with no RFC3161 timestamp. The customer rejected the bundle and requested re-run with stronger guarantees and an auditable manifest. The quick-export path does not currently support entity-level redaction based on customer-specified regexes.
logs: 2026-02-11T03:22:14Z INFO ingest:tenant=fs-prod-09 request_id=req_1a2b3c route=/v1/chat model=redwood-1.1-quant tokens=187
2026-02-11T03:22:14Z DEBUG payload:req_1a2b3c user_text="Transfer $54,900 to account 987-65-4321 for John Doe"
2026-02-11T03:24:01Z INFO export:bundle_id=exp_20260211_fs-prod-09 status=generated checksums=md5:1a79a4d60de6718e8e5b326e338ae533
source.investigation_notes: - Support verified customer's redaction CSV and regex rules; the quick-export path ignores custom regexes. - Export service v2 supports 'field-mappings' but currently only for preconfigured tenant profiles. - KMS-signed manifests are supported by manifest-service but RFC3161 stamping requires a separate timestamping service which is off-path for immediate ad-hoc exports. - A safe process must be used to redact model-generated PII: recommend entity recognition + deterministic pseudonymization with reversible mapping kept in encrypted vault (if customer requests reversible mapping, requires legal authorization).
source.mitigation_and_workaround: 1) Manual pipeline: create a copy of the requested event slice, run redactor job (uses NER + customer's regexes) in isolated processing environment, generate SHA256 checksums, call manifest-service to produce KMS-signed envelope, and submit RFC3161 request to timestamping service. 2) Deliver bundle to customer via an expiring presigned S3 URL (7-day) and notify via secure email. 3) Log all operator actions to audit trail and provide operator-signed statement for chain-of-custody.
source.work_remaining: - Build a one-off job to run redaction rules over the specified window (ENG task). - Wire the job output into manifest-service to produce SHA256 + KMS-signed envelope (ENG/Security). - Trigger RFC3161 timestamping and attach token (Security/Infra). - Prepare delivery package and legal attestation (Support + Legal).
source.sla_commitment: Customer requested 5 business day turnaround; target to complete by 2026-03-17 per internal SLA for enterprise legal holds.
comments: 2026-03-12 10:14 - Lina Morales: Initial ticket received with redaction CSV attached. Customer indicates counsel will need reversible pseudonymization if court order requires re-identification. Escalated to Eng/Security.

2026-03-12 13:02 - Ethan Park: Assigned. Preliminary review: quick-export not suitable. Will spin one-off pipeline and coordinate KMS signing. Need access to customer's redaction CSV and written authorization for reversible mapping if requested.

2026-03-12 16:45 - Jun Wei (Security): Reviewed requirement for RFC3161. Timestamping will be performed by our external TSA provider; we need to schedule and confirm the time window. Also recommend HSM-backed signing for manifest.

2026-03-13 09:07 - Legal (Maria Castillo): Confirmed receipt of support request and provided written authorization template; customer to sign for reversible mapping. Will prepare attestation language for manifest.

2026-03-13 12:22 - Ethan Park: One-off redactor job started on isolated cluster. Estimated 24h runtime for the window size. Will post progress and sample artifacts for customer review (redacted snippet only).
source.resolution: In progress. One-off redaction + manifest pipeline initiated. Delivery pending RFC3161 timestamp and Legal attestation. Final bundle expected by SLA date. Customer will receive redacted sample for approval before full delivery.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-ops: parameterize quick-export to accept custom redaction regexes (backlog ticket)
Security: integrate RFC3161 stamping into manifest-service (project request)
Support: confirm whether customer requires reversible pseudonymization and keep signed authorization on file
source.notes_for_postmortem: Recommend instrumenting quick-export to fail-fast when custom redaction rules are supplied but not supported; track time spent on one-off exports to evaluate SLA cost. Consider building a 'legal export' workflow in Console to automate signed-manifest + timestamping.

source.summary: Persistent 502/503 plateau from proxy TLS-handshake queueing and slow-starting upstream runtimes
source.description:
Issue summary:
ScribeAI reported a persistent elevated 502/503 error rate starting 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC across multiple endpoints. Errors present as short-lived waves that plateau rather than sharp spikes — API gateway returning 502/503 while upstream runtimes report long TLS handshake queues and delayed worker start.

Impact:
- Customer-facing requests to ScribeAI (dedicated) experienced 10-20% error rate for ~3 hours during peak traffic windows.
- Streaming sessions intermittently failed to establish with 502s.
- Latency for successful requests increased by ~2x during plateau periods.

Environment:
- prod/us-east (dedicated pool for ScribeAI)
- Models: gptx-13b-v2 pinned variant
- Redwood stack: api-gateway -> ephemeral-proxy group -> serving-runtime (dedicated GPU pool)

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. Generate steady small-payload traffic simulating ScribeAI client (60 rps).
2. Observe api-gateway logs: elevated upstream 502/503 responses, short-lived connection drops.
3. Correlate with ephemeral-proxy metrics: handshake_queue_depth spikes followed by slow worker spin-ups.

Observed behavior:
- TLS handshake queue depth increases to thousands of pending handshakes in the proxy layer.
- Ephemeral proxy threads report limited fd availability and per-thread accept backlog growth.
- Serving-runtime logs show a slow-start pattern for newly provisioned runtimes (delayed model init ~6-9s) leading to temporary capacity shortage and preemption events.
- api-gateway surfaces 502/503s (upstream reset / no healthy upstream) rather than 504s.

Expected behavior:
- Handshake queue remains low under normal concurrency; rapid accept/dispatch to runtime workers.
- Upstreams should be healthy or gracefully drain with fallback routing; no sustained 502/503 plateau.

Logs / metrics / traces (high level):
- api-gateway: errors: 502/503 rate jump at 02:08 UTC, sustained plateau until 05:30 UTC.
- ephemeral-proxy: handshake_queue_depth: 0 -> 3.2k at plateau; accept_queue_write_latency increased.
- runtime: worker_init_latency 6000-9000ms for new runtimes; repeated preemptions observed in autoscaler logs.
- netstat / kernel: no obvious ephemeral port exhaustion; socket TIME_WAIT in normal range.
- TCP/TLS traces indicate incomplete TLS handshake stages (server hello sent but no application data) corresponding with proxy accept backlog.

Immediate mitigation applied (by SRE):
- Temporarily raised ephemeral-proxy accept backlog and increased proxy thread pool by 25% to relieve handshake queue.
- Applied conservative rate-shed on low-priority tenants to prioritize dedicated customers.
- Enabled sticky routing to healthy warmed runtimes for ScribeAI to avoid cold-starts where possible.

Notes on customer communication: 
- Customer notified at 02:30 UTC that we were investigating TLS-handshake/backlog causes and applied temporary mitigations.
- Follow-up plan shared: deeper investigation into proxy accept handling, autoscaler slow-start tuning, and kernel tuning for accept queueing.
comments: 2026-03-12 02:27 UTC - Support (Ava Chen): Opened ticket after ScribeAI reported elevated 502/503; collected initial logs and traffic windows.

2026-03-12 02:45 UTC - SRE (Miguel Ortega): Observed handshake_queue_depth spike on ephemeral-proxy. Applying temporary increase to proxy thread pool; requesting autoscaler logs for runtime spin-up delays.
2026-03-12 03:12 UTC - Customer (ScribeAI, CS rep): Confirmed error rate decreased slightly after mitigation but plateau persists during bursts. Asked for ETA on permanent fix.
2026-03-12 03:40 UTC - Infra (Connor Blake): Kernel ACCEPT backlog tuning applied on a subset of nodes; monitoring for regressions.

2026-03-12 04:50 UTC - Eng (Samir Gupta): Identified correlation between recent autoscaler change (PR #981) and increased worker_init_latency on cold starts. Kicking off a rollback of the aggressive cold-start optimization.
2026-03-13 09:10 UTC - Support (Ava Chen): Notified customer of planned rollback and next steps; scheduled post-incident follow-up meeting.
2026-03-13 15:00 UTC - Engineering (Samir Gupta): Rollback completed on a canary subset. Metrics show handshake_queue reduced and 502/503 plateau abating; rolling to remaining fleet.
2026-03-13 18:22 UTC - SRE (Miguel Ortega): Confirmed sustained recovery; recommending a postmortem to cover root causes and long-term mitigations.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis A: Proxy thread pool + accept backlog under-provisioned for bursty handshake-heavy clients when runtimes are cold.
Hypothesis B: Recent autoscaler tweak that batches runtime launches to reduce GPU churn inadvertently increased worker_init_latency, creating a supply lag that amplified proxy queueing.
Evidence: Correlation between PR #981 deployment times and the start of error plateau; worker_init_latency from runtime metrics increased after the change; canary rollback reduced latencies.
Kernel-level checks: no ephemeral port exhaustion; accept backlog metrics were the most prominent signal. Consider TCP backlog tuning (somaxconn/accept4 flags) as part of remediation.
Customer-specific traffic shape: ScribeAI has many short-lived streaming sessions (frequent TLS handshakes) which amplify the effect of cold slow-starts in dedicated pool.
Recommended data captures: full pcap/TCP dumps for a sample window, flamegraphs for proxy thread pools during plateau, autoscaler event timeline and runtime init tracing.
source.resolution:
Root cause: interaction between an autoscaler optimization (PR #981) that delayed individual runtime readiness and an under-provisioned proxy accept backpressure handling. The delayed runtime start created a supply shortage; the proxy queued TLS handshakes which manifested as a persistent 502/503 plateau for the dedicated tenant.

Fixes applied:
- Rolled back the autoscaler change from PR #981 to restore prior runtime start behavior.
- Increased proxy thread pool and accept backlog defaults; added a dynamic scaling rule for proxy threads based on handshake_queue_depth.
- Short-term kernel tune applied on hot nodes (somaxconn increased) and will be codified in infra bootstrap.
- Enabled prioritized sticky routing for warmed runtimes for dedicated customers to reduce cold handshake exposure.

Follow-ups / long-term actions:
- Engineering to implement a graceful handoff/fallback so api-gateway will inline-fallback to warmed variants when handshake_queue crosses threshold. (ENG-1221)
- Autoscaler team to add a readiness probe and split warm vs cold init paths to reduce worker_init_latency. (INT-9042)
- SRE to codify kernel tuning in instance image (PR pending).

Customer impact: mitigations reduced error rate back to baseline within 6 hours; ScribeAI experienced degraded availability for the duration but no data loss. Ticket will remain open until long-term actions are complete and verified.

source.summary: Cascading API-key handovers causing tenant concurrency exhaustion and intermittent 429 bursts
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a burst of 429 responses across their tenant after an automated API key rotation during a high-throughput streaming upload window. The pattern is reproducible when their client rotates keys and simultaneously spawns short-lived prefetch workers that fan out requests to multiple routes.

Impact:
- LexaHealth observed ~30% request failures (429) for 7–12 minutes after an automated key rotation during a nightly data push.
- Business impact: ingestion pipeline stalled; downstream throughput SLA missed.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Configure two API keys (K-A primary, K-B rotated-in) with equal privileges under same tenant.
2) Client spawns 40 short-lived prefetch workers that request streaming completions and embeddings concurrently (burst over ~10s).
3) Client triggers automated rotation: start sending requests with both K-A and K-B for an overlap period of ~30s.
4) Observe 429s returned to many workers within 1–2 minutes of rotation, continuing for several minutes.

Observed behavior:
- 429 responses show rate-limiter reason in headers: X-RateLimit-Reason: concurrency_window_exhausted
- Token-bucket headers show remaining=0 for tenant-level bucket while per-key buckets occasionally show small positive values.
- Logs include many short-lived connection opens/terminates tied to rotated key handoff.

Expected behavior:
- Smooth handover with exponential backoff from clients should avoid tenant-level token exhaustion; short overlap should not trigger wide 429 storms.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Upload link to request traces (redacted): s3://leixa-logs/rotation-2026-03-09/traces.tar.gz
- Curl examples and client pseudocode for rotation logic included in attachments.

Immediate ask:
- Guidance on mitigation steps to stop ongoing 429 bursts and recommended configuration changes for dedicated customers performing frequent key rotations.
investigation_notes: 03/10 09:40 — Support: Confirmed tenant-wide 429s correlate with rotation window in provided traces.
03/10 11:05 — SRE: Rate-limiter metrics show tenant token-bucket depletion coincident with a spike in short-lived connections; per-key buckets were distributed but tenant global window exhausted.

03/11 14:20 — Eng hypothesis: Client-side overlap + prefetch worker fan-out created a concurrency-pattern that bypasses smooth ramp controls and bursts queued requests into serving runtime, draining tenant pool faster than refill rate.

03/11 16:00 — Observed that streaming sessions that start close to rotation emit many small, parallel prefetch requests (metadata, embeddings) which count toward concurrency window immediately; aborts create extra churn.

03/12 09:00 — Suggested mitigations drafted: reduce overlap during rotation, add client-side jitter/backoff, enable per-key gradual ramp (request throttling), and consider increasing tenant concurrency limits temporarily for the rotation window.
03/12 10:30 — Engineering flagged related internal task INT-4882 to evaluate adding a graceful-key-swap header and server-side short buffer to smooth handovers for dedicated customers.
customer_logs: Trace summary: tenant_bucket_remaining=0 tenant_refill_rate=500 tokens/minute active_connections_peak=120
comments: 2026-03-10 09:05 — LexaHealth (N. Patel): We're seeing a lot of 429s after our scheduled rotation. Rotation is automated via our CI; overlap window is ~30s. We need an emergency workaround.
2026-03-10 09:20 — Ava Chen (Support): Acknowledged. Can you attach request traces and the rotation script? Also please confirm whether you use short-lived worker processes for prefetching.
2026-03-10 11:20 — LexaHealth (N. Patel): Traces uploaded to provided S3 path. Yes, we spawn ~40 prefetch workers for each batch job and ramp down after completion.

2026-03-11 14:30 — Marcus Lee (SRE): Preliminary review shows tenant bucket drained during rotation spike. Workarounds: (1) pause rotation during heavy ingest, (2) add exponential backoff and jitter on client, (3) request temporary quota bump for rotation window. Can you test pausing rotation in next run?
2026-03-11 15:10 — LexaHealth (N. Patel): We can schedule a window to pause rotation — will test tonight and report results.
2026-03-12 10:35 — Marcus Lee (SRE): Engineering is evaluating a server-side smoothing feature (INT-4882). We'll update once we have a plan; in the meantime please test the client mitigations above.
source.resolution: Pending — customer to validate client-side mitigation (pause rotation / add backoff). Engineering investigating server-side smoothing (INT-4882).
next_steps: Customer to run one job with rotation paused and confirm whether 429s persist (due date: 2026-03-12).
If customer still observes 429s, collect full pcap and extended traces and upload to support bucket.
Engineering to prototype 'graceful-key-swap' header and short server-side buffering to smooth tenant token consumption (linked INT-4882).
SRE to evaluate temporary tenant concurrency increase for LexaHealth during scheduled rotations and provide change request if needed.

- Curl examples and client pseudocode for rotation logic included in attachments.

Immediate ask:
- Guidance on mitigation steps to stop ongoing 429 bursts and recommended configuration changes for dedicated customers performing frequent key rotations.

source.summary: Reserved pool throughput degradation caused by GPU power-capping + fragmented micro-batching
source.description: Issue summary: Customer BeaconAI observed sustained p95/p99 latency regressions and intermittent 504 timeouts during peak windows on their dedicated reserved pool. Impact: Customer reports request timeouts and model responses taking 2x-4x expected time; production product is degrading for end users. Timeline: problem first reported 2026-03-10 08:10 UTC, support triaged and opened this ticket. Initial hypothesis included noisy-tenant and autoscaler undershoot. This ticket captures investigation showing a combination of node-level GPU power capping (thermal/clock throttling) combined with workload micro-batching fragmentation and a conservative autoscaler min_replicas setting.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Send steady bursty traffic of short-sequence inference requests (avg tokens=18) across 40 concurrent clients to BeaconAI's reserved pool endpoint.
2) Observe throughput and p95/p99 latency via customer-side metrics and Redwood Console per-route dashboard.
3) Correlate with node-level telemetry (nvidia-smi, node exporter) and autoscaler scaling events during bursts.
4) Reproduce higher latency when a single large streaming job (long-seq, KV-cache heavy) is co-located on the same node as many small short-seq jobs.
Expected: p95 < 400ms for these short sequences; Observed: p95 spikes to 900ms and p99 > 1.8s with occasional 504s.
logs: 2026-03-10T08:12:34Z node-gpu-07 nvidia-smi: GPU clock throttled from 1665MHz to 810MHz (thermal)
2026-03-10T08:13:05Z serving-runtime: detected KV-cache churn on model redwood-13b-quant (cache misses increased 8x)
2026-03-10T08:14:10Z autoscaler: evaluate: min_replicas=1, desired_replicas=1, scale_up_threshold=0.85, observed_gpu_util=0.96
2026-03-10T08:14:45Z scheduler: colocated_job_hint: job=large-stream-req-57 flagged; colocated_pods=5 on node-gpu-07
Customer client logs: sequence timeouts at 08:15:03Z with 504 from gateway
investigation_notes: Node telemetry shows repeated GPU clock downshifts on node-gpu-07 and node-gpu-09 during peak bursts; nvidia-smi and DCGM telemetry indicate clocks capped due to sustained high board/VRAM temps. Not purely driver OOMs.
Serving runtime traces indicate many tiny batches created (batch size=1-3) for short-seq traffic; batching efficiency collapsed leading to high per-token overhead.
Large streaming job on the same node caused persistent KV-cache priming and memory working-set spikes, producing background DRAM pressure and increased kernel pagecache activity.

Autoscaler configuration for BeaconAI reserved pool had min_replicas set to 1 in the policy (intended as cost-saving); during bursts scheduler could not spread short sequences across more GPUs because scaling decision window is 90s and desired_replicas stayed at 1 until sustained util > threshold.
SRE reproduced by forcing a synthetic long-seq stream colocated with small-seq clients and saw identical p95 spikes; isolating streaming job to separate nodes returned p95 to baseline.
Confirmed customer did not change client-side retry/backoff behavior; traffic pattern changed (new marketing campaign) resulting in more concurrent short requests than usual.
No kernel OOM or GPU ECC errors found; thermal events and power-limiter logs are primary hardware signal.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:02 - Support (Ava Chen): Customer reports p99 > 1.8s and intermittent 504s during morning peak. Asked for request ids and sample traces.
2026-03-10 10:18 - SRE (Rafael Ortega): Pulled node telemetry and found sustained clock throttling on node-gpu-07. Suspect thermal throttling plus poor batching. Investigating autoscaler policy.
2026-03-10 12:04 - Customer (Lan Nguyen - BeaconAI): Confirmed a long-running streaming ingestion job started at 07:50 UTC. Can move it if helpful; prefer a fix that lets it coexist.

2026-03-11 02:33 - Eng (Priya Desai): Verified serving-runtime traces show batch fragmentation due to many small concurrent requests. Suggest enabling adaptive coalescing and increasing min_replicas for this pool as a stopgap.

2026-03-12 07:20 - Support (Rafael Ortega): Applied emergency workarounds: node isolation label added, large stream job drained off node-gpu-07, min_replicas set to 3 for BeaconAI reserved pool. Monitoring metrics show p95 returned to within baseline after draining.
2026-03-12 10:05 - Customer (Lan Nguyen): Verified product SLA is back to expected performance. Thanks — awaiting permanent fixes.

2026-03-12 12:10 - Eng (Priya Desai): Created follow-up engineering task INT-4321 to address autoscaler tuning and scheduler improvements to avoid colocating long-seq streaming with latency-sensitive short-seq workloads.
resolution: Workaround applied 2026-03-12: 1) Drained and cordoned node-gpu-07 to evict the large streaming job. 2) Temporarily increased reserved pool min_replicas to 3 to improve headroom and batching efficiency. 3) Added node-label isolation rule for high-memory streaming workloads.
Immediate effect: p95 reduced from ~900ms to ~350ms for short-sequence traffic; 504s ceased during observation window.

Permanent fix plan: (a) Autoscaler: change evaluation window and lower scale_up_threshold for dedicated pools with short-seq traffic; (b) Scheduler: implement affinity rules to avoid colocating extreme streaming jobs with latency-sensitive pods; (c) Runtime: implement adaptive coalescing optimization to avoid micro-batch overhead for bursty tiny requests. See INT-4321 for engineering tasks and milestones.

Follow-up actions assigned: Rafael Ortega to monitor for 72h and coordinate postmortem. Priya Desai to own INT-4321 and propose PRs for autoscaler/scheduler changes. SRE to check node thermal profile and adjust host power limits if necessary.
Customer follow-up: Offer to run a dedicated isolated node group for BeaconAI's streaming jobs and provide recommended autoscaler policy template for reserved pools.
Status: In Progress (mitigations applied, engineering follow-up open).

source.summary: Intermittent timeouts and doubled latency for repeated prompts: stale prefix / KV cache misses during streamed retry path
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer (AtlasWrite, Dedicated) reports intermittent per-request latency spikes (1.8-2.5x baseline) and occasional streaming timeouts when users resubmit identical prompts or when client SDK retries a broken stream. Impact: Several customer-facing flows (autosummarize & chat history replay) experience user-facing slowdowns and ~5-10% request failures during peak traffic windows. Environment: prod/us-east, dedicated pool id d-pool-12. Observed primarily on gptx-13b-quantized and gptx-8b-finetune. Steps to reproduce: 1) Send a multi-turn conversation with a repeated prompt token prefix. 2) Force a transient stream disconnect on the client to trigger retry logic (SDK exponential backoff). 3) Observe server-side logs and latency metrics for repeated prompt ID. Expected behavior: Repeat prompt should hit warmed prefix/KV cache and return near-baseline latency with streaming retry preserving KV cache. Actual behavior: First retry shows KV cache miss, full KV lookup or cold fetch (~150-300ms extra) and in some cases the retry path times out after streaming proxy backpressure, producing a 504 to the caller. Recent change: Queryable prefix indexing refactor (serving-runtime v2.8.0) rolled 2026-03-08 suspected to coincide with regression window. Attachments: customer traces, sample request headers, and timing graphs uploaded to ticket attachments.

Logs / snippets: Error patterns observed in logs (prod us-east): "kv-cache: prefix-miss for key=prefix_0x4f2a" followed by "kv-fetch: cross-pool fetch latency=210ms" and then "streaming-proxy: retry-forward - client-side disconnect, initiating retry path". Several entries show the serving node evicting prefix entries after background compaction even when traffic indicates hot prefixes.
investigation_notes: 2026-03-10 10:20 - Support: Confirmed customer reproducible with SDK simulated disconnect. Collected p95/p99 latency traces and raw streaming logs.
2026-03-10 12:05 - SRE: Correlated latency spikes to increased KV miss rate for prefix keys during retry windows. KV eviction rate spiked after compaction window in pool d-pool-12.

2026-03-10 14:30 - Eng (serving-runtime): Found that refactor introduced a race where prefix entries marked as 'candidate-for-evict' were eligible for eviction earlier, but retry path assumes presence of warmed prefix and skips prefetch.

2026-03-11 09:10 - Hypothesis: Streaming retry path does not trigger prefix pre-warm when it attaches to existing request context; combined with aggressive eviction this yields cold lookup latency and occasional streaming backpressure leading to timeouts.
2026-03-11 13:45 - Repro test: In staging, toggling eviction threshold + enabling pre-warm on retry reduced p95 from 420ms to 180ms for repeated prompts.

2026-03-12 08:00 - Risk: If rollout continues, more dedicated pools with high retry rates may see increased user errors; customer impact likely for high-frequency repeat prompts (chatbots, summarizers).
comments: 2026-03-10 10:45 - Ava Chen (Support): Notified AtlasWrite success team and requested sample trace ids. They provided traces: trace-az-8912 and trace-az-8919.
2026-03-10 12:25 - Diego Martinez (SRE): Spun up targeted flamegraphs for d-pool-12 and confirmed elevated kv-fetch latencies aligning with customer traces.

2026-03-11 11:00 - Priya Nair (Serving Eng): Identified race in prefix eviction logic introduced in v2.8.0. Created a branch to add an explicit pre-warm on the retry hot-path and to reduce eviction aggressiveness for 'recently-accessed' prefixes.
2026-03-11 16:35 - Customer (AtlasWrite, support@atlaswrite.example): Provided user impact report. They requested immediate mitigation or a config knob to reduce timeouts on their dedicated pool.

2026-03-12 09:20 - Diego Martinez (SRE): Deployed a temporary config to d-pool-12 to extend prefix TTL and enabled retry pre-warm. Monitoring shows p95 latency back to baseline and streaming errors falling to ~0.8% from 6%.
2026-03-12 12:00 - Ava Chen (Support): Informed customer of temporary mitigation and ETA for full patch (see resolution). Customer accepted mitigation and requested postmortem once resolved.
source.root_cause: A race introduced in the prefix/KV cache refactor caused aggressive eviction of recently used prefix entries while the streaming retry code assumed warmed prefixes. Retry path did not trigger prefix pre-warm, resulting in cold KV fetches; the extra latency combined with streaming proxy backpressure caused retries to hit timeouts.
source.workaround: Temporary config change applied to d-pool-12: increase prefix TTL and enable explicit pre-warm on streaming retry path. This reduces cold fetches and drops streaming timeouts for the customer.
source.resolution: Patch accepted: serving-runtime PR #3124 adds explicit prefix pre-warm on retry path and tightens eviction gating for recently-used prefixes. Patch scheduled for canary in prod on 2026-03-13 and full rollout on 2026-03-14 after monitoring window. Post-deploy: confirm with AtlasWrite that their observed errors are resolved and close ticket.
follow_up_tasks: Post-deploy: run 48h regression tests on dedicated pools with synthetic retry storms to validate no hidden perf regressions.
Add an observability alert: sustained increase in kv-miss-rate for hot-prefixes tied to streaming retry counts.
Confluence: Draft a short postmortem and add preventative checklist for future prefix refactors.
Customer communication: Schedule a 30-min review with AtlasWrite success team to walk through fix and confirm SLA satisfaction.

source.summary: Blended invoice missing per-workload rate memo for mixed streaming+batch usage
source.description: Issue summary: Customer BrightScale.ai received March invoice with a single blended line item labeled 'multi-workload blended rate' but there is no memo breakdown showing how streaming vs batch embeddings usage were apportioned or which rate tiers applied. Impact: customer cannot reconcile costs against internal chargebacks and is requesting detailed memo lines or provisional credit. Environment: production billing run for billing period 2026-02-01 to 2026-02-28 covering hosted and dedicated fallbacks across us-east and eu-west. Customer says invoice total matches expectation but line-item explanation is insufficient for their finance team.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Identify org ID 98765432 for BrightScale.ai in billing UI. 2) Generate invoice #INV-2026-03-BS-98765432 in admin console. 3) Observe line items: single 'multi-workload blended rate' line with blended tag and no per-workload memo. 4) Compare usage export for period and note separate streaming token counts and batch embedding token counts; reconciliation expected to show per-workload rates applied.
source.logs: billing-run: run_id=2026-03-02-07z, invoice_job_id=job-8f3b21, postback_processor=v2.14.3, blend-decider=v1.9.0. Exported usage file: s3://billing-exports/2026/02/brightscale/usage.csv (attached). Error logs: invoice-generator did not throw errors; memo-builder logged INFO 'applied blended policy' but WARN 'memo-fragment-suppressed: no-diff-threshold' at 2026-03-02T07:12:44Z.
comments: 2026-03-10 10:22 - Support (Ava Chen): Opened ticket after customer support call. Customer requests either a) full memo lines showing per-workload rates and quantities, or b) provisional credit until we provide an audit. Provided them with exported usage CSV as interim artifact.

2026-03-10 11:05 - Billing Ops (Noah Kim): Investigating the invoice-generator memo-fragment suppression flag — looks like a recent threshold change collapsed small-line deltas into the blended bucket. I will fetch the decision logs.

2026-03-11 09:40 - Engineering (Marcus Patel): Found the blend-decider change in PR github.com/redwood-inference/billing/PR#2899; a threshold adjustment was deployed to reduce line noise. It suppressed memo fragments when per-workload delta < 0.5% of invoice total. For BrightScale the batch embed delta was 0.32% and got suppressed.

2026-03-11 14:05 - Support (Ava Chen): Customer unhappy with suppression rule; they want per-workload detail regardless of delta for enterprise tier. Requested escalation to apply exception and reissue invoice memo lines.

2026-03-12 08:30 - Billing Ops (Noah Kim): Prepared a manual audit CSV with per-workload attribution and suggested provisional credit equal to 0.32% of blended line (rounded) while we re-run memo generator with override.
source.investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: recent change to memo-fragment suppression threshold (PR#2899) collapsed small per-workload lines into single blended line for cleanliness. Confirmed by searching decision logs in blend-decider for org 98765432: 'suppressed_fragments=[batch_embed]' with ratio=0.0032. Audit of usage export shows batch embedding tokens=1,204,320 (0.32% cost contribution) and streaming tokens=378,123,442. The customer is enterprise and has contract clause requiring line-itemization for chargebacks; suppression should not apply to enterprise. Roots: conditional exception flag for enterprise was not being applied because org tag normalization changed during tag migration (tag=enterprise->enterprise-v2).
source.resolution: Workaround applied: Billing Ops ran a manual memo re-generation for invoice INV-2026-03-BS-98765432 with override 'force_itemize=true'. Updated invoice notes attached and provisional credit of $18.72 applied to customer's account (corresponds to suppressed fragment). Permanent fix: engineering to backfill tag normalization mapping and add a unit test to ensure enterprise exception honours both legacy and new tags. PR opened: github.com/redwood-inference/billing/PR#2923 (fix + test). Rollout plan: hotfix merged and scheduled for 2026-03-13 morning billing window. Follow-up actions: reissue corrected invoice PDF and email to BrightScale finance, confirm acceptance of provisional credit, and update confluence memo explaining suppression thresholds and enterprise exceptions.
next_steps: Apply tag-normalization hotfix to blend-decider (PR#2923) and deploy to staging for smoke tests
Re-run memo-generator for Feb billing and reissue corrected invoice PDF to customer
Confirm customer acceptance of provisional credit and close ticket
Add retrospective note to #support-billing and Confluence explaining the change and exception rules

source.summary: Partial-stream resume replay leads to duplicate external webhook side-effects for Python function-calls
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports duplicate external API calls (webhook POSTs) when using Python SDK streaming + function-calling. Behavior occurs after a network interruption causes the client to reconnect and resume a partially delivered streaming response. The resumed session appears to replay the function invocation without the original idempotency token reaching the downstream webhook, resulting in side-effect duplication (duplicate invoices / duplicate task creation).

Impact:
- Customer (SlateCRM Inc.) is observing duplicate third-party webhook side-effects impacting billing and downstream task systems.
- Occurs intermittently on long-running streaming interactions where the client reconnects to resume.
- Business impact: customer flagged as P1 due to operational impact on billing pipeline.

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated (customer-owned VPC) in us-east region.
- SDK: python-sdk v1.8.7 pinned by the integration.
- Server: streaming-proxy v2.12.
- Webhook consumer is a third-party billing service that relies on a header idempotency-key to dedupe requests.

Desired outcome:
- Prevent duplicated external side-effects on stream resume by ensuring idempotency metadata persists across reconnect/resume flows, or provide a recommended workaround pattern for safe function execution from streaming clients.
source.steps_to_reproduce:
1) Create a Python streaming session using python-sdk v1.8.7 and enable function-calling for external webhook invocation.
2) Trigger a function call that results in a webhook POST (payload includes business operation such as invoice.create).
3) During the streaming response, interrupt network (simulate client disconnect) after the function-call frame but before final stream close.
4) Reconnect and resume the stream using the SDK's resume logic.
5) Observe two webhook POSTs at the billing partner: one from the original attempt and a second on resume.

Notes: Customer can reproduce ~30% of the time using a flaky network emulator. Reproduction is faster with larger function payloads and longer stream durations.
logs: [2026-03-09T15:12:03Z] client: stream-disconnect: websocket EOF
[2026-03-09T15:12:06Z] client: reconnect attempt: resume_token=abc123
[2026-03-09T15:12:07Z] proxy: resume accepted, session_id=sess-77
[2026-03-09T15:12:07Z] function-exec: invoked external webhook idempotency-key: <MISSING>
[2026-03-09T15:12:07Z] billing-partner: POST /invoices 200 id=inv_554
[2026-03-09T15:12:07Z] billing-partner: POST /invoices 200 id=inv_555 (duplicate)
investigation_notes: Initial support triage: confirmed duplicate webhook receipts from customer's partner logs and corresponding stream resume events in streaming-proxy traces.

Hypothesis: idempotency metadata (header or per-call token) is not being attached to resume frames; resume path reconstructs a function invocation without original metadata, so webhook receives a new request without dedupe key.

Trace inspection: server-side function-call frame created at t=15:11:59 included idempotency-key header in initial POST. On resume, proxy generated a new POST lacking the header—logs show '<MISSING>' where original key should be.
SDK behavior: python-sdk v1.8.7 persists resume_token but does NOT explicitly persist per-function idempotency header across reconnects for streaming sessions (confirmed by SDK team).

Repro rate correlates with network disconnect timing: if the upstream already received and ACKed the function-call, resume should not re-fire; but current resume logic sometimes replays the frame when ACK was lost to client.

Related internal note: streaming-proxy resume flow deserializes pending frames and re-emits them; it relies on frame metadata provided by client during resume. If client omits per-call headers during resume, downstream receives incomplete requests.
comments: Support (Aisha Kapoor) [2026-03-10]: Acknowledged ticket, requested partner logs and full SDK client logs. Asked if customer can reproduce with an isolated webhook stub to confirm duplicate payloads are coming from Redwood path.

Customer (Simon Lee, SlateCRM) [2026-03-10]: Provided partner logs showing duplicate invoice ids and attached client trace from python-sdk. Reproduction steps and flaky network simulator config included.

Eng (Ethan Park) [2026-03-11]: Reproduced locally using network emulator, confirmed resume path can replay function-call without original idempotency header. Filed SDK change to persist per-call idempotency in resume frames (see PR link).

Support (Aisha Kapoor) [2026-03-11]: Provided temporary mitigation guidance to customer: (1) add an application-level idempotency token in the function payload (body) that their webhook consumer can dedupe on, (2) enable server-side confirmation callback pattern if possible, (3) upgrade to upcoming SDK patch when available.
Customer (Simon Lee) [2026-03-12]: Implemented application-level token dedupe and observed no further duplicates in 48h smoke tests. Requested recommendation for permanent fix and timeline.

Eng (Ethan Park) [2026-03-12]: Proposed two fixes: (A) SDK change to persist per-call idempotency headers in resume frames and ensure streaming-proxy replays those headers (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/987), (B) server-side safety: streaming-proxy could reject replay of frames already ACKed by the downstream if it's able to detect progress via ack markers. Plan: land SDK patch first for faster rollout.
resolution_notes: Workaround implemented by customer: application-level idempotency token in function payload dedupes at billing partner — successful in smoke tests.
Short-term fix: SDK patch in python-sdk v1.8.8 will persist function-call idempotency header across resume flows (PR #987). Patch scheduled for staged rollout to dedicated customers on 2026-03-18.
Long-term backlog item: enhance streaming-proxy to include explicit ack markers for function-call frames so resume cannot replay already-acknowledged side-effects. Logged as INT-4320 for infra team.
Customer notified of planned SDK rollout and long-term server-side work. Recommend customer to keep application-level dedupe until patch rolled out.

source.summary: Intermittent p99 timeouts after client stream restarts due to continuous-batcher linger accumulation
source.description:
Issue summary: Customers (NimbleRetail dedicated tenancy) observed intermittent p99 user-facing timeouts (504 / request timeouts) concentrated after client-side stream restarts. Impact: Multiple production routes serving chat completions saw p95->p99 divergence, with p99 climbing from ~450ms baseline to 3-6s and occasional 504s. Affected customer reports started 2026-03-10 14:20 UTC and continued in waves.

Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated pool tenant for NimbleRetail, models: redwood-1.0-mk4 (default), fallback to gptj-14b configured.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens a streaming connection and makes a short request (~12 tokens).
2) Client intentionally restarts the stream shortly after (~300-800ms) to refresh headers/session.
3) Server continuous-batcher observed many short-lived streams that resume quickly; linger timers accumulate pending micro-batches.
4) After a burst of restarts, batch flushes become interleaved and inflate processing latency for a subset of requests, producing p99 spikes and eventual client timeouts.

Observed behavior: Periodic clustering of very short-lived streams leads to "linger buildup" inside continuous-batcher. Instead of flushing quickly, micro-batches coalesce across restart boundaries and wait for stray tokens/kv-cache warmups, creating head-of-line blocking for otherwise short requests. Metrics show increased batch sizes for affected cohorts paired with elevated per-request scheduling delay.

Expected behavior: Short-lived resumed streams should either be routed to a fast-path (low-linger flush) or isolated so that stream restart churn does not elevate latency for unrelated short requests.

Logs/metrics:
- Request traces: https://grafana.redwood/company/d/streams/stream-traces?var-tenant=nimbleretail&from=2026-03-10T14:15:00Z&to=2026-03-10T15:30:00Z
- SLO alerts: #incidents in Slack: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C05SUPPORT/p1646990000
- Example backend traces (zip): s3://redwood-support-dumps/SUP-135790/traces-2026-03-10.zip
- Notable log excerpt: "batcher: linger accumulated 42ms > configured20ms; flush delayed by kv-cache-warmup(1)" repeated across affected shards.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced locally by running a client harness that repeatedly opens and closes streams with 200-700ms gap; showed same pattern of increased scheduling delay and larger-than-expected micro-batches.
- Continuous-batcher metrics show a drift in per-cohort linger window when there are many rapid stream lifecycle events; the cohort state machine was leaving stale timers active.
- KV-cache miss/warmup interactions: when a resumed stream is first routed to a node with cold KV cache, the batcher delays flush waiting for cache population which can exceed linger threshold for other coalesced requests.
- Autoscaling and GPU utilization looked healthy; not a capacity shortage.

Root-cause hypothesis: An interaction between stream lifecycle events (frequent restarts), cohort timer state, and kv-cache warmup causes the continuous-batcher to extend effective linger windows for a short period. That extended linger allows otherwise short requests to be coalesced into larger micro-batches or wait on cache-population, causing p99 latency spikes and some timeouts.

Immediate mitigations tested:
- Toggle: applied a config override for NimbleRetail tenant to reduce continuous-batcher linger max to 8ms (from 20ms) for affected routes; saw p99 drop back toward baseline in canary cluster.
- Enabled per-connection fast-path for short-lived streams (experimental) which bypasses cohort coalescing; reduced observed 504s by ~80% in smoke tests.

Next steps / action items:
1) Engineering: implement deterministic cohort timer reset on stream restart (owner: @Ethan Park, follow PR #2891). Target: revert lingering state immediately when a stream is closed/reopened.
2) SRE: roll out tenant config to all NimbleRetail routes and monitor for 24h (owner: @Marta Ruiz).
3) Product/Docs: add guidance for customers about safe stream reuse patterns and recommended client retry jitter (owner: @Aisha Khan).
4) Postmortem: schedule follow-up review with continuous-batcher owners and include workload patterns that trigger the state drift.

Customer impact summary: NimbleRetail observed degraded tail latency impacting chat user sessions intermittently. No data loss. Multiple support alerts and escalations to customer success.

source.expected_result: Short requests and quick stream restarts should not cause head-of-line latency for unrelated requests; p99 should remain within SLO (~<800ms).

title: Intermittent p99 timeouts after client stream restarts due to continuous-batcher linger accumulation
preview:
Issue summary: Customers (NimbleRetail dedicated tenancy) observed intermittent p99 user-facing timeouts (504 / request timeouts) concentrated after client-side stream restarts. Impact: Multiple production routes serving chat completions saw p95->p99 divergence, with p99 climbing from ~450ms baseline to 3-6s and occasional 504s. Affected customer reports started 2026-03-10 14:20 UTC and continued in waves.

Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated pool tenant for NimbleRetail, models: redwood-1.0-mk4 (default), fallback to gptj-14b configured.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens a streaming connection and makes a short request (~12 tokens).
2) Client intentionally restarts the stream shortly after (~300-800ms) to refresh headers/session.
3) Server continuous-batcher observed many short-lived streams that resume quickly; linger timers accumulate pending micro-batches.
4) After a burst of restarts, batch flushes become interleaved and inflate processing latency for a subset of requests, producing p99 spikes and eventual client timeouts.

Observed behavior: Periodic clustering of very short-lived streams leads to "linger buildup" inside continuous-batcher. Instead of flushing quickly, micro-batches coalesce across restart boundaries and wait for stray tokens/kv-cache warmups, creating head-of-line blocking for otherwise short requests. Metrics show increased batch sizes for affected cohorts paired with elevated per-request scheduling delay.

Expected behavior: Short-lived resumed streams should either be routed to a fast-path (low-linger flush) or isolated so that stream restart churn does not elevate latency for unrelated short requests.

Logs/metrics:
- Request traces: https://grafana.redwood/company/d/streams/stream-traces?var-tenant=nimbleretail&from=2026-03-10T14:15:00Z&to=2026-03-10T15:30:00Z
- SLO alerts: #incidents in Slack: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C05SUPPORT/p1646990000
- Example backend traces (zip): s3://redwood-support-dumps/SUP-135790/traces-2026-03-10.zip
- Notable log excerpt: "batcher: linger accumulated 42ms > configured20ms; flush delayed by kv-cache-warmup(1)" repeated across affected shards.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced locally by running a client harness that repeatedly opens and closes streams with 200-700ms gap; showed same pattern of increased scheduling delay and larger-than-expected micro-batches.
- Continuous-batcher metrics show a drift in per-cohort linger window when there are many rapid stream lifecycle events; the cohort state machine was leaving stale timers active.
- KV-cache miss/warmup interactions: when a resumed stream is first routed to a node with cold KV cache, the batcher delays flush waiting for cache population which can exceed linger threshold for other coalesced requests.
- Autoscaling and GPU utilization looked healthy; not a capacity shortage.

Root-cause hypothesis: An interaction between stream lifecycle events (frequent restarts), cohort timer state, and kv-cache warmup causes the continuous-batcher to extend effective linger windows for a short period. That extended linger allows otherwise short requests to be coalesced into larger micro-batches or wait on cache-population, causing p99 latency spikes and some timeouts.

Immediate mitigations tested:
- Toggle: applied a config override for NimbleRetail tenant to reduce continuous-batcher linger max to 8ms (from 20ms) for affected routes; saw p99 drop back toward baseline in canary cluster.
- Enabled per-connection fast-path for short-lived streams (experimental) which bypasses cohort coalescing; reduced observed 504s by ~80% in smoke tests.

Next steps / action items:
1) Engineering: implement deterministic cohort timer reset on stream restart (owner: @Ethan Park, follow PR #2891). Target: revert lingering state immediately when a stream is closed/reopened.
2) SRE: roll out tenant config to all NimbleRetail routes and monitor for 24h (owner: @Marta Ruiz).
3) Product/Docs: add guidance for customers about safe stream reuse patterns and recommended client retry jitter (owner: @Aisha Khan).
4) Postmortem: schedule follow-up review with continuous-batcher owners and include workload patterns that trigger the state drift.

Customer impact summary: NimbleRetail observed degraded tail latency impacting chat user sessions intermittently. No data loss. Multiple support alerts and escalations to customer success.

source.summary: Edge failover + auth rotation causes missing metadata envelope and trailing paragraph silence on streaming resume
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent incomplete final metadata and a silent trailing paragraph when their websocket or SSE stream resumes after an edge failover/auth cookie rotation. The response body contains the content tokens but the final metadata envelope (terminal JSON object containing stream-level fields like 'id'/'usage'/'choices' metadata) is sometimes missing or cut and the last paragraph is not emitted, causing the client SDK to throw a parse error or appear to hang.

Impact:
- Several Inkwell Media users see a parse error or a UI spinner after a reconnect; last paragraph (summary) often never appears.
- Occurs ~0.5-1% of long-running streaming sessions for this customer during peak traffic.
- Affects critical editor flows where the final paragraph contains a license blurb.

Environment:
- Customer: Inkwell Media (dedicated account)
- Region: us-east
- Model: redwood-3b-v1 pinned
- Path: Streaming via websocket upgrade (wss) through edge-proxy cluster with header rewrite and cookie rotation set by customer-auth service
- Client: Browser webapp using Redwood JS SDK v1.4.2 behind corporate NAT/proxy

Steps to reproduce (attempted):
1) Start long-form streaming generation via websocket from the customer app with conversation payload > 800 tokens.
2) While streaming, trigger an auth cookie rotation (customer does so via SSO refresh) or simulate an edge failover by cycling one of the edge-proxy instances.
3) Client reconnects and requests resume using the session token; streaming continues but the final metadata object is missing or truncated and the closing paragraph is not emitted.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming tokens continue to arrive, and the last content tokens look complete, but no terminal metadata envelope (closing JSON with 'id' and aggregated usage) is present.
- Client SDK keeps waiting for the terminal event and does not surface the final paragraph. In some cases the SDK receives a small number of trailing bytes that appear to be a gzip fragment or partial header left by the edge-proxy layer, preventing parser completion.
- Reconnect sequences sometimes produce a duplicate initial system message, but not the terminal envelope.

Expected behavior:
- On resume, stream completes and the terminal metadata envelope is emitted exactly once; client SDK finalizes output and UI shows the concluding paragraph.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- 3 zipped websocket capture logs (wscat) showing trailing bytes on the final packet (attached to the ticket).
- Edge-proxy logs showing instance swap at 2026-03-10T17:12:34Z.
- SDK debug trace showing parser awaiting final 'end' event.
comments: 2026-03-10 18:05 - Ava Chen: Created ticket after customer provided captures. Customer reports ~0.8% failure rate during peak; reproduces with auth rotation + edge swap.

2026-03-10 19:20 - Diego Martinez: Acknowledged. Assigned to investigating SRE and runtime teams. Asking for full PCAP and edge-proxy debug logs. Requested customer to reproduce with verbose logging enabled.

2026-03-11 09:10 - SRE (Maya Patel): Initial look — edge-proxy showed conn close with FIN prior to origin sending terminal metadata in ~2 of 6 captures. Also see truncated chunk with non-UTF8 suffix in one trace. Suspect proxy buffer flush on failover.

2026-03-11 11:45 - Runtime Eng (Liam O'Connor): We instrumented streaming runtime around terminal frame emission; in our internal test we saw terminal envelope emitted but client-side reassembly failed if a 0-byte keepalive was coalesced with the final chunk by the proxy. Added hypothesis that header modification (cookie rewrite) triggers a small mid-stream rewrite, causing chunk boundary shift.

2026-03-12 08:00 - Diego Martinez: Reached out to customer CS with a curated test build (edge-proxy config toggles) and a client SDK recommended retry. Customer responses pending; asked CS to provide next repro window.
2026-03-12 15:30 - Customer (Inkwell Support): Confirmed they could reproduce on demand in staging by toggling SSO session rotation and that applying the temporary proxy setting stops the truncation.
investigation_notes: Edge-proxy cluster shows conn handoff events matching customer timestamps; at handoff, some streams are sent a TCP-level RST to the midstream socket on one of the older edge instances.
One capture contains trailing bytes 0x1f8b (gzip magic) appended to the final data segment — likely proxy compression state incorrectly closed mid-stream.

Serving runtime logs (token emitter) show terminal envelope written to socket on origin at time T, but the sequence number mapping between socket write and TLS record on the egress path occasionally loses the final record if the proxy does an aggressive drain and rebind.
Reproduction in our lab by simulating cookie rewrite + immediate connection migration increases chance of missing terminal envelope from ~0.3% to ~5% when using the current edge-proxy config.

SDK parser currently waits for a clear 'stream_end' marker; if trailing garbage bytes appear after expected JSON close, it does not auto-trim and will throw a parse error rather than attempt best-effort completion.
next_steps: SRE to deploy a canary edge-proxy build that preserves compression state and ensures graceful socket drain (rollback-safe toggle) — target ETA 2026-03-14.
Runtime team to add a defensive post-write flush acknowledgement for terminal envelope and emit a short retry if socket reports early close — experimental patch PR opened.
SDK team to ship a minor patch (v1.4.3) to tolerate small trailing non-UTF8 bytes when final JSON envelope is otherwise complete (best-effort parse fallback).
Coordinate with customer to run a controlled repro window and confirm mitigation on their dedicated cluster once canary is live.
source.resolution_notes: (pending) Partial mitigations identified. Edge canary and SDK tolerance patch are in flight. No confirmed permanent fix yet; customer accepted temporary workaround (adjust proxy drain timeout) in staging and saw reproduction stop in their tests.
source.public_notes: Customer-facing summary: We are actively investigating a rare streaming resume failure where the final metadata envelope may be dropped during an edge failover combined with auth cookie rotation. We're rolling out an edge canary and a short-term SDK tolerance patch; we'll follow up with a permanent fix after root cause validation.

source.summary: Dedicated pool throughput collapse from connection-reservoir leak causing batched-slot starvation
source.description:
Issue summary:
DataScribe reported a sudden drop in dedicated pool throughput in us-west-2 starting 2026-03-10 14:10 UTC. Customer traffic experienced elevated p50/p95/p99 for batched routes; many short requests were queued and not scheduled to GPUs.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p99 increased from ~220ms to >2.8s for batched generation routes.
- Throughput dropped ~40% for the reserved pool attached to DataScribe's dedicated capacity.
- Automatic fallback to lower-tier variants triggered repeatedly, causing billing and quality regressions.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: ds-prod-reserved-17 (us-west-2)
- Serving runtime: 2026.02.21-release
- Autoscaler: adaptive-v3
- Affected models: redwood-v1-13b (pinned), redwood-v2-7b (fallback)

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Run high concurrency burst of many short chatlet streams (avg 120 tokens, 50ms client think-time) pinned to ds-prod-reserved-17.
2) Observe steady-state: throughput falls after ~20 minutes of sustained small-stream traffic; p99 climbs.

Observed behavior:
- Long-lived TCP connection handles and scheduler slots remained reserved even when requests idle between tokens.
- Scheduler shows high "connection_reservoir_in_use" metric approaching pool capacity; batchers unable to form optimal batches because slots appear held by idle connections.
- Autoscaler did not provision additional GPUs due to perceived capacity fullness; eviction signals low.

Expected behavior:
- Idle connections should yield reservoir slots after short idle window so batchers can reassign slots to active requests.

Relevant logs and dashboards:
- Loki query: {job=\"serving-scheduler\",pool=\"ds-prod-reserved-17\"} |~ \"reservoir\"
- Grafana dashboard: /d/pool/ds-prod-reserved-17/throughput-latency
- Trace sample: trace.redwood.ai/trace/8a3f2e7b
source.investigation_notes:
Summary of findings:
- Reproduced in staging using a synthetic small-stream flood test (script attached to ticket).
- Scheduler maintains a per-connection reservoir counter; we observed a regression in connection idle timeout policy after release 2026.02.21 where ephemeral streams keep reservoir entries for up to 5 minutes instead of configured 15s.
- Root causes identified: (1) change in connection pool handshake path that delayed idle detection; (2) a bug in reservoir bookkeeping where multi-part streams created duplicate reservations that were not folded on reconnect.
- Evidence: metric 'scheduler.connection_reservoir_occupied' moves to 95% while 'gpu_attach_success' remains nominally green. Autoscaler sees pool as saturated because reservoir occupancy is used as a gating signal.
- Correlation with customer traffic pattern: DataScribe uses many long-lived interactive streams with intermittent typing pauses (~30-90s), which previously yielded slots quickly; regression now keeps them reserved causing slot starvation for batched short requests.
comments: 2026-03-10 15:05 - Support (Ava Chen): Acknowledged customer, collected initial traces and asked for traffic sample. Escalated to SRE with high priority.
2026-03-10 15:28 - SRE (Diego Martinez): Started reproducer against staging. Observed reservoir holding behavior. Added temporary alert on scheduler.connection_reservoir_occupied > 80%.
2026-03-10 16:10 - Eng (Priya Kapoor): Confirmed recent handshake change in runtime PR #4821 could affect idle detection; requested runtime logs for connection state transitions.
2026-03-10 18:02 - Support (Ava Chen): Customer accepted a short maintenance window to apply a configuration rollback to restore 15s idle timeout on their pool.

2026-03-11 02:14 - SRE (Diego Martinez): Rolled back the timeout config for ds-prod-reserved-17 as an emergency mitigation — reservoir occupancy fell from 96% to 28% and p99 returned to ~240ms. Noted this is a temporary mitigation.

2026-03-11 09:30 - Eng (Marcela Ruiz): Proposed code fix to fold duplicate reservations and added an integration test for multi-part streams. Patch target: autoscaler v3 + scheduler bookkeeping. Linked PR: github.com/redwood-inference/autoscaler/pull/1299.

2026-03-12 11:05 - Support (Ava Chen): Customer report: latency stabilized after mitigation; however they saw a single recurrence during a midnight burst — investigating follow-up automation to prevent regressions.

2026-03-12 14:40 - Eng (Diego Martinez): Hotfix deployed to scheduler that ensures X milliseconds idle threshold is respected and merges duplicate reservoir entries on reconnect. Monitoring shows stable reservoir occupancy across pools.
source.resolution:
Mitigation and fix:
- Emergency mitigation: rolled back the idle timeout config for the affected dedicated pool (ds-prod-reserved-17) from 5m -> 15s which immediately freed reservoir slots and restored batching behavior.
- Code fix: merged hotfix to scheduler that (a) enforces idle detection on connection state transitions and (b) merges duplicate reservations for multi-part streams. Patch landed in runtime and autoscaler PRs and shipped to prod on 2026-03-12 14:30 UTC.
- Customer outcome: DataScribe throughput returned to baseline; p99 dropped to pre-incident levels.
source.root_cause: Regression introduced in release 2026.02.21 that increased connection idle retention and a separate bug where multi-part streams duplicated reservoir reservations. Together they caused effective slot hoarding in dedicated pools which looked like GPU shortage to the autoscaler, triggering reduced batch formation and throughput collapse.
follow_up_tasks: ENG: Add integration tests for reservoir folding and multi-part-stream lifecycle (owner: Marcela Ruiz).
SRE: Add a safeguard in autoscaler to ignore reservoir occupancy spikes from >50% transient duplicate reservations for 2 minutes before making scaling decisions.
DX: Update runbook for dedicated pools to include connection-reservoir occupancy as a primary triage metric.
Support: Contact DataScribe with RCA and offer a post-incident call (owner: Ava Chen).

title: Dedicated pool throughput collapse from connection-reservoir leak causing batched-slot starvation
preview:
Issue summary:
DataScribe reported a sudden drop in dedicated pool throughput in us-west-2 starting 2026-03-10 14:10 UTC. Customer traffic experienced elevated p50/p95/p99 for batched routes; many short requests were queued and not scheduled to GPUs.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p99 increased from ~220ms to >2.8s for batched generation routes.
- Throughput dropped ~40% for the reserved pool attached to DataScribe's dedicated capacity.
- Automatic fallback to lower-tier variants triggered repeatedly, causing billing and quality regressions.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: ds-prod-reserved-17 (us-west-2)
- Serving runtime: 2026.02.21-release
- Autoscaler: adaptive-v3
- Affected models: redwood-v1-13b (pinned), redwood-v2-7b (fallback)

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Run high concurrency burst of many short chatlet streams (avg 120 tokens, 50ms client think-time) pinned to ds-prod-reserved-17.
2) Observe steady-state: throughput falls after ~20 minutes of sustained small-stream traffic; p99 climbs.

Observed behavior:
- Long-lived TCP connection handles and scheduler slots remained reserved even when requests idle between tokens.
- Scheduler shows high "connection_reservoir_in_use" metric approaching pool capacity; batchers unable to form optimal batches because slots appear held by idle connections.
- Autoscaler did not provision additional GPUs due to perceived capacity fullness; eviction signals low.

Expected behavior:
- Idle connections should yield reservoir slots after short idle window so batchers can reassign slots to active requests.

Relevant logs and dashboards:
- Loki query: {job=\"serving-scheduler\",pool=\"ds-prod-reserved-17\"} |~ \"reservoir\"
- Grafana dashboard: /d/pool/ds-prod-reserved-17/throughput-latency
- Trace sample: trace.redwood.ai/trace/8a3f2e7b
