source.summary: Erratic 5xx surge correlating with prefetch stall + connection-throttling on API gateway
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer (CloudNote Labs, dedicated) started seeing a sudden increase in 5xx responses for text-generation API calls beginning ~2026-03-10 01:18 UTC. Errors are intermittent but sustained in bursts over 10-30 minute windows.

Impact:
- ~18% of requests from the customer's endpoint returned 5xx during bursts, affecting production feature serving (user-facing).
- Visible increase in tail latency for streaming sessions.

Environment:
- Hosted: Dedicated pool for CloudNote (us-east region).
- Models: redwood/open-llama-7b pinned -> gptq-6b fallback configured.
- Traffic pattern: periodic prefetch-heavy workload (long prompts + multi-call prefetch).

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Run a sustained throughput test with prefetch_enabled=true and keep-alive connections from ~100 concurrent clients.
2) Observe API gateway connection throttling counters spike and upstream runtime 5xx rates rise.
3) Some streaming sessions drop with 502/503; non-streaming requests see 500/504.

Observed behavior:
- API gateway shows 'conn_throttle' metric jumps correlated to 5xx bursts.
- Runtime logs show short-lived worker exits with OOM/EAGER-EVICT warnings around same timestamps.
- KV-prefetch subsystem reports elevated cache eviction/repoll events (kv_prefetch_miss_rate).
- Autoscaler shows cooldown windows preventing immediate scale-up during initial burst.

Expected behavior:
- Stable 2xx success rate with graceful autoscaler scaling, no connection-level throttling for dedicated customers within committed capacity.
comments: 2026-03-10 02:05 - Support (Ava Chen): Initial triage. Received customer-provided request IDs and sample logs. Opening ticket and notifying SRE channel #support.
2026-03-10 02:40 - SRE (Marcus Li): Confirmed transient 'conn_throttle' spikes in us-east apigw cluster 14. Sampling gateway traces for request IDs SUP-206789-*.

2026-03-10 03:15 - Customer (CloudNote SRE): Provided curl samples and indicated they saw elevated prefetch activity after a code deploy at 00:30 UTC. Asked if rollout could have affected kv-cache sizing.
2026-03-11 09:20 - Support (Ava Chen): Collected metrics links and asked customer to toggle prefetch to false for narrow repro; customer will try during next maintenance window.
2026-03-12 11:00 - Engineering (Marcus Li): Autoscaler cooldown misconfiguration identified for dedicated pools. Draft PR to adjust scale policy and add backpressure smoothing. See PR linked.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Prefetch wave causes sudden memory pressure in runtimes leading to evictions and worker exits -> upstream 5xx.
Hypothesis 2: API gateway throttling threshold is too aggressive for dedicated pools under connection-churn from prefetch clients, amplifying request failures before the autoscaler can react.
Metric evidence: apigw.conn_throttle_count increased by 320% during 01:18-01:48 UTC windows; kv_prefetch_eviction_rate rose concurrently by 210%.
Logs: runtime worker stderr shows repeated 'KVCache eviction triggered; memory high' and occasional 'terminate: runtime exited with status 137' around spikes.
Trace sampling: full traces show requests queued at gateway layer for ~90-300ms before proxying; some timeouts then surface as 504s.
source.resolution:
Workaround applied (2026-03-12): temporarily reduced apigw aggressive-throttle setting for the customer's dedicated pool and enabled a conservative autoscaler fast-path for prefetch-detected traffic patterns. Customer validated reduction in 5xx bursts during staged verification window.

Fix and rollout plan:
- Engineering to merge PR adjusting dedicated autoscaler cooldown and adding prefetch-aware admission control (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4217). Target deploy: 2026-03-14 canary -> 2026-03-16 full.
- Add metric alert for kv_prefetch_eviction_rate > 3x baseline and apigw.conn_throttle_count > 100/min for dedicated pools.
- Postmortem follow-up assigned to Marcus Li and Priya Kapoor; include runbook updates and config guardrails.

Follow-ups / Action items:
- [ ] Update prefetch runbook with capacity knobs and recommended client-side keep-alive/connection reuse patterns.
- [ ] Validate autoscaler policy across additional dedicated pools in staging.
- [ ] Coordinate with CloudNote Labs on a controlled prefetch rollout after fixes.

Status: mitigation in place; engineering fix in review and scheduled for canary. Monitoring continues.

source.summary: Streaming tool/function output tokens billed on final invoice despite console estimate showing zero
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer reports that streaming requests which include function/tool calls produced console estimates showing 0 tool-output tokens (console estimate column), but the finalized invoice contains token charges attributed to the tool outputs. Impact: LexiHealth reports a $9,200 unexpected charge on their monthly invoice tied to streamed sessions that used function calls for structured data retrieval. Environment: Production, us-east-1, enterprise account with reserved capacity and hosted fallback enabled. This affects streaming chat endpoints where the model returns tool invocations (function calls) and the tool outputs are streamed back as separate chunks.

Expected behavior: Console and estimate API should include a consistent token estimate for tool outputs (either zero if non-billable or the same token estimate used for invoice attribution). Final invoice should not introduce previously-unestimated token charges for tool outputs.

Actual behavior: Console estimate showed zero tool-output tokens for ~1.5M tokens across all tool invocations during the billing window. Final invoice shows ~1.45M billed tokens under a line labeled "tool-invoke-output" with reserved-rate fallback applied.

Customer impact: Revenue-impacting billing dispute, customer is holding renewal request pending resolution.

Attachments: Customer provided sample request IDs and screenshot of console estimate vs invoice lines.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use a streaming chat request to /v1/chat/stream with function_call enabled
2) Have the model return a function call and stream the tool output as separate chunks using the streaming protocol
3) Observe console estimate and /v1/estimates endpoint showing tool-output token count = 0 for these sessions
4) After billing cycle finalization, inspect invoice line items and see billed tokens attributed to 'tool-invoke-output' with nonzero count
logs: Customer provided request ids: p-req-20260301-7a1, p-req-20260304-2f3, p-req-20260307-9b0
Gateway logs (sample): 2026-03-01T14:12:07Z TRACE request p-req-20260301-7a1 stream chunk tool-output bytes=12456 tokens=478
Estimate API sample response (for p-req-20260301-7a1): {"model":"gptx-8k", "estimated_tokens": {"prompt": 512, "completion": 1200, "tool_output": 0}}
Billing postback shows: line_item id li-202603-lexi-tool-output tokens=1450000 rate=hosted-reserve-fallback
investigation_notes: 03-10 10:15 - Support: validated customer provided request IDs and fetched gateway traces; traces show tokenized counts at gateway level for tool-output chunks.

03-10 14:30 - Metering team: initial hypothesis - estimate pipeline ignores post-streamed tool-output tokenization if tool outputs are produced by external tool adapters (post-processing), leading to 0 in console estimate.

03-11 09:02 - Billing team: finalizer job uses ingestion of gateway token events (event-sourced) and included tool-output token events; rate applied based on route at time-of-execution and account fallback settings.

03-11 13:45 - Correlation: estimator uses preflight cost model which receives only model-internal token counts and not post-hook tool-output tokens; billing uses the event stream that includes tool-output tokens emitted after model completion.
03-11 16:00 - Engineering produced a reproducer: streaming tool-output emitted via external adapter produced an extra event with tokens but was not surfaced to estimator API.

03-12 08:20 - Confirmed scope: affects streaming sessions with function_call mode where tool outputs are generated by external adapters and streamed as separate events. Non-streamed (batch) tool outputs are estimated correctly.
comments: 2026-03-10 11:04 - Support (Ava Chen): Thanks for the info. We've pulled the gateway traces and escalated to metering and billing. We'll update within 48 hours.

2026-03-11 09:25 - Metering (Rajat Singh): Reproducer created. The estimate pipeline currently subscribes to model tokenizer events; tool-output events are emitted on a different topic 'tool-stream' and are not included in estimate aggregation. Working on a patch to include tool-stream token counts in estimates.

2026-03-11 15:12 - Billing (Maya Lopez): Billing pipeline is correct: it charges against the canonical event stream. We can provide a provisional credit while estimator fix is developed. Suggest temporary credit equal to billed tool-output tokens for the cycle pending root cause fix and audit.

2026-03-12 09:45 - Customer (LexiHealth - Jordan Park): Appreciate rapid triage. We need an estimated timeline for credit and a fix to prevent recurrence. Renewal decision is on hold until this is resolved.

2026-03-12 12:10 - Support (Liam O'Keefe): Ack. Metering has a PR queued (see related_github_prs). Billing team proposed provisional credit; preparing credit memo draft for $9,200 pending sign-off. Next update EOD 2026-03-12.
source.resolution_notes: Provisionally crediting the billed tool-output tokens for the March cycle pending a permanent fix to the estimator. Engineering to land estimator subscription change to include 'tool-stream' events and add an integration test verifying console estimates match billed events for streaming tool outputs. Billing will issue credit memo after engineering sign-off and audit.
next_steps: 1) Metering: land PR to estimator to subscribe to 'tool-stream' topic and include tool-output token counts in /v1/estimates (owner: Rajat)
2) Engineering: add end-to-end integration test and enable canary for us-east-1 (owner: Priya Desai)
3) Billing: prepare provisional credit memo and hold renewal pause notes until resolution (owner: Maya Lopez)
4) Support: communicate weekly updates to customer and confirm remediation & credit application (owner: Liam O'Keefe)

source.summary: Halogen Energy — fanout stall causing streaming silence, duplicate responses, and invoice exposure (post-incident follow-up)
source.description: Issue summary: During a high-concurrency scheduled batch for Halogen Energy (enterprise, prod us-east), the request fanout path experienced a sustained stall resulting in partial streaming responses followed by duplicate completions. Impact: 11% of in-flight user sessions experienced either (a) a streaming silence lasting 6–18s before a replayed completion, or (b) duplicate completions delivered after client-side retry. Billing exposure: duplicate tokens were recorded for 0.8% of requests between 2026-03-10T09:42Z and 2026-03-10T10:26Z. User-visible effect: client-facing SDKs reported truncated transcripts and duplicated final messages; several Halogen automated workflows misinterpreted duplicates and triggered payment processing retrials. Environment: production, us-east, Dedicated pool: ded-pool-us-east-3, routing policy: capacity-first with multipath fanout to 3 backends. Time window: 2026-03-10T09:42Z — 2026-03-10T10:26Z. Steps to reproduce: 1) Replay customer traffic (provided capture) through multipath fanout; 2) Observe long tail in request-to-first-byte and duplicated completions when scheduler replays to alternate backend; 3) Reproduce with stress harness targeting redwood-xl-quantized at sequence lengths >800 tokens. Observed behavior: stalled mux in front of streaming worker pools (mux did not surface 5xx, but stopped forwarding chunks), scheduler retried to alternate node which completed the request again; dedup headers were inconsistently applied during the transient. Temporary mitigation performed: emergency rollback of a recent scheduler change (PR 427) and temporary reduction of fanout concurrency to 1 per request (via routing policy override).
comments: 2026-03-10 09:46 — Priya Patel (Support): Acknowledged with Halogen. Opened #support-halogen and escalated to SRE. Notified account exec (Lena Novak).

2026-03-10 09:55 — Miguel Torres (SRE): Correlated with elevated mux queue depth on ded-pool-us-east-3; investigating scheduler retry storm. See dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/serving/fanout-metrics?orgId=1&var-pool=ded-pool-us-east-3
2026-03-10 10:03 — Samir Gupta (Eng): Rolled back scheduler PR 427 to stop aggressive speculative retries; applying an immediate config change to set max_fanout=1 for affected customer route.
2026-03-10 10:20 — Halogen contact (Jordan Reyes): Customer reports streaming resumed for most sessions; asked for a timeline and credit for duplicate charges.
2026-03-10 11:02 — Billing Ops (No assignee yet): Requested list of impacted request IDs for manual credit evaluation. Linked: SUP-214495 for billing reconciliation.

2026-03-11 08:45 — Samir Gupta (Eng): Confirmed root cause hypothesis: race between mux backpressure and scheduler speculative retry path causing double-completion when dedup metadata dropped on fallback path. Working on a patch to enforce dedup token propagation and add an end-to-end test.
2026-03-12 14:03 — Ava Chen (Support): Sent customer final incident summary and proposed credit: one week of committed capacity waived from next invoice. Customer accepted on 2026-03-12 16:10.
2026-03-12 16:20 — Samir Gupta (Eng): Deployed patch to mux and scheduler (PRs 1382 + 1430). Monitoring shows no reoccurrence in 48h window on ded-pool-us-east-3. Closing as Resolved.
investigation_notes: Initial signal: elevated request-to-first-byte and mux queue depth spike at 09:42 correlated with a small increase in 5xx flank but not a full outage.

Mux logs show sudden increase in first-chunk latency followed by a silent connection close in ~2.3% of cases; connection close path did not emit a 5xx to the client because the mux acknowledged the stream then lost upstream chunks.

Scheduler change (PR 427) introduced a speculative retry flow that triggers when per-backend p99 latency > 250ms; under mux backpressure this caused the system to start a fallback path while the original upstream later resumed and completed — dedup metadata was not copied to the fallback request in every code path.

Temporal sequence: mux backpressure -> scheduler starts speculative retry -> fallback node completes (with dedup header missing) -> original upstream finishes and completes with dedup header -> client receives duplicate completions and both are billed.

Post-deploy checks: end-to-end tests against replicated ded-pool reproduced duplicate completions until dedup header propagation bug fixed. Targeted unit tests added to ensure header propagation and integration test added to CI harness.
Telemetry: billing logs exported to S3 path s3://redwood-incident-exports/INC-2026-03-10-07/; sample request ids appended in related_confluence page.
resolution: Short-term: Rolled back scheduler PR 427 and set routing override max_fanout=1 for Halogen Energy route to stop speculative retries (2026-03-10T10:03Z).

Medium-term: Deployed patch that ensures dedup metadata is copied to any scheduler-initiated fallback path and added strict validation in mux to surface upstream chunk loss as explicit 5xx (PRs 1382, 1430 deployed 2026-03-12).

Long-term: Add canary gating for speculative retry config changes, expand synthetic coverage for streaming and fanout scenarios, and add billing reconciliation job to detect duplicate-token anomalies within 24h so credits can be automated.

Customer remediation: Issued billing credit equivalent to one week of committed capacity (accepted 2026-03-12). Engineering will provide a follow-up runbook for Halogen on recommended routing policy settings for heavy long-sequence workloads.
Follow-up tasks: INT-214520 (internal test harness improvements), SUP-214495 (billing reconciliation), and a postmortem action tracker on Confluence (link in related_confluence_pages).
postmortem_followups: Action item 1: Create an end-to-end stress test for mux + scheduler speculative retry that runs nightly (owner: Samir, due: 2026-03-26).
Action item 2: Implement automated billing-delta detector to alert on duplicate-token spikes (owner: Billing Ops, due: 2026-04-02).
Action item 3: Update canary policy to block speculative-retry config changes unless latency improvement > 15% on canary (owner: Release Eng, due: 2026-03-30).

source.summary: session-kv-lease-sticky-causing-resumed-conversation-p99-latency-spike
source.description:
Issue summary: ClearHealth reported a series of resumed conversational requests (user resumes session after short disconnect) that observe elevated tail latency (p99 ~4.2s) and occasional timeouts, compared to normal p99 ~250ms. This occurs after sessions have been inactive for ~30-90s. Impact: user-facing latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts causing degraded UX in production. Behavior is reproducible for certain session IDs and high-frequency resume patterns.

Context: This ticket was opened by Support after customer escalation. Initial triage indicates the serving runtime enters a blocking cold-path when a session key is considered "leased" by another backend shard due to a lease state that does not expire promptly. The behavior started after the kvcache lease change in release v2.18.
source.impact: Customer-visible p99 latency increase to ~4s for resumed conversational turns; ~0.8% request timeout rate for affected sessions during the window. Affects ClearHealth production in us-east and may affect other enterprise customers using session pinning/lease feature.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer flow: create a long-lived chat session, send several turns until KV caching warm (baseline p99 ~200ms)
2) Allow session to be idle for 30-90 seconds
3) Resume conversation with a short prompt (identical to prior recent prompt), observe p99 latency spike and occasional 504
4) Reproduce by replaying traffic through our proxy with the same session_id and headers; behavior crops up intermittently for a subset of session_ids
logs: 2026-03-10T14:22:11Z [serving-runtime] session_id=ch-9a2 lease_check: found lease owner shard-7, lease_expiry=1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (infinite)
2026-03-10T14:22:12Z [kvcache] session_id=ch-9a2 warmup: blocking rehydrate sync (caller blocked for 3.9s)
2026-03-10T14:22:12Z [api-gateway] request_id=... status=504 latency=4207ms upstream_error=timeout
comments: 2026-03-10 - Priya Nair (Support): Customer reports resumed chat p99 ~4s after 30-60s idle. They see 504s at ~1% of resumed attempts. Attached sample request_ids and session ids.
2026-03-10 - Diego Morales (SRE): Initial hypothesis: lease TTL not expiring for sessions under error path introduced last week. Scheduling quick investigation on kvcache lease state metrics.
2026-03-11 - Priya Nair (Support): Customer confirmed that restarting the client (which creates a new session_id) avoids the issue temporarily.
2026-03-11 - Diego Morales (SRE): Observed elevated metric sticky_lease_count on shard-7 and shard-12 starting around 2026-03-10T02:00Z, coinciding with deployment of kvcache change (commit abc123).
investigation_notes: Hypothesis A: Lease TTL regressed to 0 (infinite) for leases that experienced an RPC error during lease refresh, leaving keys marked as 'leased' indefinitely. This causes a new request to perform synchronous rehydrate under mutex, causing blocking latency.

Hypothesis B: Lease owner shard experiences GC pause and does not renew leases; other shards treat lease as active and synchronously attempt to take ownership using blocking backoff path introduced in v2.18.

Data: metric sticky_lease_count spiked from 0.02% -> 0.9% of session keys between 2026-03-10 02:00Z and 06:00Z. Correlates with increased p99 on serving-runtime for resumed sessions. Heap and GC traces on shard-7 show recurring short STW pauses ~200-400ms at times of spike.

Reproduction attempts: Ran a traffic replay tool that simulates 10k resumed-session requests across 5 shards; reproduced p99 spike only when replay included sessions that hit shards with sticky_lease_count > 0.6%.
Quick mitigation tested: Manually triggered eviction on shard-7 kvcache entries for 12 sample session_ids; resumed requests for those sessions returned to baseline latency.

Related artifacts: Slack thread #support link: https://slack.redwood/support/CK12345; Perf dashboard: https://grafana.redwood/s/dash/kv-lease; Erroring commit: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving/commit/abc123
source.resolution_notes: Workaround applied: manual eviction script run against affected shards (shard-7, shard-12) to clear sticky leases for impacted sessions; instructed customer on session-safety workaround: rotate session_id when possible. Root cause and permanent fix: Reverted lease behavior change from commit abc123 that left leases non-expiring on RPC failure. Deployed patch v2.18.1 that restores lease fallback TTL and adds a non-blocking rehydrate path (async warmup) so callers are not blocked. Added alerting for sticky_lease_count > 0.2% and a runbook to forcibly evict leases if alert fires. Follow-ups: create a test harness to simulate RPC error paths on lease refresh and add unit tests for lease expiry, update changelog and deployment notes. Customer-facing follow-up: support will send an incident summary and timeline to ClearHealth and schedule a 1:1 with their SRE in the next 48 hours.

title: session-kv-lease-sticky-causing-resumed-conversation-p99-latency-spike
preview:
Issue summary: ClearHealth reported a series of resumed conversational requests (user resumes session after short disconnect) that observe elevated tail latency (p99 ~4.2s) and occasional timeouts, compared to normal p99 ~250ms. This occurs after sessions have been inactive for ~30-90s. Impact: user-facing latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts causing degraded UX in production. Behavior is reproducible for certain session IDs and high-frequency resume patterns.

Context: This ticket was opened by Support after customer escalation. Initial triage indicates the serving runtime enters a blocking cold-path when a session key is considered "leased" by another backend shard due to a lease state that does not expire promptly. The behavior started after the kvcache lease change in release v2.18.

The behavior started after the kvcache lease change in release v2.18.

source.summary: On-demand delta CSV export with UTC normalization, rotating MD5 manifest, and proof-of-exclusion for deleted records
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer requests a one-off delta export of their tenant activity as a CSV with strict UTC timestamp normalization, a rotating MD5 manifest for each batch file, and a machine-verifiable 'proof-of-exclusion' artifact listing records deleted since the last snapshot. They need the package for a regulatory evidence submission and require deterministic ordering and replayability.

Requirements: (1) CSV delta with canonical UTC timestamps and ISO8601 formatting; (2) each batch file accompanied by an MD5 manifest entry and a signed manifest file (HMAC-SHA256 with their provided per-tenant key); (3) a separate "proof-of-exclusion" file: a compact list of deleted record IDs with per-ID deletion-tombstone hashes and a top-level Merkle-root signed by Redwood to prove non-inclusion in prior snapshots; (4) resume tokens and offsets for replay; (5) timezone normalization mapping notes and a field-translation glossary in the bundle.

Why unique: Customer explicitly needs evidence that certain records were omitted (deleted) and wants cryptographic proof to present to compliance that deletions are auditable and non-reversible in the delivered export.
source.impact: Customer cannot complete regulatory submission without per-record deletion attestation. Blocking legal review and audit deadline for FinCloud Bank. High urgency because compliance audit window closes in 10 calendar days.
source.steps_to_reproduce: N/A - this is a data/evidence export request rather than a bug. Repro steps for generation: 1) identify tenant id and last delivered offset; 2) run delta exporter with --normalize-timestamps=UTC --batch-size=10000; 3) generate per-batch MD5s and aggregate into signed manifest; 4) produce deletion tombstone list and compute per-id hash and Merkle root; 5) sign manifest and Merkle root using Redwood export signing key; 6) deliver bundle to customer's SFTP endpoint.
logs_and_artifacts: Example CSV sample attached (redacted) in ticket comments
Customer-provided tenant HMAC key: (redacted) provided via secure form
comments: 2026-03-10 09:12 - Ava Chen (Customer): Hi Support - We need a delta export for tenant fincloud-prod with proofs for deletions. Audit window closes 2026-03-20. Please confirm format and delivery options (SFTP preferred).

2026-03-10 11:05 - Diego Morales (Support): Acknowledged. Can you confirm the last export offset (timestamp) you received? Also we support HMAC-signed manifests; will you provide a per-tenant HMAC key or do you want Redwood to sign and provide a verifiable certificate?

2026-03-10 13:47 - FinCloud (Customer): We have last offset token: off_2026_02_25_17_03. We will provide tenant HMAC key via secure upload. Proof-of-exclusion should be verifiable by auditors (Merkle root acceptable).

2026-03-11 08:34 - Diego Morales (Support): Internal note -> pinging infra: need export job to include deletion-tombstones, compute per-id SHA256 hash, and assemble Merkle tree. Also need short-lived signed manifest generation using export-signing key (sig-v1). Assign to Eng export run team.

2026-03-11 14:20 - Nora Patel (Eng - Export Run): Investigating feasibility. Our current delta exporter can include tombstones but does not produce Merkle roots. Estimate 2 workdays to add Merkle compute and signed-manifest step as a one-off pipeline change. Will propose a service account to sign the manifest and provide a verification public key.

2026-03-12 10:02 - Diego Morales (Support): Customer updated: delivery window acceptable for 2026-03-16 if we can produce signed artifacts. Asked Eng for timeline and temporary workarounds (e.g., provide per-id hashed tombstone list and sign the file with our PGP key).
investigation_notes: Confirmed: existing export pipeline supports per-record tombstones and resume tokens. It does MD5 per-file but does not currently compute Merkle roots or per-id hashes in the artifact output.
Hypothesis: implement Merkle root generation as a post-processing step on the tombstone list; sign Merkle root using export-signing-key (HSM-backed) and include signature file alongside manifest.

Partial finding: MD5 manifest currently computed by batch_writer service; we can extend batch_writer to also emit a tombstone-hash file and an iterator for Merkle building. Requires adding streaming Merkle library to exporter.
Security review note: proposed signing must use credentials stored in export-service KMS scope; require a short-lived delegation and audit log entry. Coordinate with Security for key-use approval.
next_steps: Obtain customer HMAC key via secure form and verify SHA256 fingerprint.
Engineering to implement Merkle root generation as one-off patch and run export in staging for verification.
Security to approve export-signing key use and provide public verification certificate to FinCloud.
Schedule delivery to customer's SFTP endpoint and confirm receipt; provide verification instructions for auditors.
source.owner_notes: This ticket requires cross-team coordination: Support to own customer comms, Eng to implement Merkle signing, Security to approve keys, and Ops to run the export. Marking high-touch and will open an internal INT ticket for key delegation if approved.

source.summary: Tool acknowledgement lost after client-side backpressure causing ambiguous replay and side-effect uncertainty
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer reports that when their client applies aggressive client-side backpressure (pause/resume on the streaming socket) while the SDK/edge is in the middle of a function/tool call, the tool acknowledgement gets lost. Impact: downstream systems observe either duplicate side-effects after replay or never-observed confirmations, leading to billing ambiguity and customer data inconsistencies.

Environment: Prod, dedicated deployment for Nimbus Health in us-east. Python SDK 1.8.2 used from a serverless worker behind an in-house proxy.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start long-lived streaming session via Python SDK to call external tool via function-calling feature.
2) Trigger a tool execution that emits a confirmation webhook synchronously from the tool handler.
3) Simulate client backpressure by pausing the TCP read loop for ~500-1500ms intermittently (server in CPU-constrained environment).
4) Resume the stream; SDK reconnects/flows may replay inflight events.

Expected behavior: SDK/edge should preserve tool acknowledgement idempotently and surface a single confirmed execution to the customer; replays should be deduplicated by idempotency keys.

Actual behavior: In ~30% of runs, the webhook confirmation is not recorded in the session state; later replay either re-invokes the tool (duplicate side-effect) or the system marks the execution as unknown.

Logs/observations:
- Edge logs show 'ack-window-timeout' correlated with paused reads at timestamp ranges.
- Kinesis traces show the outbound webhook was accepted (200 OK) but the confirmation event did not reach session persistence due to a write timeout to Redis during the pause window.
- Customer observed two charges for the same tool call in billing logs for one replayed invocation.

Customer impact: Potential duplicate side-effects and billing disputes. Requires mitigation guidance and a reliable long-term fix.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis A: Client-side pause causes edge to drop in-flight ACK update RPCs to session-store (Redis) due to connection pooling exhaustion under backpressure.
Hypothesis B: SDK replays inflight messages without strictly replaying tool confirmation idempotency headers leading to new invocations.
Ran a repro in staging with identical pause pattern; observed Redis write timeouts (avg 1200ms) and edge logs 'inflight-ack-dropped'.
Found related code path in streaming coordinator where confirmation propagation is async and not gated behind durable write acknowledgement.
Short-term mitigation: recommend customer set SDK client to use 'ack_mode: confirm_before_close' and increase socket-read buffer to avoid pausing the stream.
Also suggested configuring dedicated deployment autoscaling to avoid CPU starvation during proxy bursts.
Follow-up: engineering to add sync-level persistence for tool confirmations or a confirmation-resend handshake on resume.
comments: 2026-03-10 | Ava Chen: Captured initial report from Nimbus Health. Customer provided sample repro script and billing trace. Marked P1 due to billing impact.
2026-03-10 | Rohan Patel (Support): Acknowledged and requested full SDK logs and proxy config. Also asked for precise timing of pause/resume.
2026-03-11 | Nimbus Health (Customer): Uploaded repro script showing pause intervals and sample webhook server logs (200 responses).
2026-03-11 | Linh Nguyen (SRE): Confirmed Redis timeouts in our staging repro. Suggested increasing connection pool and adding telemetry for ack-write-failures.
2026-03-12 | Rohan Patel (Support): Provided temporary workaround to customer: enable 'confirm_before_close' and set env var REDWOOD_STREAM_READ_TIMEOUT=3000. Customer to test and report.
2026-03-12 | Nimbus Health (Customer): Applied workaround; initial verification shows no duplicate charges in two-hour test window.
source.resolution_notes: Temporary workaround recommended and validated by customer: enable client-side 'confirm_before_close' mode and increase stream read timeout; also advised scaling edge proxy and increasing Redis connection pool. Engineering to implement durable confirmation persistence and a resume-handshake to ensure confirmations are reconciled on reconnect. Track fix in runtime PR #4021 and follow up with customer once deployed to dedicated cluster.

source.summary: Reserved pool throughput drop from rapid tenant scatter causing slot thrash and GPU allocation shortfalls
source.description: Issue summary: During the customer's recurring midday window we observed a sustained p95/p99 throughput degradation for NovaDocs' reserved pool that coincided with a large number of short-lived tenant connections spreading across many slots (tenant scatter). Impact: customer requests experience 3x p99 increase and burst 429/RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED for batched routes, causing downstream timeouts for user-facing documents ingestion pipelines. Environment: production reserved pool (dedicated) in us-east. Observed starting 2026-03-10 11:40 UTC. Steps to reproduce: 1) Replay customer traffic (small, high-concurrency short-streams) into reserved pool using synthetic tenant generator 2) Observe slot churn and repeated small allocations 3) Watch autoscaler not react quickly due to many ephemeral allocations and scheduler fragmentation Observed behavior: rapid slot attach/detach, high scheduler RPC latencies, GPU allocation failures, increased queue length and autoscaler hysteresis. Expected behavior: pool should absorb tenant short-stream spikes without sustained p99 throughput degradation; autoscaler should scale smoothly or fall back to higher-capacity routing. Logs/metrics: see linked dashboards. Key error signatures include repeated "attach-timeout" and "slot-evict" traces plus GPU attach failures with low free-HBM headroom. Customer impact: production ingestion jobs delayed and some documents dropped after retries. Temporary mitigation requested by customer: route small streams to fallback shared pool until resolved.
comments: 2026-03-10 11:55 - Ava Chen (Support): Initial triage completed; capturing metrics and requested sample request IDs from customer.
2026-03-10 12:15 - Diego Morales (SRE): Observed repeating attach-timeout pattern on pool 'reserved-us-east-42' correlated with scheduler heartbeat spikes. Attaching top-of-stack logs to ticket.
2026-03-10 12:45 - Customer (NovaDocs): Confirmed we saw increased 429s between 11:40-12:05 UTC; sent anonymized request traces and traffic profile.

2026-03-10 13:10 - Diego Morales (SRE): Applied short-term mitigation: enabled customer fallback routing to shared low-latency pool and temporarily raised pool burst limit for 15 minutes; customer reports partial improvement.

2026-03-11 09:20 - Runtime Eng (Priya Rao): Noted KV-cache growth not implicated; but scheduler log shows many tiny allocation batches (avg <300 tokens) leading to slot thrash. Adding hypothesis to investigation notes.
2026-03-12 08:05 - Diego Morales (SRE): Scaling policy change proposed: increase attach-grace-period and add aggregation window for ephemeral short streams. Awaiting review from autoscaler owners.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis A: Tenant scatter (many short-lived streams spread across slots) causing slot-level fragmentation; autoscaler decision logic misclassifies signal as noise leading to delayed scale-up.
Hypothesis B: A subset of tenants triggering frequent prefetch/pin cycles on GPUs leading to HBM pressure and attach failures; observed small memory spikes at attach time.
Telemetry: scheduler RPC latency spikes aligned with p99 increases; attach/release rate for slots jumped from baseline ~6/min to ~250/min for the affected pool between 11:37-11:50 UTC.
Dashboards: Reserved pool heatmap, attach/release rate, GPU HBM headroom, autoscaler decisions timeline, request queue length snapshots (links in related_confluence_pages).
Reproduction: Synthetic short-stream runner reproduced slot thrash at 2x nominal concurrency; when aggregation window added (300ms) the system maintained p95 latency at baseline in test cluster.
Temporary workaround validated: routing short streams to shared pool reduced attach churn and p99 latency by ~60% for customer workload during window.
resolution: Workaround applied: Tenant fallback to shared pool enabled for NovaDocs for the affected windows. Burst limit temporarily increased; rollout monitored.

Mitigation steps planned: 1) Autoscaler owners to implement short-stream aggregation window and extend attach-grace-period to reduce slot thrash; 2) Scheduler to surface attach-rate anomalies as an alertable signal; 3) Add heuristics to detect tenant scatter patterns and recommend routing changes automatically.

Permanent fix: Deploy autoscaler PR that incorporates ephemeral-stream aggregation and a dynamic attach backoff (see related_github_prs). Target rollout to prod in two sprints after QA (ETA 2026-03-26).
Customer follow-up: CS to coordinate a scheduled maintenance window for controlled testing of the autoscaler change with NovaDocs, and provide post-change monitoring report.

Post-incident tasks: Create runbook entry for 'reserved pool tenant scatter' (owner: Platform QOE), add alert for attach/release rate > 10x baseline per pool, and schedule a postmortem with involved teams.

source.summary: Intermittent websocket half-close after corporate TLS rekey -> trailing 'done' envelope never arrives
source.description: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent websocket sessions that appear to close from the server side after a TLS rekey performed by their corporate TLS-inspecting proxy. The stream is truncated: the final JSON envelope with status/done metadata is never received, leaving the client waiting indefinitely. Impact: Multiple production users at Acme AI Labs experience truncated generations and must reissue requests; affects business-critical inference pipelines. Observed first at ~2026-03-09 22:15 PST and recurred several times. Environment: prod cluster behind ALB -> CloudFront -> corporate TLS-inspection -> customer client. No code changes on Redwood side around times of first occurrence.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer establishes long-running websocket streaming request against /v1/streams with model redwood-1-large. 2) While the model is streaming a ~4000-token completion, customer network triggers TLS session renegotiation / rekey (corporate proxy performs active MITM). 3) Connection experiences a short TCP-level close followed by client reconnect; client tries to resume but never receives final 'done' envelope or terminal metadata. 4) Repeating with curl/wscat behind same proxy triggers the same truncated stream ~30% of runs. Reproducer cURL/websocket notes included in logs.
source.logs:
[2026-03-09T22:15:12.457Z] conn=abcd1234 ws-session open token=redacted
[2026-03-09T22:17:45.791Z] upstream-proxy: TLS_REKEY observed, client-ip=10.8.1.24
[2026-03-09T22:17:45.892Z] conn=abcd1234 ws half-close detected (FIN from client-path)
[2026-03-09T22:17:46.003Z] worker=svc-23 trace=tr-9987 last-kv-prime=0x7f3a
[2026-03-09T22:17:46.004Z] choice-stream: flushed tail fragments but no terminal-envelope written
[2026-03-09T22:18:05.112Z] client reconnected and attempted resume with resume_id=rc-556
[2026-03-09T22:18:05.113Z] resume: snapshot mismatch, refusing resume -> fallback served but terminal metadata absent
[customer-provided] client-js log: "received 3 partial choices, no final event, ws.onclose code=1006"
investigation_notes: Initial analysis (Support): pattern correlates to external TLS rekey events; connection transitions show half-closed state on server logs.
SRE step: Captured pcap from edge node and confirmed short TCP FIN followed by immediate SYN from same client IP; no backend worker crash seen.

Hypothesis: Proxy-inserted TLS rekey modifies client TCP/TLS state in a way that our edge/keepalive handling treats as half-close and does not queue or persist terminal envelope to resume snapshot. Resume path then rejects resume due to ephemeral snapshot mismatch.
Reproducer: Engineering reproduced by running corporate TLS-inspection appliance (testbox) that forces TLS rekey mid-stream; observed identical symptom in ~4/10 runs.

Edge behavior detail: When FIN arrives while last chunk is buffered but not flushed to persistent stream-snapshot, the server sets session state to HALF_CLOSED and GC probes remove pending terminal metadata after 10s. This window aligns with observed data loss.
source.proposed_next_steps:
- Short term workaround: advise customer to disable mid-stream TLS rekey/rewrite on corp proxy for inference traffic or mark Redwood endpoints in proxy bypass list.
- Medium term: implement atomic flush of terminal envelope to durable snapshot before transitioning session to HALF_CLOSED; add aggressive snapshot checkpoint on any FIN/close path.
- Add metric/trace to alert when session enters HALF_CLOSED with non-flushed-terminal for >5s.
- Run PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1219 to staging with instrumentation and reproduce under testbox.
source.resolution_notes: (Pending) No customer-visible resolution yet. Customer accepted bypass workaround to unblock pipelines; engineering own implementation plan queued.
comments: 2026-03-10 08:12 - Maya Thompson (Support): Opened with customer-provided logs and a minimal repro. Customer says 12% of requests on long-running chats see truncated end.

2026-03-10 09:03 - Ethan Park (SRE): Confirmed logs show FIN before terminal-envelope; collecting pcap from edge-03 for deeper analysis. Posted findings to #support and #sre (link: slack://channels/support).

2026-03-10 11:25 - Lina Gomez (Customer): We ran an immediate test disabling TLS inspection for *.redwood.ai and saw no truncated streams during a 2h window. We'd prefer server-side fix but applying bypass is acceptable short term.

2026-03-11 14:40 - Arun Patel (Infra): Reproducer built with test TLS-inspector box. Reproduced 3/10. Found race where buffer flush wins vs. FIN timing; created PR with checkpoint flush (see related_github_prs).

2026-03-12 10:02 - Ethan Park (SRE): Rolling 'instrumented snapshot' change to staging for soak. Added alert rule to paging when half-closed sessions >5s with pending-terminal. Assigning owner for rollout: @Arun.
2026-03-12 12:30 - Support (Maya): Updated customer that we have a staged patch and recommended bypass until fix is deployed. Customer acknowledged and set proxy bypass for Redwood endpoints.

title: Intermittent websocket half-close after corporate TLS rekey -> trailing 'done' envelope never arrives
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent websocket sessions that appear to close from the server side after a TLS rekey performed by their corporate TLS-inspecting proxy. The stream is truncated: the final JSON envelope with status/done metadata is never received, leaving the client waiting indefinitely. Impact: Multiple production users at Acme AI Labs experience truncated generations and must reissue requests; affects business-critical inference pipelines. Observed first at ~2026-03-09 22:15 PST and recurred several times. Environment: prod cluster behind ALB -> CloudFront -> corporate TLS-inspection -> customer client. No code changes on Redwood side around times of first occurrence.

source.summary: Long-lived keepalive connections from small-stream clients holding GPU slots causing regional capacity squeeze
source.description:
Issue summary:
BrightForms is reporting intermittent throughput collapse for their dedicated pool in us-east starting 2026-03-10 11:40 UTC. Symptoms align with a regional GPU supply squeeze where many GPUs appear allocated but traffic shows a high proportion of very short inference streams with persistent TCP/TLS keepalive.

Impact:
- Customer-facing routes (real-time chat) p95/p99 latency sharply increased; observed request queues and autoscaler unable to reclaim GPUs quickly.
- Other tenants in the same reserved cluster experienced degraded throughput and increased fallbacks to lower-cap models.
- Business impact: customer reported degraded UX for production traffic across multiple endpoints.

Timeline & quick facts:
- 2026-03-10 11:38 UTC: BrightForms first observed degraded latency and opened a support channel.
- 2026-03-10 11:45 UTC: Support identified elevated "active_slot_count" with many connections showing <50ms compute but socket persisted >60s.
- 2026-03-10 12:05 UTC: Autoscaler attempted to add GPUs but allocation failed due to shortfall in the region (ephemeral shortage reported by infra).

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens long-lived streaming connection with frequent keepalive pings but sends only occasional short inference calls (1-5 tokens).
2) Serving runtime pins a GPU slot for the connection to maintain KV/cache affinity.
3) Connection remains open for minutes; batcher cannot efficiently combine tiny requests into useful batches.
4) When many clients behave similarly, GPU slot utilization becomes fragmented: many slots show low compute duty but remain reserved.

Observed metrics/logs (snippets):
- allocator metrics: "active_slots: 182 / 200", "avg_slot_utilization: 0.12"
- serving logs (sample): "INFO 2026-03-10T11:46:21Z connection_id=xyz keepalive=62s last_compute=0.043s pinned_gpu=gpu-17"
- autoscaler: "scale_attempt=1 -> fail: insufficient_available_gpus(region=us-east)"
- kernel-level: no OOMs; NVLink utilization low; PCIe spikes correlate with background snapshotter in other cluster shards.

Hypothesis:
- Not classic noisy tenant heavy compute: instead small-streams + long connections are causing slot linger (affinity retention) which fragments capacity and prevents autoscaler from recovering effective throughput.
- Combined with a short-term regional provisioning lag and a background snapshotter IO wave (seen in other shards), this amplified the impact.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- Support signaled customer to reduce client keepalive to 15s where possible and to batch short requests client-side (temporary).
- On our side SRE applied a short-lived aggressive reclaim heuristic on the cluster (60s idle compute -> allow slot relinquish). This recovered ~30 GPUs back to the pool but needs careful rollback testing.

Next steps (planned):
- Short window: monitor reclaimed capacity metrics (assigned to SRE, see comments).
- Medium: add a scheduler feature flag to allow opportunistic slot preemption for connections with low compute density after a configurable grace period.
- Long: instrument per-connection compute duty cycle and expose it to console for customers to diagnose keepalive-heavy clients.

Requested from customer:
- Example client connection logs (tcp dumps / client-side timestamps) and sample session IDs to correlate.
- Whether they can apply client-side batching or reduce keepalive interval as a workaround.
comments: 2026-03-10 11:50 - Ethan Park (Support): Acknowledged issue. Collecting allocator and serving runtime traces. Asked BrightForms for example connection IDs and client logs.
2026-03-10 12:03 - Aisha Kaur (Support): Observed slot utilization fragmentation; forwarded initial findings to SRE channel #sre-dedicated-us-east.

2026-03-10 12:16 - Lina Morales (SRE): Ran a targeted reclaim job with conservative thresholds; we recovered ~30 GPUs but saw transient reconnections on some customers. Reclaim job is paused pending validation.
2026-03-10 12:42 - BrightForms (Customer): Provided three session traces and confirmed they can try lowering keepalive to 20s within a maintenance window.

2026-03-11 09:05 - Ethan Park (Support): Scheduled a small canary for opportunistic preemption feature under feature-flag on a single pool (INT-1122). Linking PR for allocator change: https://github.com/redwood-inference/allocator/pull/421

2026-03-12 08:30 - SRE (Investigation): Hypothesis updated: background snapshotter IO correlated with allocator sluggishness; need to coordinate with infra to reduce concurrent snapshot windows. Created follow-up INT ticket for snapshotter cadence adjustment.
2026-03-12 12:00 - Customer Success (CS): Coordinating a BrightForms maintenance window to validate client keepalive reduction and monitor improvements.

2026-03-12 13:15 - Ethan Park (Support): Marking ticket In Progress while we validate reclaimed capacity stabilization. No final resolution yet; customer notified of next maintenance window on 2026-03-13 02:00 UTC.
investigation_notes: Checked allocator logs: fragmentation metric increased from 0.08 -> 0.37 between 11:30-11:50 UTC.
KV-cache pin counts are normal; this is not because of long context usage but connection lifetime.
Autoscaler attempted to spin up 10 GPUs but region reported supply shortfall; coordination with infra needed to confirm temporary capacity dip in us-east.
Reclaim heuristic had side effects: observed a ~1.5% error-rate bump for short-lived clients during the reclaim; recommend canary before broad rollout.
Proposed config: idle_compute_threshold=60s; min_preemption_interval=300s; expose flag as allocator.oom_preempt=false by default for now.

source.summary: SSE -> WebSocket upgrade race leads to truncated final chunks and missing attachment URL
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customers see a truncated final output and missing attachment URL when the client performs an SSE stream that upgrades to WebSocket mid-response. The last few tokens (often the attachment link or final punctuation) are dropped and the stream terminates without the terminal metadata envelope.

Impact:
- KiteNote (enterprise) reports 12% of long-running generation requests lost last-line attachments during heavy traffic windows.
- Affects production traffic in us-west; some artifacts seen in us-east during cross-region failover.

Environment:
prod - us-west, api gateway behind ALB -> edge proxy cluster -> serving-runtime pool. Customer using SDK-js v1.2.4 with automatic SSE-to-WS upgrade enabled.

Expected behavior:
When a client upgrades an open SSE stream to a WebSocket (preferred for bi-directional control), the server should deliver remaining buffered tokens and the final metadata envelope ("event: done" + attachments list) exactly once.

Actual behavior:
During an upgrade window the stream sometimes closes with a partial final chunk; the JSON/metadata envelope for attachments is missing or arrives but with empty attachments array. The socket on the client shows a 101 upgrade then a FIN shortly after with no more data.

Attachments/Logs referenced below.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Start a long-generation SSE request using SDK-js v1.2.4 with streaming upgradable set to true.
2) After ~8-12 seconds of token stream, trigger client to proactively upgrade the connection to WebSocket (client does upgrade handshake).
3) Observe server behavior under moderate load (50-100 concurrent streams) and a behind-ALB probe cycling every 30s.
4) Look at final tokens & attachment metadata on client; compare to server logs for that request id.
logs: [2026-03-10T17:45:22Z] ingress: conn_id=abc123 accept from 10.12.0.45, proto=sse
[2026-03-10T17:45:30Z] gateway: request_id=req-7f2 upgrade_to_ws=true -> proxied 101 to edge-proxy/node-3
[2026-03-10T17:45:31Z] edge-proxy/node-3: forwarded 101, observed FIN from client-side peer within 200ms
[2026-03-10T17:45:31Z] serving-runtime/slot-22: finished stream early: last_buffered_tokens=18, final_metadata_sent=false
[2026-03-10T17:45:31Z] tracer: span=req-7f2 tags={upgrade_race:true, lb_probe_cycle:1}
investigation_notes: Checked ALB/edge proxy logs: pattern shows upgrade attempts collocated with ALB health-check spikes (30s) which sometimes trigger a short connection rebind.
Packet captures show client sends WS upgrade and server replies 101; a TCP FIN arrives from upstream peer within ~150-400ms and stream close occurs before final metadata flush completes.

Reproduced locally with a synthetic ALB probe: when probe causes ephemeral socket reassign, the server's async flush path does not guarantee metadata envelope persistence across the upgrade handshake boundary.

Hypothesis: race between upgrade handshake completion and runtime's final-buffer flush; on fast upgrade + brief NAT/ALB rebind the runtime treats connection as half-closed and drops post-upgrade flush tasks.
Observed correlation with SDK-js v1.2.4 behavior: client performs aggressive immediate WS upgrade instead of waiting for server confirmation of final envelope; earlier SDKs less susceptible.

Search for similar regressions: linked SUP-100275 (early-termination-onflight-batched-ws-handshake) suggests related handling but this appears distinct (attachment metadata lost rather than prefix replay).
comments: Ava Chen (Support) [2026-03-10T18:05Z]: Customer provided sample request_id=req-7f2 and a short screen capture showing the final attachment URL missing. Customer reports ~12% failure during 09:00-10:30 PST window.

Diego Martinez (SRE) [2026-03-10T18:40Z]: Captured tcpdump and proxied logs. Noted fast FIN after 101; putting service in debug mode for next repro window. Temporarily disabled upgrade for KiteNote to mitigate.

Priya Nair (Runtime) [2026-03-11T09:12Z]: Reproduced in staging with simulated ALB rebind. Filing kernel-level order maintenance issue and drafting a fix to make final-metadata flush transactional across upgrade boundary. PR TBD.
KiteNote (Customer) [2026-03-11T14:20Z]: Thanks for the quick mitigation (forcing SSE-only). Can you estimate ETA for fix? This impacts our attachments reliability and downstream indexing.

Diego Martinez (SRE) [2026-03-12T08:00Z]: Workaround validated. Patch skeleton created: ensure final-metadata flush is persisted to connection buffer before switching upgrade state. POC PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/4278
source.resolution: Mitigation applied: temporary opt-out flag set for KiteNote to prevent automatic SSE->WS upgrade (api gateway config toggled). Engineering patch drafted and POC PR opened (see related_github_prs). Planned fix: make metadata-envelope flush atomic with upgrade handshake; test under ALB probe churn. Target for patch roll to staging: 2026-03-16, canary to prod after validation. Post-deploy will include a follow-up ticket for SDK recommendation: add a small 'drain_wait_ms' default when performing client-side upgrades.
notes: Follow-up: Coordinate with SDK team to release SDK-js v1.2.5 with upgrade backoff recommendation.
Add an observability metric for 'upgrade_in_flight_with_pending_metadata' to detect recurrence.
Runbook update: add a checklist item to disable upgrades for affected customers as temporary mitigation.
Postmortem trigger if customer impact persists after 72 hours

source.summary: Progress-callbacks + client-side retry causing unacknowledged tool executions and billing confusion
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer uses the TypeScript SDK in a serverless worker to issue long-running orchestrations that include multiple tool/function calls per user request. They've enabled progress callbacks (per-action progress events) so their frontend can display live status. Recently they observed duplicate billing for tool executions: the backend logs show the tool run completed, but the client timed out waiting for the final progress acknowledgement and retried the whole orchestration, which triggered the tooling to re-execute. The customer is requesting guidance on how to avoid unverified double-executions and how to reconcile charges.

Impact:
- Customer reports 2-8% increase in tooling cost for a specific heavy workflow.
- UI shows transient "stuck" state when progress callbacks are lost; sometimes user retries from browser which results in duplicate downstream effects (e.g., duplicate emails).
- Customer is concerned about side effects and billing.

Environment:
- TypeScript SDK (serverless): sdk-js v1.8.2
- Node.js 18 running in CloudRun-like environment behind a proxy with aggressive request timeouts (15s).
- Model: redwood/gpt-medium-1 pinned.
- Region: us-east.

Steps to reproduce (as reported by customer):
1) Start orchestration that fires three sequential tool calls with progress events enabled.
2) Simulate a proxy 15s timeout during the final streaming acknowledgement.
3) Client performs a retry of the orchestration (retry-on-timeout).
4) Server-side logs show first run succeeded and emitted tool completions, but the client retry triggers a second set of tool runs.

Expected behavior:
- Either the retry should detect previous successful run and not re-execute side-effectful tools (idempotent behavior), or progress/ack should be robustly delivered so client doesn't retry unnecessarily.

Observed logs (snippets provided by customer):
- "[2026-03-08T11:05:12Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f"
- "[2026-03-08T11:05:14Z] tool_execute COMPLETE toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f status=200"
- "[2026-03-08T11:05:27Z] client_timeout waiting for final ack, connection closed"
- "[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] RETRY orchestration re-submitted idempotency_key=missing"
- "[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-9c5b"

Additional context from customer:
- They rely on progress callbacks to display per-tool status in frontend and to allow manual retry of failed steps.
- They were not explicitly setting per-tool idempotency metadata in the orchestration payload because they assumed the SDK would propagate a top-level idempotency key to nested tool invocations.

Logs and attachments: customer attached a debug trace and a cURL reproduction script.
comments: 2026-03-10 - Customer (J. Patel): We see duplicate downstream emails and extra billing. We cannot disable retries in user browsers. Need a reliable pattern to avoid double side effects.

2026-03-10 - Support (Ava Chen): Requesting full request+response traces and the SDK telemetry for that run. Can you include the orchestration payload (sanitized) and confirm whether you set any idempotency headers per-tool?

2026-03-11 - Customer (J. Patel): We attached traces. We did not set per-tool idempotency metadata; we only set a top-level operationId on our side but not passed to the SDK. The SDK call shows no idempotency_key in nested tool RPCs.

2026-03-11 - Support (Diego Martins): Reproduced locally with given trace and proxy timeout. Observed: SDK v1.8.2 does not automatically inject top-level idempotency keys into nested tool invocations when using progress callbacks and streaming resume. This leads to the second orchestration retry being treated as a fresh run by the serving runtime.

2026-03-11 - Engineering (SRE) note: The serving runtime records tool executions by an internal execution id; idempotency detection relies on client-provided idempotency metadata propagated to tool calls. Without it, side effects are not deduplicated. Also noted connection close on ack path causes SDK to treat whole request as failed and will trigger client-side retry semantics.

2026-03-12 - Support (Diego Martins): Provided short-term mitigation steps and logged an SDK bug (see related PR). Advised customer on per-tool idempotency headers and suggested increasing proxy timeouts while we release a fix.
2026-03-12 - Customer (J. Patel): Confirmed workaround reduced duplicates in staging. Asked about recommended long-term patterns and whether Redwood will provide server-side de-dup.
investigation: Reproduced with Node 18 and sdk-js v1.8.2 behind a 15s proxy timeout. Progress callbacks cause intermittent loss of final ack when client connection is closed by proxy; SDK treats connection closure as failure and does not persist per-tool idempotency headers to nested tool RPCs.

Observed that the SDK's streaming resume flow focuses on top-level request resume tokens and currently does not include a per-tool acknowledgement token in the resume handshake. This means resumed requests will lack the original nested call idempotency metadata unless the developer sets it explicitly.
Serving runtime confirms both executions reached execution stage; no global orchestration-level dedupe occurs for nested side effects without explicit idempotency tags on those tool calls.
Billing: metering records two completed tool executions; finance/metrics confirm chargeable events are per-tool-complete and were emitted twice.
source.resolution:
Workaround provided (short term):
1) Add explicit per-tool idempotency metadata in orchestration payload: include a deterministic per-tool idempotency_key (e.g., operationId + toolName + stepIndex hash). The SDK will forward provided metadata into nested RPCs so the serving runtime can deduplicate.
2) Increase proxy/request timeouts to allow final ack to arrive (temporary).
3) Avoid client-side whole-orchestration retry on partial streaming failures; switch to per-step retry or provide the frontend a "resume" UX that replays only missing steps.

Medium-term fix (in progress):
- SDK change in PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-js/pull/483 to propagate top-level idempotency_key into nested tool calls when progress callbacks / streaming resume is enabled, and to emit per-tool ack tokens persisted across resume. Targeted release: sdk-js v1.9.0.
- Platform improvement: runtime will consider adding an optional orchestration-level dedupe token that applies to nested side effects for customers that opt-in; design TBD (linked to internal task INT-4129).

Follow-up actions assigned:
- Diego Martins: draft SDK release notes and include migration guidance (due 2026-03-15).
- SRE: validate metric exposure for retries vs unique tool executions (due 2026-03-18).
- Support: work with customer on billing reconciliation; opened internal billing ticket INT-4129.
next_steps: Customer to implement per-tool idempotency keys as a stopgap and validate in staging (customer said they'd test by 2026-03-13).
Monitor for reduction in duplicate executions; Support will follow up after staging test.
Track SDK PR and notify customer when v1.9.0 is released; include automated tests for streaming resume + idempotency propagation.
Create a short runbook entry for "progress-callbacks and retries" in Confluence to surface the pattern to other customers.

Logs and attachments: customer attached a debug trace and a cURL reproduction script.
text:
Issue summary:
Customer uses the TypeScript SDK in a serverless worker to issue long-running orchestrations that include multiple tool/function calls per user request. They've enabled progress callbacks (per-action progress events) so their frontend can display live status. Recently they observed duplicate billing for tool executions: the backend logs show the tool run completed, but the client timed out waiting for the final progress acknowledgement and retried the whole orchestration, which triggered the tooling to re-execute. The customer is requesting guidance on how to avoid unverified double-executions and how to reconcile charges.

Impact:
- Customer reports 2-8% increase in tooling cost for a specific heavy workflow.
- UI shows transient "stuck" state when progress callbacks are lost; sometimes user retries from browser which results in duplicate downstream effects (e.g., duplicate emails).
- Customer is concerned about side effects and billing.

Environment:
- TypeScript SDK (serverless): sdk-js v1.8.2
- Node.js 18 running in CloudRun-like environment behind a proxy with aggressive request timeouts (15s).
- Model: redwood/gpt-medium-1 pinned.
- Region: us-east.

Steps to reproduce (as reported by customer):
1) Start orchestration that fires three sequential tool calls with progress events enabled.
2) Simulate a proxy 15s timeout during the final streaming acknowledgement.
3) Client performs a retry of the orchestration (retry-on-timeout).
4) Server-side logs show first run succeeded and emitted tool completions, but the client retry triggers a second set of tool runs.

Expected behavior:
- Either the retry should detect previous successful run and not re-execute side-effectful tools (idempotent behavior), or progress/ack should be robustly delivered so client doesn't retry unnecessarily.

Observed logs (snippets provided by customer):
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:12Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:14Z] tool_execute COMPLETE toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-7a3f status=200\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:27Z] client_timeout waiting for final ack, connection closed\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] RETRY orchestration re-submitted idempotency_key=missing\"
- \"[2026-03-08T11:05:28Z] tool_execute START toolId=pay-processor txn=tx-9c5b\"

Additional context from customer:
- They rely on progress callbacks to display per-tool status in frontend and to allow manual retry of failed steps.
- They were not explicitly setting per-tool idempotency metadata in the orchestration payload because they assumed the SDK would propagate a top-level idempotency key to nested tool invocations.

Logs and attachments: customer attached a debug trace and a cURL reproduction script.

source.summary: Differential audit-merge manifest for PSP reconciliation and compliance evidence
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer (Orchid Bank, enterprise) requires a differential (delta) audit-merge manifest that maps Redwood activity records to their internal PSP transaction IDs for reconciliation and regulator evidence. They want cryptographically verifiable manifests (per-batch HMAC) plus a small sample payload demonstrating mapping logic for two days of high-volume traffic.

Impact:
- Orchid Bank cannot complete month-end PSP reconciliation without the mapping manifest and cryptographic proof. Potential regulatory reporting delay.
- High priority due to accounting/legal timelines for transaction dispute windows.

Environment:
- Production (us-east primary, eu-west failover).
- Customer uses Redwood hosted API and writes audit exports to their S3 bucket (s3://orchid-bank-redwood/exports/).

Requested deliverables from Redwood Support/Eng: 
1) A differential audit-merge manifest format spec (JSONL) that includes: export_window_start/end, per-record redwood_event_id, timestamp_utc, customer_psp_id, payment_reference, HMAC-SHA256 (per-batch) and batch_checksum (sha256).
2) One on-demand sample delta bundle for 2026-02-28 -> 2026-03-01 (max 5k records) with signed manifest and accompanying index.csv mapping redwood_event_id -> customer_psp_id.
3) Documentation: example ingestion snippet for their PSP system and a short verification script (python) to validate HMAC and checksums.
4) Statement of retention/ttl semantics and legal-hold override procedure (how to request snapshot pinning).

Steps to reproduce / what we will provide to Eng: 
- Customer S3 prefix: s3://orchid-bank-redwood/exports/delta/2026-02-28_to_2026-03-01/
- Sample query they run: SELECT * FROM audit_events WHERE event_time BETWEEN '2026-02-28T00:00:00Z' AND '2026-03-01T00:00:00Z' AND service = 'payments' LIMIT 5000;
- Expected mapping: event.metadata.transaction_id => customer_psp_id (customer provided mapping rules).

Constraints and questions we asked the customer: 
- Confirm whether the PSP ID should be written into the customer_psp_id field (they confirmed yes).
- Confirm acceptable signing key rotation window (customer requests daily-rotated KEK with published thumbprints).

Acceptance criteria:
- Manifest follows spec and is verifiable by their auditor script.
- Delivered sample bundle for the two-day window and proof-of-delivery to their secure SFTP endpoint or the S3 prefix above.

Notes on sensitivity: bundles contain payment transaction references and must be treated as highly sensitive (PII/financial). Delivery must follow Orchid Bank's secure delivery requirements (AES-256-GCM encrypted ZIP with pre-shared key + per-file signature).
comments:
2026-03-10 11:05 - Amanda Park (Support): Acknowledged receipt. Assigning to Eng for manifest spec and sample export. SLA 4 hours for first response. 
Action: collect mapping rules and S3 prefix from customer.

2026-03-10 12:15 - Diego Ramos (Eng): Reviewed request. Hypothesis: our existing delta exporter can produce the fields requested but needs a manifest wrapper and per-batch HMAC extension. Will prototype small export; asking SRE for signed-delivery helper and KMS usage.

2026-03-10 14:40 - SRE (Liam O'Connor): KMS support confirmed. We can generate per-batch HMAC using a service key; rotation cadence can be daily. Need PR to add batch_checksum and hmac fields to existing exporter.

2026-03-11 09:20 - Support (Amanda Park): Customer provided mapping rules and SFTP endpoint. Customer asked for completion ETA. Informing them we'll deliver sample within 48 hours while full automation will follow.

2026-03-12 16:00 - Diego Ramos (Eng): Delivered ad-hoc sample bundle to customer's S3 prefix and uploaded verification script to their SFTP. Pending customer validation. Linked PR created: https://github.com/redwood-inference/audit-export/pull/118
investigation_notes: Checked exporter logs for 2026-02-28 window: export job id job-20260228-77 completed successfully at 2026-03-01T01:12Z (see CloudWatch /exports/job-20260228-77).
Confirmed event schema contains metadata.transaction_id for payments service but mapping field name is customer-specific (customer provided mapping rules to map transaction_id -> customer_psp_id).
Prototype run produced 4,912 records for requested window. Initial HMAC computed with service-key v3 (KMS alias: alias/audit-manifest) matches local verification script.
Relevant dashboards: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/exports/export-jobs?var-job=job-20260228-77
Open engineering work: add signed-manifest wrapper and AES-GCM ZIP packaging to ad-hoc exporter; PR created and queued for small rollout to on-demand path only.
source.resolution: Partial delivery: ad-hoc sample delta bundle (2026-02-28 -> 2026-03-01) delivered to s3://orchid-bank-redwood/exports/delta/2026-02-28_to_2026-03-01/ with signed manifest and per-batch HMAC. Verification script provided. Next steps: scheduled automation for nightly differential exports and documentation updates. Customer validated HMAC locally and reported no mismatches (2026-03-12).
follow_up_tasks: ENG-EXPORTS: Automate nightly differential manifests for Orchid Bank (owner: Diego Ramos, ETA: 2026-03-19)
SEC: Rotate KEK publication and update customer onboarding doc with thumbprints (owner: Liam O'Connor, ETA: 2026-03-16)
DOCS: Publish example ingestion snippet and verification script to Confluence page (owner: Laura Singh, ETA: 2026-03-17)
SUPPORT: Confirm legal-hold override runbook with Orchid Bank and store signed LOA in customer folder (owner: Amanda Park, ETA: 2026-03-13)

source.summary: IdP-initiated SLO probe truncates session cookie domain causing re-auth loops and 401s in private VPC
source.description: Issue summary: After configuring IdP-initiated SSO for Nimbus Health's Private deployment, periodic IdP single-logout (SLO) probes from their vendor (AuthWave) are observed to cause the Redwood ingress to rewrite the session cookie domain by truncating tenant namespace suffixes. Impact: End-users and service accounts intermittently hit 401/reauth loops across the Redwood admin and ingest APIs. Expected: SLO probes should not alter or truncate session cookie domain/path; logout events should be processed without breaking other sessions. Environment details: Private control plane deployed into customer's VPC using edge-proxy v2.1.7, ingress-proxy v3.4.0, auth-bridge v1.9. Deployed certs: internal mTLS + customer-managed IdP metadata via SAML. Observed window: recurring since 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC, roughly every 6–8 hours when IdP health probes run.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Set up IdP-initiated SSO with AuthWave SAML metadata in a Private deployment (tenant-scoped).
2) Enable periodic IdP SLO/health probe from AuthWave (probe includes SLO endpoint + cookie-less GET).
3) Observe ingress logs when probe performs a request that triggers session cookie handling (AuthWave probe sends Host header without tenant suffix).
4) Attempt normal user API calls or console operations immediately after the probe; note 401 and redirect-to-login behavior.
logs: 2026-03-09T02:05:12Z ingress-proxy[router] INFO cookie_rewrite: original_cookie_domain=customer-tenant.nimbus.internal.local
2026-03-09T02:05:12Z ingress-proxy[router] WARN cookie_rewrite: computed_domain=customer-tenant.nimbus.internal (truncated suffix)
2026-03-09T02:05:12Z auth-bridge[session] ERROR session_validate: missing_tenant_namespace; session rejected, returning 401
2026-03-09T02:07:40Z control-plane[audit] INFO auth_flow: idp_slo_received idp_request_id=aw-probe-5514
comments: 2026-03-10 09:12 - Ava Chen (Support): Customer reports large number of users are hitting login loops after IdP maintenance windows. Collected ingress and auth logs; provided preliminary correlation to SLO probe timestamps.

2026-03-10 10:03 - Ravi Patel (SRE): Reproduced probe in staging; ingress-proxy treats Host header without tenant suffix as 'global' and applies fallback cookie-domain logic that strips dot segments. Hypothesis: domain canonicalization code doesn't preserve multi-label internal domains when Host lacks tenant prefix.

2026-03-11 15:45 - Marcus Li (Auth team): Found a change in ingress-proxy v3.4.0 that tightens effective TLD parsing; edge case triggers when Host header contains fewer labels than expected. Preparing patch to skip truncate behavior for session cookies.

2026-03-12 08:20 - Customer (Nimbus Health): Confirmed that when our IdP probe uses tenant-less host header the admin console fails for all web sessions. We can temporarily disable the IdP probe but need a durable fix.

2026-03-12 12:05 - Ravi Patel (SRE): Deployed a hotfix to staging that adds a guard: only perform cookie-domain truncation if host contains tenant namespace pattern. Requesting customer validation and will schedule private control-plane patching if validated.
investigation_notes: Initial correlation shows 1:1 mapping between AuthWave probe timestamps and increased 401s across the tenant's sessions.

Cookie rewrite logic located in ingress-proxy/router/session_cookie.go; function CreateEffectiveCookieDomain(host) uses naive last-two-label heuristic that fails for internal multi-label domains and tenant prefixes.
AuthWave probe omits tenant prefix in Host header for health check optimization; this triggers fallback path that strips trailing label (eg. .local) producing non-matching cookie domain.
Reproduction steps executed in internal staging with a synthetic AuthWave probe confirmed cookie domain change and session invalidation when probe executed.
Patch candidate: change heuristic to prefer longest-match against tenant registry; skip truncation when Host header label-count < expected or when request path contains /slo endpoint.
source.resolution: Workaround: Nimbus Health disabled IdP SLO/health probes to stop immediate 401s. We validated a staging hotfix that conservatively bypasses cookie-domain truncation when Host header lacks tenant prefix; patch PR #4832 created for ingress-proxy. Plan: schedule controlled rollout to customer Private control plane during maintenance window 2026-03-14 02:00 UTC. Post-deploy follow-up: add unit tests for multi-label internal domains and update SSO runbook to recommend IdP probes include tenant-scoped Host or use probe path that doesn't hit session-handling endpoints. Owner for rollout: Ravi Patel.
